Goldendoodle.brushingRoutine: Brush every 2-3 days with a slicker brush to prevent matting, focusing on behind the ears, armpits, and collar area. Follow up with a steel greyhound comb to check for hidden tangles close to the skin. For curlier coats, line-brush in sections from the skin outward to avoid surface-only brushing that leaves mats underneath.
Goldendoodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a moisturizing shampoo formulated for curly or wavy coats. Apply a detangling conditioner and let it sit for 3-5 minutes before rinsing thoroughly. Use lukewarm water and ensure complete drying with a high-velocity dryer, as damp Goldendoodle coats mat rapidly.
commonIssues: Matting behind ears, in armpits, and around collar
Ear infections from moisture trapped in floppy, hair-filled ear canals
Eye staining and tear buildup around the muzzle
Sanitary area tangles requiring regular trimming
Goldendoodle.extendResults: Keep a slicker brush by the couch and do a 5-minute brush-out every other evening. Spritz a light leave-in conditioner before brushing to reduce static and breakage. Remove the collar daily and brush the neck area underneath, as this is the number one matting spot between grooms.
Goldendoodle.seasonalCare: Goldendoodles do not have a traditional shedding season, but humidity in summer causes coats to tangle faster. In winter, dry indoor heat can make the coat brittle. Consider a shorter maintenance clip in summer and a slightly longer coat in winter with increased brushing frequency.
Goldendoodle.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Brush thoroughly every 2-3 days at home. Check ears weekly for odor or redness. Trim sanitary areas and paw pads between grooms if needed.

Poodle (Standard).brushingRoutine: Brush daily or every other day with a slicker brush, working in sections from the skin outward. Use a steel comb afterward to verify no tangles remain. Pay extra attention to the topknot, ears, and leg furnishings where the dense, curly coat mats most quickly.
Poodle (Standard).bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks using a volumizing or clarifying shampoo suited for curly coats. A quality conditioner is essential to keep the tight curls manageable. Blow-dry completely using a high-velocity dryer while brush-drying to achieve a fluffy, even finish and prevent mats from forming as the coat air-dries.
commonIssues: Severe matting if brushing is skipped even a few days
Ear infections due to hair growth inside the ear canal
Tear staining, especially on lighter-colored Poodles
Skin sensitivity requiring hypoallergenic products
Overgrown nails hidden by foot hair
Poodle (Standard).extendResults: Maintain a consistent brush-out schedule between grooms, as Poodle coat texture means mats form rapidly once neglected. Use a light grooming spray before each brushing session. Keep the face, feet, and sanitary areas trimmed between full grooms to maintain a clean appearance.
Poodle (Standard).seasonalCare: Poodles do not shed seasonally, but coat density increases slightly in winter. Summer humidity can cause the coat to frizz and tangle more. A shorter clip in summer reduces maintenance burden. In winter, a longer coat provides insulation but demands more frequent brushing.
Poodle (Standard).maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks. Daily or every-other-day brushing at home. Ear hair plucking and cleaning at each groom. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Shih Tzu.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush for the outer coat, then follow with a fine-toothed comb to catch tangles near the skin. For dogs kept in a longer coat, part the hair down the back and brush each side downward in sections. A small slicker brush works well for the face and leg areas.
Shih Tzu.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo designed for long, silky coats. Use a lightweight conditioner to prevent tangling without weighing down the coat. Keep water out of the flat face and ears during bathing, and dry thoroughly with a low-heat dryer.
commonIssues: Eye irritation and tear staining from facial hair
Ear infections from heavy, low-hanging ear flaps trapping moisture
Matting in the armpit and belly areas
Skin fold irritation around the nose and face
Dental issues that affect breath and face grooming comfort
Shih Tzu.extendResults: Use a topknot or clip the face hair short to keep eyes clear between grooms. Wipe the face daily with a damp cloth to reduce tear staining. A light leave-in spray before brushing keeps the silky coat from breaking. If maintaining a puppy cut, schedule trims every 4-6 weeks to keep the style fresh.
Shih Tzu.seasonalCare: Shih Tzus do not have a heavy shed cycle, but their long coat picks up debris and allergens in spring and summer. A shorter puppy cut is practical for Houston summers. In winter, the coat can become dry and static-prone indoors, so use a moisturizing spray.
Shih Tzu.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks. Daily brushing for long coats, every 2-3 days for puppy cuts. Daily face wipe. Weekly ear checks. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Golden Retriever.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush for the outer coat and an undercoat rake for the dense undercoat. Focus on the feathering behind the ears, chest, belly, and back of the legs where tangles form. A steel comb through the feathering catches anything the brush misses.
Golden Retriever.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks or as needed using a de-shedding shampoo and conditioner to loosen dead undercoat. Avoid over-bathing, as it strips the natural oils that make the Golden's water-resistant double coat healthy. Use lukewarm water and dry thoroughly, especially the dense chest and belly areas.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding requiring consistent undercoat removal
Hot spots from moisture trapped in the dense undercoat
Ear infections from floppy ears and water-loving behavior
Feathering mats behind ears and on legs
Dry, flaky skin if bathed too frequently
Golden Retriever.extendResults: A thorough de-shedding treatment during professional grooming dramatically reduces shedding at home for 2-3 weeks. Between grooms, use an undercoat rake weekly to remove loose fur before it mats. Keep ear feathering trimmed and tidy. Never shave a Golden Retriever, as the double coat provides temperature regulation.
Golden Retriever.seasonalCare: Goldens blow their undercoat heavily in spring and fall. Increase brushing to daily during these periods and consider a professional de-shedding treatment. In Houston summers, ensure the coat is free of undercoat buildup to allow airflow. In winter, the coat naturally thickens for insulation.
Golden Retriever.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks with de-shedding treatment. Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Weekly ear cleaning. Nail trims every 3-4 weeks. Trim paw pads and sanitary areas as needed.

Labrador Retriever.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times per week with a rubber curry brush or deshedding tool to remove loose undercoat. Follow with a bristle brush to smooth the outer coat and distribute natural oils. During shedding season, increase to daily brushing with an undercoat rake.
Labrador Retriever.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a de-shedding shampoo that helps release dead undercoat. Labs have a naturally oily, water-resistant coat, so avoid over-bathing. A de-shedding conditioner left on for a few minutes before rinsing helps loosen dead fur. Dry thoroughly to prevent that distinctive wet-dog smell.
commonIssues: Year-round shedding with heavy seasonal blowouts
Oily coat that develops odor between baths
Ear infections from floppy ears, especially after swimming
Hot spots in warm, humid climates
Tail and hip areas prone to thinning from allergies
Labrador Retriever.extendResults: A professional de-shedding treatment with a high-velocity dryer blowout removes an enormous amount of dead coat and keeps shedding manageable for 2-3 weeks. At home, a quick 5-minute curry brush session a few times a week makes a significant difference. Keep ears dry after any water activity.
Labrador Retriever.seasonalCare: Labs shed heavily in spring and fall as they transition coats. In Houston's heat, ensure the undercoat is not packed and trapping heat against the skin. Never shave a Lab, as the double coat protects against sunburn and heat. Winter coat thickens naturally and requires less maintenance.
Labrador Retriever.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming or de-shedding treatment every 8-10 weeks. Brush 1-2 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Clean ears weekly, more often if the dog swims. Nail trims every 3-4 weeks.

German Shepherd.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with an undercoat rake to manage the dense double coat. Use a slicker brush for the outer guard hairs and a deshedding tool during heavy shed periods. Pay special attention to the thick ruff around the neck, the pants on the hind legs, and the bushy tail where fur accumulates.
German Shepherd.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks using a de-shedding shampoo and conditioner. German Shepherds have sensitive skin, so use a gentle, soap-free formula. Over-bathing strips essential oils from their coat. Use warm water and dry completely with a high-velocity dryer, which also blows out loose undercoat.
commonIssues: Extreme shedding year-round with massive seasonal blowouts
Skin allergies and hot spots, especially in humid climates
Dry, flaky skin if bathed too often or with harsh products
Undercoat compaction if not regularly brushed out
Nail overgrowth from rear dewclaws
German Shepherd.extendResults: A professional de-shedding treatment with high-velocity drying is the single most effective way to manage GSD shedding between grooms. At home, dedicate 10 minutes every other day to undercoat raking. A deshedding spray before brushing helps release trapped fur. Keep the home vacuum schedule aligned with brushing days.
German Shepherd.seasonalCare: German Shepherds blow their entire undercoat twice a year, typically in spring and fall, producing dramatic amounts of loose fur for 2-3 weeks. In Houston heat, remove as much undercoat as possible to prevent overheating. Never shave the coat. In winter, the dense undercoat provides critical insulation.
German Shepherd.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming with de-shedding every 8-10 weeks. Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blowouts. Check ears biweekly. Nail trims every 3-4 weeks.

French Bulldog.brushingRoutine: Brush once a week with a soft rubber curry brush or grooming mitt to remove loose hair and stimulate the skin. A soft bristle brush works well for finishing and distributing natural oils. Despite the short coat, Frenchies shed more than most people expect.
French Bulldog.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a gentle, hypoallergenic or oatmeal-based shampoo. French Bulldogs are prone to skin sensitivities, so avoid harsh products. Use lukewarm water and dry thoroughly, paying special attention to getting between all skin folds completely dry to prevent bacterial and yeast infections.
commonIssues: Skin fold dermatitis in facial wrinkles, tail pocket, and body folds
Allergic skin reactions causing itching and redness
Ear infections from narrow, bat-shaped ear canals
Tear staining below the eyes
Tail pocket infection if not cleaned regularly
French Bulldog.extendResults: Clean facial folds daily with an unscented pet wipe or damp cloth, then dry completely. Apply a thin layer of nose and wrinkle balm to prevent irritation. Check and clean the tail pocket 2-3 times per week. After walks in hot weather, wipe down the entire body to remove allergens and sweat.
French Bulldog.seasonalCare: Frenchies shed moderately year-round with a slight increase in spring. In Houston summers, skin issues increase due to heat and humidity. Keep folds extra dry, limit outdoor time in peak heat, and watch for heat exhaustion signs. In winter, their thin coat provides little insulation, and dry indoor air can worsen skin dryness.
French Bulldog.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Weekly brushing at home. Daily facial fold cleaning. Tail pocket cleaning 2-3 times weekly. Ear cleaning weekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Yorkshire Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush for long coats or every 2-3 days with a slicker brush for shorter trims. The fine, silky hair tangles easily, especially behind the ears, in the armpits, and around leg joints. Use a fine-toothed comb after brushing to catch small knots close to the skin.
Yorkshire Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks using a gentle, moisturizing shampoo for silky coats. Yorkies have hair similar to human hair, so a lightweight conditioner prevents tangling and breakage. Avoid heavy products that weigh down the fine coat. Use lukewarm water and blow-dry on a low heat setting to prevent a frizzy finish.
commonIssues: Matting in the fine, silky coat, especially behind ears and under legs
Tear staining around the eyes
Dental disease affecting overall grooming comfort
Dry, brittle hair prone to breakage
Skin sensitivity to grooming products
Yorkshire Terrier.extendResults: Use a light leave-in conditioner spray before each brushing session to protect the delicate hair. If kept in a longer style, use a topknot to keep hair out of the eyes and reduce face staining. Wrap techniques can protect show-length coats between grooms. A shorter puppy cut reduces daily maintenance significantly.
Yorkshire Terrier.seasonalCare: Yorkies have hair rather than fur, so they do not have a seasonal shed cycle. However, the coat may become drier and more static-prone in winter due to indoor heating. In summer, a shorter cut keeps them comfortable in Houston heat. Protect them from sunburn on any close-clipped areas.
Yorkshire Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks. Daily brushing for long coats, every 2-3 days for short clips. Face wiping daily. Dental care as recommended by vet. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Dachshund.brushingRoutine: For smooth-coat Dachshunds, brush weekly with a soft bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt. For longhaired varieties, brush every 2-3 days with a pin brush and follow with a comb through the ear, chest, and belly feathering. For wirehaired varieties, use a slicker brush weekly and hand-strip the coat 2-3 times per year.
Dachshund.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks for smooth coats, every 4-6 weeks for longhaired and wirehaired. Use a mild, moisturizing shampoo. For longhaired Dachshunds, apply a detangling conditioner to the feathering. Dry thoroughly, especially the long ears and chest area where moisture lingers.
commonIssues: Ear infections from long, heavy ear flaps that restrict airflow
Back sensitivity requiring gentle handling during grooming
Skin allergies causing itchiness along the belly
Longhaired variety matting in ear and chest feathering
Nail overgrowth due to low ground clearance and less natural wear
Dachshund.extendResults: For longhaired Dachshunds, a quick comb-through of the ear and belly feathering every other day prevents tangles from setting in. Keep the ears clean and dry inside. For all coat types, a weekly wipe-down with a grooming cloth removes dirt and distributes oils. Trim paw pad hair and nails regularly since their short legs mean nails wear less on pavement.
Dachshund.seasonalCare: Smooth-coat Dachshunds shed moderately in spring and fall. Longhaired varieties shed less but require more coat maintenance. In Houston summers, check the belly for irritation from hot surfaces since they ride low to the ground. In winter, smooth-coated Dachshunds may need a sweater for outdoor walks in colder periods.
Dachshund.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks for smooth coats, every 6-8 weeks for longhaired or wirehaired. Weekly brushing for smooth coats, every 2-3 days for longhaired. Ear cleaning weekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Cavalier King Charles Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush or pin brush to prevent tangles in the silky coat. Use a comb through the ear feathering, chest, belly, and leg furnishings where mats form most easily. Be gentle around the ears, as the long, heavily-feathered ear hair is prone to painful tangles.
Cavalier King Charles Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a gentle, moisturizing shampoo for silky coats. Use a lightweight conditioner on the feathering areas. Rinse thoroughly, as product residue attracts dirt and causes matting. Dry with a low-heat dryer, brushing through the feathering as you dry to prevent tangles from forming.
commonIssues: Ear infections from long, heavy ears with poor airflow
Matting behind the ears and in the armpit feathering
Tear staining, particularly in Blenheim and tricolor markings
Skin sensitivity and allergies
Heart-related exercise intolerance affecting grooming positioning
Cavalier King Charles Spaniel.extendResults: Focus between-groom brushing on the ears, chest, and behind the front legs where feathering mats fastest. Use a detangling spray before each session. Keep ear hair clean and check underneath the ear leather weekly for redness or odor. A light trim of the feathering between grooms keeps the coat tidy without changing the breed's signature look.
Cavalier King Charles Spaniel.seasonalCare: Cavaliers shed moderately year-round with heavier shedding in spring and fall. The silky coat picks up debris easily in any season. In summer, the feathering can trap heat, so keep it trimmed and free of mats. Winter coats thicken slightly and require consistent brushing to prevent undercoat tangles.
Cavalier King Charles Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Brush 3-4 times weekly, focusing on feathered areas. Ear cleaning weekly. Face wiping for tear stains every 1-2 days. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Cocker Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush daily or every other day with a slicker brush, working through the abundant ear, leg, and skirt feathering in sections. Follow with a steel comb to ensure all tangles are removed down to the skin. The dense, wavy coat mats quickly and requires thorough, not just surface, brushing.
Cocker Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks using a moisturizing shampoo and a rich conditioner to keep the thick coat manageable. Rinse extremely thoroughly, as Cocker coats hold shampoo residue that causes skin irritation. Dry completely with a high-velocity dryer, then finish with a slicker brush while fluff-drying the feathering.
commonIssues: Chronic ear infections from long, heavy ears that trap moisture and reduce airflow
Severe matting in the dense leg, ear, and belly feathering
Skin conditions including seborrhea
Anal gland issues requiring regular expression
Eye discharge and staining
Cocker Spaniel.extendResults: The ears are the priority between grooms. Brush ear feathering every other day and keep the inside of the ear leather clean. Tie ears back during meals to prevent food from caking in the fur. A shorter sport clip dramatically reduces daily maintenance while keeping the Cocker looking sharp. Trim sanitary areas every 2-3 weeks.
Cocker Spaniel.seasonalCare: Cockers shed moderately year-round. In humid Houston summers, the thick coat becomes a magnet for matting and skin issues. A shorter trim is highly recommended for summer comfort. In spring and fall, increased brushing manages the heavier shed. Winter coats are fuller and require consistent daily brushing if kept long.
Cocker Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks. Daily or every-other-day brushing at home. Ear cleaning 1-2 times weekly. Sanitary trim every 2-3 weeks. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Bernese Mountain Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with an undercoat rake to manage the thick double coat, followed by a slicker brush to smooth the long outer coat. Focus on the heavy feathering behind the ears, on the chest and belly, and on the hind legs where matting is worst. A wide-toothed comb works well for the tail and ruff.
Bernese Mountain Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a de-shedding shampoo and a moisturizing conditioner to help release the dense undercoat. This is a large dog with a lot of coat, so ensure thorough rinsing. Use warm water and dry completely with a high-velocity dryer, as the thick coat holds moisture for hours and can develop hot spots if left damp.
commonIssues: Heavy year-round shedding with extreme seasonal blowouts
Hot spots from moisture trapped in the dense undercoat
Matting in the ear, chest, and leg feathering
Heat sensitivity making summer grooming critical
Elbow calluses from lying on hard surfaces
Bernese Mountain Dog.extendResults: A professional de-shedding treatment with thorough blow-drying removes massive amounts of dead undercoat and significantly reduces home shedding for 2-3 weeks. Between grooms, focus undercoat raking on the chest, pants, and behind the ears. Keep the paw pads trimmed and the feathering between toes short to prevent ice balls in winter and debris collection in summer.
Bernese Mountain Dog.seasonalCare: Bernese Mountain Dogs blow their undercoat heavily in spring and moderately in fall. In Houston heat, this breed is particularly vulnerable to overheating, so keeping the undercoat thoroughly raked out is essential for temperature regulation. Never shave this breed. In winter, the full double coat provides excellent insulation.
Bernese Mountain Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming with de-shedding every 6-8 weeks. Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blowouts. Ear cleaning weekly. Nail trims every 3-4 weeks. Paw pad and feathering trims as needed.

Australian Shepherd.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with an undercoat rake to manage the medium-length double coat. Follow with a slicker brush to smooth the outer coat and address tangles in the mane, feathering, and pants. Use a comb through the ear feathering and behind-the-ear areas where mats hide.
Australian Shepherd.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a de-shedding shampoo and conditioner to release dead undercoat. Aussies have a naturally dirt-resistant coat that cleans itself fairly well between baths. Dry thoroughly with a high-velocity dryer, paying attention to the dense chest ruff and hind leg feathering.
commonIssues: Moderate to heavy seasonal shedding
Matting behind ears and in the mane ruff
Burrs, stickers, and debris tangled in feathering after outdoor activity
Sensitive skin under the dense coat
Hind leg feathering that collects dirt and fecal matter
Australian Shepherd.extendResults: After outdoor adventures, do a quick comb-through of the belly, legs, and tail to remove debris before it sets into mats. Keep the sanitary area and hind leg feathering trimmed for hygiene. A de-shedding spray before brushing sessions helps release more undercoat. The natural coat oils will keep the outer coat looking good between baths.
Australian Shepherd.seasonalCare: Australian Shepherds blow their undercoat in spring and fall. Increase brushing to daily during these periods. In Houston summers, thorough undercoat removal is critical for heat management. Never shave this breed, as the double coat provides sun protection and temperature regulation. Winter coat fills in naturally.
Australian Shepherd.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks with de-shedding treatment. Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Ear cleaning biweekly. Nail trims every 3-4 weeks. Sanitary trim every 4-6 weeks.

Siberian Husky.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with an undercoat rake, which is the most important tool for this breed. Follow with a wide-toothed comb through the dense ruff and pants. During coat blowouts, daily raking sessions of 15-20 minutes are necessary to keep up with the volume of loose undercoat.
Siberian Husky.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks, as Huskies have a self-cleaning coat that rarely develops odor. Use a de-shedding shampoo and conditioner to maximize dead coat removal. A thorough high-velocity dryer blowout after bathing is the most effective single step for managing Husky shedding. Expect the dryer session alone to take 20-30 minutes.
commonIssues: Extreme seasonal coat blowouts producing massive amounts of loose fur
Undercoat compaction if not regularly raked
Zinc-responsive dermatosis causing crusty, flaky skin
Sensitive to over-bathing and harsh chemicals
Escape behavior during grooming due to independent temperament
Siberian Husky.extendResults: A professional de-shedding treatment with an extended high-velocity dryer blowout is the single best investment for Husky coat management. Between grooms, consistent undercoat raking prevents the dead coat from packing against the skin. Never shave a Husky, as the double coat protects against both heat and cold, and may not grow back correctly.
Siberian Husky.seasonalCare: Huskies blow their entire undercoat twice a year, typically in spring and fall, in a dramatic process that lasts 2-4 weeks. In Houston's climate, this breed struggles with heat, so keeping the undercoat raked out is critical for thermoregulation. The topcoat reflects sunlight and insulates, so shaving exposes them to sunburn and removes their cooling system.
Siberian Husky.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming with de-shedding every 8-12 weeks. Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blowouts. Minimal ear issues but check biweekly. Nail trims every 3-4 weeks.

Maltese.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush for long coats, or every 2-3 days for shorter puppy cuts using a slicker brush. The single-layer, silky white coat tangles easily, especially behind the ears, in the armpits, and on the chest. Use a fine-toothed comb to check for small mats close to the skin after brushing.
Maltese.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks using a whitening or brightening shampoo to maintain the signature white coat. Follow with a lightweight conditioner to prevent tangling without making the coat limp. A purple-toned shampoo helps counteract yellowing. Use lukewarm water and blow-dry on low heat while brushing to achieve a silky, straight finish.
commonIssues: Severe tear staining around the eyes
Yellowing or discoloration of the white coat
Fine hair that mats easily and breaks when dry-brushed
Dental disease that is extremely common in the breed
Skin sensitivity to dyes and fragrances in products
Maltese.extendResults: Wipe the eye area 1-2 times daily with a damp cloth or tear stain wipe to prevent buildup. Use a leave-in conditioner spray before every brushing session to protect the delicate hair from breakage. Keep facial hair trimmed or in a topknot. If the coat yellows between baths, a waterless whitening spray can freshen the appearance.
Maltese.seasonalCare: Maltese do not shed seasonally since they have hair, not fur. In summer, a shorter cut keeps them cool and reduces maintenance. The white coat shows dirt easily in any season. In winter, the single coat provides minimal insulation, so limit cold exposure. Dry indoor heat can make the coat brittle and static-prone.
Maltese.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 3-4 weeks for long coats, every 4-6 weeks for puppy cuts. Daily brushing for long coats. Daily eye area cleaning. Dental care as recommended. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Bichon Frise.brushingRoutine: Brush every 1-2 days with a slicker brush, working in sections against the growth direction to lift and separate the curly coat. Follow with a steel comb to ensure no hidden mats remain near the skin. The cotton-ball texture of the Bichon coat makes surface brushing deceptive, as mats form close to the skin while the outer coat looks fine.
Bichon Frise.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks using a whitening shampoo and a volumizing conditioner. The coat needs to be clean to maintain its characteristic puffiness. Blow-dry completely using a high-velocity dryer while brush-drying with a slicker brush to achieve the round, fluffy Bichon silhouette. Never air-dry, as the coat will mat and lose its shape.
commonIssues: Matting close to the skin hidden by the fluffy outer coat
Tear staining below the eyes
Skin allergies and sensitivities
Ear infections from hair growth in the ear canal
Dental issues common in small breeds
Bichon Frise.extendResults: The key to extending a Bichon groom is consistent every-other-day brushing that reaches all the way to the skin. Use a line-brushing technique, parting the coat in rows and brushing from the skin outward. A light grooming spray prevents static. Keep the eye area wiped daily and the ear hair managed between grooms.
Bichon Frise.seasonalCare: Bichons do not shed seasonally, but the coat grows continuously and requires consistent maintenance year-round. In humid Houston summers, the coat may feel heavier and mat faster. In winter, dry indoor air causes static. A slightly shorter trim in summer reduces maintenance burden without compromising the breed's distinctive look.
Bichon Frise.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks. Brush every 1-2 days at home using line-brushing technique. Daily face wipe for tear stains. Ear cleaning and hair check weekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Pomeranian.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush, working through the abundant double coat in sections. Use an undercoat rake gently through the dense undercoat, especially around the chest ruff, behind the ears, and on the hind legs. A pin brush works well for the outer coat finishing. Be gentle, as rough brushing can damage the delicate topcoat.
Pomeranian.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a volumizing shampoo to maintain the signature fluffy appearance. Apply a lightweight conditioner to the undercoat. Dry completely with a high-velocity dryer on a moderate setting, fluff-drying with a slicker brush to achieve maximum volume. The double coat holds moisture, so thorough drying is essential.
commonIssues: Alopecia X (black skin disease) causing coat loss
Matting in the dense undercoat behind ears and on the chest ruff
Tear staining on lighter-colored Poms
Dental disease extremely common in the breed
Patellar luxation making standing positions during grooming uncomfortable
Pomeranian.extendResults: Maintain the fluffy Pom silhouette between grooms by brushing against the coat growth to lift and separate. A light volumizing spray before brushing adds body. Trim the foot pads and sanitary area at home between grooms if comfortable. Never shave a Pomeranian, as the coat may not grow back properly and the breed is prone to post-clipping alopecia.
Pomeranian.seasonalCare: Pomeranians shed their undercoat in spring and fall, with a particularly heavy shed in spring. Females may also blow coat after heat cycles. In Houston summers, keeping the undercoat raked out helps with heat management. Never shave for summer. Winter coats fill in beautifully and require regular brushing to prevent matting.
Pomeranian.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Face wiping for tear stains every 1-2 days. Dental care as recommended. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Miniature Schnauzer.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush on the body and a comb through the beard, eyebrows, and leg furnishings. The wiry topcoat requires different care than the soft undercoat. Use a stripping knife or comb if maintaining a hand-stripped coat for show, or a slicker brush for clipped pet coats.
Miniature Schnauzer.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a shampoo formulated for wiry or terrier coats. For hand-stripped coats, use a texturizing shampoo to maintain the coarse texture. Wash the beard separately with each bath, as it collects food and water stains. Rinse thoroughly and dry with a medium-heat dryer.
commonIssues: Beard staining from food, water, and saliva
Schnauzer comedone syndrome (blackheads along the back)
Ear hair growth requiring regular plucking
Leg furnishing mats from snow, mud, or debris
Skin bumps and cysts common in the breed
Miniature Schnauzer.extendResults: The beard is the high-maintenance area between grooms. Wipe it after every meal and water session to prevent staining and odor. Comb the beard and leg furnishings every other day to prevent tangles. A quick wipe along the back with a medicated pad can help manage Schnauzer bumps between grooms.
Miniature Schnauzer.seasonalCare: Miniature Schnauzers are low-shedding dogs, but hand-stripped coats need stripping every 8-12 weeks to maintain proper texture. In summer, the furnishings can be kept shorter for easier maintenance. In winter, the wiry coat provides decent insulation. Beard care is year-round and is the most demanding part of Schnauzer maintenance.
Miniature Schnauzer.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 5-7 weeks. Brush body 2-3 times weekly. Comb beard and leg furnishings every 1-2 days. Beard wipe after meals. Ear cleaning weekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Boxer.brushingRoutine: Brush once a week with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt in circular motions to remove loose hair and stimulate the skin. Follow with a soft bristle brush to smooth the coat and distribute natural oils. Despite the short coat, Boxers shed moderately and benefit from regular brushing.
Boxer.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo. Boxers have sensitive skin, so avoid harsh products. An oatmeal-based shampoo soothes their often allergy-prone skin. Use warm water and towel-dry or use a low-heat dryer. The short coat dries quickly but ensure skin folds around the face are completely dry.
commonIssues: Skin allergies causing itching, hives, and hot spots
Facial fold irritation around the muzzle and jowls
Excessive drooling that irritates chin and chest skin
Seasonal shedding more than expected for a short-coated breed
Calluses on elbows from lying on hard surfaces
Boxer.extendResults: Wipe facial folds daily with a pet wipe to prevent irritation and bacterial buildup. A weekly curry brush session keeps shedding manageable. For dogs with allergies, wiping down the coat with a damp cloth after walks removes pollen and environmental allergens. Apply paw balm and elbow balm as needed to prevent cracking.
Boxer.seasonalCare: Boxers shed more noticeably in spring and fall. In Houston summers, watch for overheating, as the short muzzle makes them heat-sensitive. Wipe down after outdoor time to remove allergens. The thin coat provides minimal sun protection, so limit direct sun exposure. In winter, Boxers may need a coat for extended outdoor time in cooler weather.
Boxer.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks. Weekly brushing at home. Daily facial fold cleaning. Ear cleaning biweekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks. Elbow balm as needed.

Beagle.brushingRoutine: Brush once a week with a bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt to remove loose hair from the short, dense double coat. A deshedding tool or hound glove used weekly during shedding season helps manage the surprising amount of hair this breed produces. The coat is easy to maintain but sheds more than most short-coated breeds.
Beagle.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a de-shedding or gentle shampoo. Beagles can develop a distinctive houndy odor, so a deodorizing shampoo helps between baths. Use warm water and dry thoroughly, paying attention to the long ears. The dense double coat holds water longer than it appears.
commonIssues: Ear infections from long, floppy ears that trap moisture and reduce airflow
Hound odor from active skin oil production
Moderate shedding that surprises owners expecting a low-shed short coat
Allergies causing itchy skin and ear problems
Weight gain that affects skin fold development
Beagle.extendResults: Clean ears weekly with a veterinarian-recommended ear solution, lifting the ear flap to allow air circulation. A weekly rubdown with a grooming mitt captures loose fur before it hits furniture. Between baths, a deodorizing grooming spray helps manage the natural hound scent. Keep nails trimmed regularly.
Beagle.seasonalCare: Beagles shed their undercoat more heavily in spring. The dense double coat does a reasonable job in both heat and cold, but ensure the undercoat is not packed in Houston summers. In winter, the coat naturally thickens. Year-round ear care is the most important breed-specific concern regardless of season.
Beagle.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks. Weekly brushing at home. Ear cleaning weekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks. Deodorizing spray between baths as needed.

Rottweiler.brushingRoutine: Brush once a week with a rubber curry brush or bristle brush to remove loose undercoat and distribute natural oils through the short, dense double coat. During shedding seasons, increase to 2-3 times weekly and add an undercoat rake to manage the heavier shed. Despite the short appearance, the coat has a substantial undercoat.
Rottweiler.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks using a de-shedding or moisturizing shampoo. Avoid over-bathing, as it strips the natural oils that keep the dense coat healthy and water-resistant. Use warm water and dry thoroughly. A high-velocity dryer works well to blast out loose undercoat during the bath routine.
commonIssues: Moderate seasonal shedding from the dense undercoat
Skin allergies and hot spots, particularly in humid climates
Lip fold dermatitis around the jowls
Elbow calluses and pressure sores from lying on hard surfaces
Nail overgrowth requiring regular maintenance
Rottweiler.extendResults: A weekly curry brush session takes only 5-10 minutes and makes a real difference in managing shedding and coat health. Wipe the lip folds regularly to prevent irritation. Apply elbow and paw balm to keep calluses soft. After outdoor activities, a quick wipe-down removes allergens that can trigger skin reactions.
Rottweiler.seasonalCare: Rottweilers shed their undercoat in spring and fall. In Houston heat, keeping the undercoat raked out helps the dog stay comfortable. The black coat absorbs heat, so limit intense outdoor activity in peak summer. In winter, the double coat provides good insulation and fills in naturally.
Rottweiler.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks. Weekly brushing, 2-3 times weekly during shedding season. Lip fold cleaning 2-3 times weekly. Ear cleaning biweekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Doberman Pinscher.brushingRoutine: Brush once a week with a rubber curry brush, grooming mitt, or soft bristle brush. The short, smooth single coat is low-maintenance but sheds lightly year-round. Circular motions with a curry brush stimulate blood flow and capture loose hair effectively. A chamois cloth rubdown after brushing adds shine.
Doberman Pinscher.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. Dobermans have thin skin that is sensitive to harsh chemicals, so avoid strong detergent-based products. Oatmeal or aloe-based formulas work well. Dry quickly, as the thin coat and lean body mean Dobermans get cold easily when wet.
commonIssues: Color dilution alopecia in blue and fawn Dobermans causing hair loss and skin issues
Dry skin and dandruff, especially in winter
Skin sensitivity to products and environmental allergens
Acne on the chin and muzzle
Thin coat provides minimal protection from cuts and scrapes
Doberman Pinscher.extendResults: A weekly rubdown with a grooming mitt keeps the coat sleek between grooms. For Dobermans prone to dry skin, add a fish oil supplement to their diet and use a moisturizing grooming spray between baths. Wipe the face after meals to prevent chin acne. The short coat shows every scratch, so paw balm and body butter for sensitive skin areas help maintain a clean appearance.
Doberman Pinscher.seasonalCare: Dobermans shed lightly year-round with a minor increase in spring. The thin single coat provides minimal insulation in winter and limited sun protection in summer. In Houston heat, they manage well due to the short coat, but watch for sunburn on lighter-colored dogs. A jacket or sweater may be needed on cooler winter days.
Doberman Pinscher.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks. Weekly brushing at home. Face wiping after meals. Ear cleaning biweekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks. Moisturizing spray for dry skin as needed.

Great Dane.brushingRoutine: Brush once a week with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt. The short, smooth coat is easy to maintain despite the massive body surface area. Use long, sweeping strokes to cover the large frame efficiently. A bristle brush provides a nice finishing shine. The sheer size of the dog means each session takes longer simply due to square footage.
Great Dane.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a gentle shampoo suited for sensitive skin. Due to their size, many owners use a walk-in shower or professional grooming facility. An oatmeal-based shampoo works well for their often-sensitive skin. Dry thoroughly with towels and a dryer if available. The short coat dries quickly but the sheer volume of dog means it takes time.
commonIssues: Bloat risk requiring calm, stress-free grooming environment
Calluses on elbows, hocks, and pressure points from their heavy weight
Lip fold dermatitis from heavy jowls and drooling
Sensitive skin prone to allergic reactions
Nail maintenance is critical due to the heavy body weight on the feet
Great Dane.extendResults: Apply elbow and hock balm regularly to prevent painful calluses from developing or cracking. Wipe facial folds and jowls daily, as drool accumulation causes skin irritation and odor. A weekly full-body rubdown with a curry brush takes only 10-15 minutes and keeps shedding minimal. Keep nails trimmed consistently, as overgrown nails on a dog this heavy affect joint alignment.
Great Dane.seasonalCare: Great Danes shed moderately year-round with a slight increase in spring. The short coat provides minimal insulation, so they are sensitive to both heat and cold extremes. In Houston summers, ensure they have shade and cooling. In winter, a coat may be needed for extended outdoor time. Harlequin and lighter-colored Danes need sunburn protection.
Great Dane.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks. Weekly brushing at home. Daily jowl and fold wiping. Callus care 2-3 times weekly. Ear cleaning biweekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Chihuahua.brushingRoutine: For smooth-coat Chihuahuas, brush once a week with a soft bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt. For long-coat Chihuahuas, brush 2-3 times per week with a pin brush, paying attention to the ear feathering, chest ruff, and tail plume where tangles form. A fine-toothed comb catches small knots in the long coat.
Chihuahua.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a gentle, tearless shampoo formulated for small breeds. Chihuahuas chill easily, so use warm water and dry quickly and thoroughly. A low-heat dryer works well. For long coats, apply a lightweight conditioner to prevent tangling. Protect the ears from water entry during bathing.
commonIssues: Dental disease extremely common in the breed, affecting grooming comfort
Molera (soft spot on skull) requiring gentle head handling
Tear staining, especially in light-colored dogs
Tracheal sensitivity requiring harness instead of collar for restraint
Anxiety and shivering during grooming from temperature sensitivity and nervousness
Chihuahua.extendResults: Chihuahuas are low-maintenance between grooms. A weekly brush keeps the smooth coat tidy. For long coats, a quick comb-through of the ear and chest feathering every few days prevents tangles. Wipe the eye area daily for tear stain prevention. Keep the grooming environment warm to reduce anxiety-related shivering.
Chihuahua.seasonalCare: Smooth-coat Chihuahuas shed moderately in spring and fall. Long-coat Chihuahuas shed less noticeably due to the longer hair cycle. In Houston summers, the small body manages heat reasonably well but avoid direct sun. In winter, Chihuahuas of both coat types need sweaters or jackets for outdoor time, as they lose body heat rapidly.
Chihuahua.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Weekly brushing for smooth coats, 2-3 times weekly for long coats. Daily eye area wiping. Dental care as directed by vet. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

Pug.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times per week with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt to manage the surprisingly heavy shedding from this short-coated breed. Follow with a soft bristle brush to smooth the coat. The dense double coat produces far more loose hair than the small size suggests.
Pug.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo. The most critical step is thoroughly cleaning and drying all facial wrinkles and the nose rope fold. Use a damp cotton pad to clean between each fold, then dry completely. An oatmeal-based shampoo soothes the allergy-prone skin. Dry the entire body thoroughly, as the dense coat holds moisture.
commonIssues: Facial fold dermatitis and infections in the deep wrinkles
Heavy shedding despite the short coat
Skin allergies and chronic itching
Breathing difficulties during grooming requiring frequent breaks
Nose rope fold infections if not cleaned regularly
Ear infections from narrow ear canals
Pug.extendResults: Clean facial folds daily with an unscented pet wipe, paying special attention to the deep nose rope fold. Dry each fold completely after cleaning. A weekly curry brush session captures loose fur before it covers furniture. Between baths, a grooming wipe-down removes allergens and keeps the coat fresh. Apply nose balm to prevent the nose from cracking.
Pug.seasonalCare: Pugs shed heavily year-round, with increased shedding in spring. In Houston summers, this breed is at high risk for overheating due to the flat face. Keep outdoor time brief and monitor breathing during grooming. Skin fold infections increase in humid weather, so vigilant daily fold care is essential. In winter, the short coat provides minimal insulation.
Pug.maintenanceSchedule: Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Brush 1-2 times weekly. Daily facial fold and nose rope cleaning. Ear cleaning weekly. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks. Nose balm application as needed.

Border Collie.brushingRoutine: Use a pin brush and slicker brush 3-4 times per week to work through the dense double coat. Focus on the feathering behind the ears, chest ruff, and leg furnishings where mats form fastest. A long-tooth undercoat rake is essential during shedding season to remove loose undercoat without damaging the topcoat.
Border Collie.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a nourishing shampoo formulated for double coats. Apply a detangling conditioner to feathered areas and rinse thoroughly to prevent residue buildup. Use lukewarm water and ensure the dense undercoat is completely dried with a high-velocity dryer to prevent hot spots.
commonIssues: Matting in feathered areas behind ears and legs
Heavy seasonal shedding (coat blow)
Hot spots from trapped moisture in undercoat
Debris and burrs caught in leg feathering
Border Collie.extendResults: Spritz a light leave-in conditioner on feathered areas between grooms to keep them tangle-free. Daily quick brushing of the chest ruff and behind the ears takes only 2 minutes and prevents the mats that require professional detangling. Keep belly and sanitary areas trimmed short for active dogs.
Border Collie.seasonalCare: Border Collies blow their undercoat heavily in spring and fall. During these periods, increase brushing to daily sessions with an undercoat rake. In Houston summers, consider a light thinning of the undercoat to improve airflow, but never shave the double coat as it provides insulation and sun protection.
Border Collie.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks. Check ears weekly for debris. Trim paw pads and sanitary areas every 3-4 weeks.

Shetland Sheepdog.brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week using a pin brush followed by a slicker brush. Pay special attention to the thick mane, chest ruff, and leg feathering. Use a steel comb to finish and check for hidden mats close to the skin, especially behind the ears and in the armpit area.
Shetland Sheepdog.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a volumizing shampoo to maintain the coat's natural loft. Use a quality conditioner on the longer furnishings to ease brushing. Ensure the dense undercoat is fully dried with a forced-air dryer, as retained moisture leads to skin irritation and mildew odor.
commonIssues: Severe matting behind ears, armpits, and rear legs
Profuse seasonal shedding
Dry skin from over-bathing
Ear infections due to hair growing inside ear canal
Static and flyaway coat in dry conditions
Shetland Sheepdog.extendResults: Use a light anti-static spray when brushing between grooms. Focus 2 minutes daily on the trouble spots: behind ears, collar area, and rear feathering. Keeping a small slicker brush by the door for quick post-walk sessions prevents outdoor debris from becoming tangled mats.
Shetland Sheepdog.seasonalCare: Shelties undergo a dramatic coat blow twice yearly, shedding the entire undercoat over 2-3 weeks. Daily raking with an undercoat tool is essential during this period. In Houston heat, ensure the undercoat is thinned professionally to allow airflow, but preserve the outer guard coat for UV and heat protection.
Shetland Sheepdog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during shedding. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks. Check and pluck ear hair monthly. Trim paw pads and sanitary trim every 3 weeks.

Havanese.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush or slicker brush, working in sections from the skin outward. Use a fine-tooth comb to check for small tangles close to the skin, especially around the collar, behind ears, and between legs. Mist the coat lightly with a detangling spray before brushing to prevent breakage.
Havanese.bathingTips: Bathe every 1-2 weeks using a gentle, moisturizing shampoo for silky coats. Apply a lightweight conditioner to maintain the soft texture without weighing down the coat. Use lukewarm water and avoid rubbing the coat roughly with a towel, as this creates tangles. Pat dry and blow dry on low heat.
commonIssues: Rapid matting without daily brushing
Tear staining around eyes
Ear infections from heavy, floppy ears
Coat discoloration from saliva licking on paws
Tangles forming in armpit and groin areas
Havanese.extendResults: Daily line brushing for 5-10 minutes is the single most effective way to extend groom results. Use a tear stain wipe daily under the eyes. Keep a spray bottle of diluted conditioner on hand for touch-up brushing. Consider a puppy clip if the owner cannot commit to daily coat maintenance.
Havanese.seasonalCare: Havanese do not shed seasonally like double-coated breeds, but their continuously growing coat requires consistent year-round maintenance. In humid Houston weather, the coat may feel limp. In summer, a shorter sport clip keeps them comfortable while reducing daily brushing needs.
Havanese.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily. Professional groom every 3-4 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Wipe tear stains daily. Trim face, paws, and sanitary areas between full grooms as needed.

West Highland White Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush to maintain the wiry outer coat. Use a fine comb around the face and ears. Hand-stripping is the traditional method to maintain proper coat texture; if clipped instead, use a stripping knife monthly to preserve some wire texture. Pay attention to the skirt and leg furnishings.
West Highland White Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks using a whitening or brightening shampoo to keep the coat brilliant white. Avoid heavy conditioners that soften the wire coat texture. Apply a whitening conditioner only to stained areas. Rinse extremely thoroughly as residue yellows the white coat quickly.
commonIssues: Coat yellowing and staining
Skin allergies and atopic dermatitis (breed-prone)
Tear staining on white face
Coat softening from repeated clipping instead of stripping
Hot spots from skin allergies
West Highland White Terrier.extendResults: Use a waterless whitening foam between baths on beard and leg stains. Wipe the face and beard after meals to prevent food staining. A light sprinkle of grooming powder helps absorb oils and maintain brightness between baths. Keep the belly area trimmed short to avoid grass staining.
West Highland White Terrier.seasonalCare: Westies have a moderate double coat that sheds lightly year-round. In spring, use an undercoat rake to remove loose hair. Houston humidity can aggravate their common skin allergies, so monitor for excessive scratching and hot spots during warm months. Consider a hypoallergenic diet during high-allergen seasons.
West Highland White Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks. Hand-strip or card every 6-8 weeks for show coat. Clean face daily. Monitor skin for allergy flare-ups weekly.

Bull Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a soft bristle brush or rubber curry brush to remove loose hair and distribute natural oils across the short, flat coat. A grooming mitt works well for quick maintenance sessions. Despite the short coat, regular brushing significantly reduces shedding around the house.
Bull Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks or as needed using a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo. Their short coat dries quickly and requires minimal effort. Use a moisturizing oatmeal shampoo if the dog has dry or sensitive skin, which is common in the breed. Avoid over-bathing as it strips natural oils.
commonIssues: Skin allergies and contact dermatitis
Dry, flaky skin
Acne on chin and muzzle
Sunburn on white or lightly pigmented skin
Seasonal shedding heavier than expected for a short coat
Bull Terrier.extendResults: Use a rubber curry brush or grooming glove 2-3 times per week to keep shedding under control. Apply a light coat conditioner or coconut oil spray to maintain skin moisture. A chamois cloth gives a beautiful shine after brushing. Keep skin folds around the face clean if present.
Bull Terrier.seasonalCare: Bull Terriers shed moderately year-round with an increase in spring and fall. In Houston summers, apply pet-safe sunscreen to areas with thin or white coat coverage, especially the nose and ears. Their short coat provides minimal insulation, so monitor for overheating in extreme heat.
Bull Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a curry brush. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Check skin weekly for irritation or allergies. Apply sunscreen as needed in summer. Clean ears biweekly.

Basset Hound.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a bristle brush or rubber curry brush to manage the short but dense coat and reduce shedding. Use a hound glove for a finishing shine. Bassets shed more than most people expect for a short-coated breed, and regular brushing is the primary shedding management tool.
Basset Hound.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks using a deodorizing shampoo, as Bassets have naturally oily skin that produces a characteristic hound odor. Use a gentle ear cleanser at every bath as their long, heavy ears trap moisture and bacteria. Ensure all skin folds and wrinkles around the face are cleaned and dried completely.
commonIssues: Chronic ear infections from long, heavy ears
Hound odor from oily skin
Skin fold infections around face and jowls
Excessive drool causing chin and chest staining
Interdigital cysts between toes
Basset Hound.extendResults: Clean and dry the ear flaps 2-3 times per week to prevent infections. Wipe facial folds and jowls daily with a pet-safe wipe to prevent bacterial buildup. Use a deodorizing spray between baths to manage hound smell. Keep the chin area dry and clean after meals and water bowls.
Basset Hound.seasonalCare: Bassets shed heavily in spring and moderately year-round. Their low-slung body picks up ground moisture and allergens in every season. In Houston humidity, ear infections become more frequent. Increase ear cleaning to every other day during summer months and ensure complete drying after any water exposure.
Basset Hound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Bathe every 3-4 weeks. Clean ears 2-3 times weekly. Wipe facial folds daily. Check between toes weekly for cysts. Trim nails biweekly.

Akita.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with an undercoat rake and slicker brush to manage the thick, plush double coat. Work in sections, raking the undercoat first, then smoothing with the slicker brush. Focus on the thick neck ruff, rear breeches, and tail plume where the coat is densest.
Akita.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a de-shedding shampoo and follow with a de-shedding conditioner to loosen undercoat. Their dense coat requires extended soak time. Use a high-velocity dryer to blow out the undercoat thoroughly as this breed's coat retains water like a sponge. Allow 45-60 minutes for proper drying.
commonIssues: Massive undercoat shedding twice yearly
Coat retaining moisture leading to hot spots
Sebaceous adenitis (breed-predisposed)
Matting in neck ruff and rear breeches
Zinc-responsive dermatosis
Akita.extendResults: Use a de-shedding tool weekly between professional grooms to stay ahead of loose undercoat. A forced-air dryer at home is a worthwhile investment for this breed. Avoid shaving or cutting the double coat as it alters the coat texture permanently. Regular undercoat removal is far more effective than length reduction.
Akita.seasonalCare: Akitas experience a dramatic coat blow in spring and fall, losing massive quantities of undercoat over 2-3 weeks. Daily raking during these periods is essential. In Houston heat, ensure the undercoat is professionally blown out to allow air circulation. The double coat actually insulates against heat when properly maintained.
Akita.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Professional groom with full blow-out every 6-8 weeks. Check ears monthly. Trim nails biweekly. Monitor skin for sebaceous adenitis symptoms.

Samoyed.brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week using a pin brush and undercoat rake. The profuse, stand-off double coat requires section-by-section brushing from the skin outward. Use a long-tooth metal comb to check for hidden mats in the dense undercoat, especially around the ruff, behind ears, rear legs, and under the tail.
Samoyed.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a whitening or brightening shampoo to maintain the brilliant white coat. Apply a heavy conditioner and allow it to soak for 5-10 minutes to penetrate the dense undercoat. This breed requires 60-90 minutes of high-velocity drying. An incompletely dried Samoyed coat will mat and can develop mildew.
commonIssues: Extreme undercoat density causing hidden mats
Coat yellowing and staining (especially around mouth and paws)
Insufficient drying leading to mildew smell and hot spots
Tear staining on white face
Coat blow producing enormous quantities of loose undercoat
Samoyed.extendResults: Use a whitening spray on stained areas between baths. The Samoyed coat is naturally dirt-repelling when properly maintained, so focus maintenance on undercoat removal rather than frequent bathing. Brush in 10-minute daily sessions rather than one long weekly session. Apply a light anti-static spray in dry conditions.
Samoyed.seasonalCare: Samoyeds blow their entire undercoat 1-2 times per year in a dramatic shedding event. Daily raking is essential during the 2-4 week blow period. In Houston's heat, professional de-shedding services every 4 weeks during summer are recommended. Never shave a Samoyed as the double coat protects against both heat and sunburn.
Samoyed.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks. Check for mats daily in problem areas. Clean tear stains daily. Full de-shedding treatment during seasonal blows.

Weimaraner.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry comb or hound glove to remove loose hair from the distinctive silver-gray coat. Their short, sleek coat is among the easiest to maintain. A chamois cloth rubbed over the coat after brushing produces the beautiful metallic sheen Weimaraners are known for. For long-haired Weimaraners, brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush.
Weimaraner.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a mild, moisturizing shampoo. Weimaraners have naturally clean coats that rarely develop odor. Use a gentle formula since their skin can be sensitive. A light conditioner maintains the coat's signature silky feel. For the long-haired variety, use a detangling conditioner on the feathering.
commonIssues: Separation anxiety making grooming difficult when owners leave
Skin allergies and hot spots, especially in humid climates
Bloat risk requiring rest before and after grooming
Thin skin that bruises and tears more easily than thicker-coated breeds
Ear infections from folded ear flaps
Weimaraner.extendResults: Use a microfiber cloth daily to maintain the sleek, silver appearance. Keep the owner present or visible during grooming to prevent anxiety. Apply a light coat spray for shine between baths. Monitor for hot spots in humid weather. Keep nails very short -- Weimaraners are active and long nails affect their gait.
Weimaraner.seasonalCare: Weimaraners shed moderately year-round with a slight increase in spring and fall. In Houston summers, their lean build and short coat manage heat reasonably well but they need shade and hydration during activity. In rare winter cold, a light jacket is appreciated since the single coat provides minimal insulation.
Weimaraner.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly rubber curry brush, daily chamois cloth wipe, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 2-3 weeks, skin checks for hot spots weekly in summer, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Vizsla.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a soft bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt. The Vizsla has a single-layer short coat with no undercoat, making grooming straightforward. A rubber curry brush effectively removes loose hair while providing a massage that Vizslas typically enjoy. Finish with a soft cloth for shine.
Vizsla.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-10 weeks using a mild, moisturizing shampoo. The single-layer coat has minimal natural oil, so avoid harsh shampoos that strip what little protection exists. Their coat is naturally odor-resistant and self-cleaning. A warm water rinse without shampoo is often sufficient between full baths.
commonIssues: Thin, single-layer coat prone to cuts and abrasions
Dry skin from over-bathing
Sensitivity to cold due to no undercoat
Allergic dermatitis
Sunburn on the nose and thinly coated areas
Vizsla.extendResults: The Vizsla coat stays clean with minimal effort. A weekly wipe-down with a damp cloth and regular curry brushing is usually all that is needed. Supplement with fish oil for optimal coat health. Their golden rust color looks best when natural skin oils are preserved through infrequent bathing.
Vizsla.seasonalCare: Vizslas shed lightly year-round with a slight increase in spring. Their single coat provides no insulation against Houston heat or sun. Apply pet-safe sunscreen to the nose and ears for extended outdoor exposure. In cooler months, they may benefit from a jacket as they lack the undercoat insulation of double-coated breeds.
Vizsla.maintenanceSchedule: Brush weekly with a soft brush or mitt. Bathe every 6-10 weeks. Check ears weekly as floppy ears trap bacteria. Apply sunscreen as needed. Trim nails biweekly.

Bulldog (English).brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a soft bristle brush or rubber curry brush. The short, smooth coat is low-maintenance for brushing but sheds more than expected. The real grooming priority for this breed is facial fold cleaning, which should happen daily. Use a damp cloth followed by a dry cloth in every skin fold.
Bulldog (English).bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo. The most critical bathing step is thoroughly cleaning and drying all facial folds, wrinkles around the nose rope, and body folds. Apply a medicated fold cream or balm after drying if fold dermatitis is present. Use a wrinkle-specific cleanser for deep folds.
commonIssues: Facial fold dermatitis and infection
Skin allergies and chronic itching
Tail pocket infection (if corkscrew tail)
Interdigital cysts between toes
Tear staining in facial creases
Yeast infections in moist skin folds
Bulldog (English).extendResults: Daily facial fold cleaning is non-negotiable for this breed. Use unscented baby wipes or pet fold wipes to clean between every wrinkle, then apply a light, breathable fold balm. Check and clean the tail pocket daily if your Bulldog has a tight corkscrew tail. Dry folds are healthy folds.
Bulldog (English).seasonalCare: English Bulldogs are extremely heat-sensitive. In Houston summers, keep grooming appointments early morning. Their compromised airways make heat dangerous. In humid weather, skin fold infections increase dramatically. Increase fold cleaning to twice daily during summer. Monitor for heat stroke signs during and after grooming.
Bulldog (English).maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Clean facial folds daily. Clean tail pocket daily. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Check between toes weekly. Monitor skin folds for redness or odor constantly.

Lhasa Apso.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and metal comb for a full-length coat. Work in small sections from the skin outward, using a detangling spray to prevent breakage. The heavy, dense coat mats rapidly without consistent attention. If kept in a puppy clip, brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush to prevent matting between clips.
Lhasa Apso.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks using a moisturizing shampoo and a rich conditioner. The long coat tangles severely when wet, so apply conditioner liberally and work through with fingers before rinsing. Never rub the coat with a towel; instead, gently squeeze excess water out and blow dry from skin outward on medium heat.
commonIssues: Severe matting in full coat, especially behind ears and legs
Tear staining on light-colored faces
Eye irritation from hair falling into eyes
Ear infections from heavy ear leather and hair
Dry, brittle coat from insufficient conditioning
Lhasa Apso.extendResults: Daily line brushing is essential for full-coat Lhasas. Part the coat down the spine and brush each section from skin to tip. Use a leave-in conditioner spray before every brushing session. For owners who cannot commit to daily grooming, a maintained puppy clip at 1-2 inches is far healthier than a neglected long coat.
Lhasa Apso.seasonalCare: Lhasa Apsos have a single coat that does not shed seasonally in the traditional sense, but they do lose hair continuously. In Houston humidity, the coat may absorb moisture and mat more easily. Use a dehumidifying coat spray in summer months. The coat provides some sun protection but monitor for overheating.
Lhasa Apso.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily for full coat, 3-4 times weekly for puppy clip. Bathe every 2-3 weeks. Clean eyes and tear stains daily. Clean ears weekly. Professional groom every 3-4 weeks.

Shiba Inu.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with an undercoat rake and slicker brush. The plush double coat is naturally clean and odor-resistant but sheds heavily. Work the undercoat rake through the dense underlayer first, then smooth with a slicker brush. Focus on the thick neck ruff, chest, and rear breeches.
Shiba Inu.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks as the Shiba coat is remarkably self-cleaning. Use a de-shedding shampoo and conditioner to loosen undercoat. The dense double coat requires extended soak time and thorough high-velocity drying. This breed is often resistant to bath time, so professional grooming from puppyhood builds tolerance.
commonIssues: Intense seasonal coat blow producing enormous volume of loose fur
Resistance to grooming and handling (the Shiba scream)
Skin allergies common in the breed
Coat retaining water after bathing
Nail trimming sensitivity
Shiba Inu.extendResults: The Shiba coat is naturally dirt-repelling and low-odor, so focus maintenance efforts on undercoat removal rather than bathing. A weekly 10-minute undercoat raking session prevents loose fur from building up. Introduce grooming tools gradually and positively, as Shibas have strong opinions about handling.
Shiba Inu.seasonalCare: Shibas blow their undercoat dramatically twice per year, typically in spring and fall. During the 2-3 week blow period, daily undercoat raking is necessary to keep up with the volume. In Houston summers, professional de-shedding treatments help remove the insulating undercoat. Never shave the double coat.
Shiba Inu.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Professional de-shedding during seasonal blow. Check ears monthly. Trim nails biweekly with patience.

Newfoundland.brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week with a long-tooth undercoat rake, pin brush, and slicker brush. The massive, dense double coat requires systematic section-by-section grooming. Focus on the heavy mane around the neck, chest feathering, rear breeches, and the dense tail plume. A wide-tooth metal comb checks for mats close to the skin.
Newfoundland.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a de-shedding shampoo and heavy conditioner. The enormous coat requires extended soak and rinse time. Budget 90-120 minutes for bathing and drying. High-velocity drying is mandatory as the thick coat retains enormous amounts of water and can take hours to air dry, creating hot spots and mildew.
commonIssues: Massive shedding volume requiring constant management
Hot spots from moisture trapped in dense undercoat
Drool staining on chest and face
Mat formation in armpits, behind ears, and rear end
Coat retaining dirt and debris on low-hanging belly
Newfoundland.extendResults: Invest in a high-velocity dryer for home use. Regular undercoat removal with a rake is far more effective than frequent bathing for this breed. Keep the belly and sanitary areas trimmed shorter for hygiene. Wipe drool from the chest daily to prevent staining and skin irritation beneath wet hair.
Newfoundland.seasonalCare: Newfoundlands blow massive amounts of undercoat in spring and fall. Daily raking during these periods can fill entire garbage bags with loose fur. In Houston heat, this breed requires careful monitoring as their dense coat and large body mass make overheating dangerous. Professional de-shedding every 4 weeks in summer is recommended.
Newfoundland.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks. Clean face and drool areas daily. Trim belly and sanitary areas monthly. Check ears biweekly.

Saint Bernard.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with an undercoat rake and pin brush for the long-haired variety, or a bristle brush and rubber curry for the short-haired variety. Both varieties have a dense double coat that sheds heavily. Focus on the neck ruff, behind ears, and rear feathering on long-haired Saints.
Saint Bernard.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a de-shedding shampoo. Like the Newfoundland, the Saint Bernard's massive coat requires extended bathing and drying time. Use a high-velocity dryer and allow 60-90 minutes for complete drying. Clean the facial folds and jowls thoroughly at every bath as drool and moisture collect in the wrinkles.
commonIssues: Excessive drool causing skin maceration and staining around jowls
Facial fold dermatitis around wrinkles
Hot spots from dense undercoat trapping moisture
Heavy seasonal shedding
Eye discharge and tear staining
Saint Bernard.extendResults: Wipe facial folds and jowls 2-3 times daily to prevent bacterial growth from drool accumulation. Keep a drool towel accessible at all times. Use a deodorizing spray between baths as the breed can develop odor from drool-soaked neck fur. Regular undercoat removal reduces shedding far more than frequent bathing.
Saint Bernard.seasonalCare: Saint Bernards shed heavily in spring and moderately year-round. In Houston's heat, this breed is at high risk for overheating. Schedule grooming appointments during cooler morning hours. Professional de-shedding every 4-6 weeks during summer helps the dog regulate body temperature. Never shave the double coat.
Saint Bernard.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Clean facial folds and jowls 2-3 times daily. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Professional de-shedding monthly in summer. Check ears biweekly.

Irish Setter.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a pin brush and long-tooth metal comb. The silky, flowing coat with feathering on the ears, chest, legs, and tail requires attentive maintenance. Use a slicker brush gently on feathered areas to prevent tangling. Pay special attention to the fine, silky ear feathering which mats quickly.
Irish Setter.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a shampoo formulated for silky coats. Apply a lightweight conditioner to the feathered areas to maintain their flowing texture without weighing them down. The rich mahogany color looks best with a color-enhancing shampoo designed for red coats. Dry with medium heat to preserve the natural wave.
commonIssues: Ear feathering matting and tangling
Ear infections due to heavy, hairy ear flaps
Burrs and debris caught in leg feathering
Dry, brittle coat from environmental exposure
Hot spots under ear leather
Irish Setter.extendResults: Mist feathered areas with a light detangling spray before brushing. A daily 5-minute brush of the ear feathering and chest alone prevents the worst matting. After outdoor activity, check legs and belly for burrs immediately rather than letting them dry and tangle deeper. Trim paw feathering to reduce debris collection.
Irish Setter.seasonalCare: Irish Setters shed moderately year-round with a slight increase in spring. In Houston heat, the feathered coat can trap warmth close to the body. Keep the belly and undercarriage feathering thinned during summer. The rich red coat can fade with excessive sun exposure, so avoid prolonged midday sun.
Irish Setter.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly with focus on feathering. Clean ears weekly. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Trim paw pads and feet feathering every 3 weeks. Check for debris after outdoor activity.

Portuguese Water Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush every other day with a slicker brush and metal comb for the curly coat type, or a pin brush for the wavy coat type. The non-shedding coat grows continuously and mats quickly without regular attention. Work methodically in sections from the skin outward. Use a detangling spray for any small tangles before they become mats.
Portuguese Water Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks using a moisturizing shampoo formulated for curly or wavy coats. Apply a hydrating conditioner and leave on for 3-5 minutes to maintain curl definition and prevent dryness. The curly coat type needs more conditioning than the wavy type. Dry with a low-heat dryer while fluffing to maintain proper coat texture.
commonIssues: Rapid matting without regular brushing, especially in curly coat type
Ear infections from water-loving activity and hairy ear canals
Coat becoming brittle from chlorine or salt water exposure
Uneven curl pattern after improper drying
Hair growing into ear canals requiring plucking
Portuguese Water Dog.extendResults: Rinse the coat thoroughly with fresh water after any swimming to remove chlorine or salt that damages coat texture. Use a leave-in conditioner after swimming. Maintain a consistent clip pattern to reduce daily brushing effort. The retriever clip and lion clip both work well between professional grooms when brushed regularly.
Portuguese Water Dog.seasonalCare: Portuguese Water Dogs do not shed seasonally, but their continuously growing coat requires consistent year-round grooming. In Houston summers, a shorter clip (1-2 inches) reduces heat burden and drying time after their frequent swims. In winter, a longer coat provides warmth but demands more brushing commitment.
Portuguese Water Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush every other day. Bathe every 3-4 weeks. Professional clip every 4-6 weeks. Clean and pluck ears biweekly. Rinse coat after every swim.

Airedale Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and metal comb to maintain their dense, wiry double coat. The hard, wiry outer coat lies close to the body while the undercoat is soft and short. Hand stripping with a stripping knife every 8-12 weeks maintains proper wire texture and the rich tan and black coloring. Comb through the leg furnishings and beard daily.
Airedale Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo for wire-coated breeds. Avoid conditioners on the body coat to preserve the wire texture. The Airedale's beard requires thorough cleaning during every bath. Blow dry while brushing through the furnishings to prevent matting.
commonIssues: Beard staining and odor
Leg furnishing tangles and mats
Coat softening from clipper cutting versus stripping
Skin irritation from dead coat buildup
Ear infections
Airedale Terrier.extendResults: Clean the beard daily after meals and water to prevent the notorious Airedale beard odor. Regular hand stripping maintains the characteristic hard, flat-lying wire coat that repels dirt and water. Keep leg furnishings combed and free of debris. A beard bib during meals reduces staining significantly.
Airedale Terrier.seasonalCare: Hand-stripped Airedales shed minimally year-round. Without stripping, dead coat builds up and causes skin irritation and dull coloring. The wire coat provides good weather protection in all seasons. Summer care may involve a shorter trim, especially in hot, humid climates. Winter coat provides natural insulation.
Airedale Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Comb beard and furnishings daily. Clean beard after meals. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Hand strip every 8-12 weeks or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim paw pads biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Scottish Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush on the body and a metal comb through the skirt, leg furnishings, and beard. The Scottie has a hard, wiry outer coat over a soft, dense undercoat. Hand-stripping maintains proper coat texture and color; clipping softens the coat and can lighten the color over time.
Scottish Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a texturizing shampoo for wire coats. Avoid conditioners on the body coat. Apply a light detangling spray only to the skirt and leg furnishings before combing. The beard and skirt should be thoroughly rinsed as they trap shampoo residue. Towel dry the body; blow dry the furnishings.
commonIssues: Skirt and furnishing matting from ground contact
Beard staining and food debris
Coat color fading from clipping instead of stripping
Skin allergies and scottie cramp
Bladder cancer (breed-predisposed, monitor skin lumps)
Scottish Terrier.extendResults: Wipe the beard and moustache after meals. The skirt picks up dirt and moisture from ground contact, so wipe or rinse the undercarriage after walks in wet conditions. Keep the skirt trimmed to a practical length that clears the ground. Monthly carding removes dead coat between stripping sessions.
