Scottish Terrier.seasonalCare: Scottish Terriers shed minimally when hand-stripped. In Houston's heat, keep the body coat shorter while maintaining the breed's characteristic silhouette. The dark coat absorbs heat, so schedule outdoor activity for early morning or evening. Monitor skin closely in warm months as the breed is predisposed to skin issues.
Scottish Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Clean beard daily. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks. Hand-strip every 6-8 weeks. Wipe skirt after wet walks. Monitor skin monthly.

Wheaten Terrier (Soft Coated).brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a medium-tooth metal comb or pin brush, working from the skin outward in small sections. The single-layer, silky coat mats rapidly and deceptively, often appearing fine on the surface while hiding tight mats close to the skin. Use a slicker brush gently on any tangles, then verify with a metal comb that reaches the skin.
Wheaten Terrier (Soft Coated).bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks using a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. Apply a lightweight conditioner and leave on for 3-5 minutes. The Wheaten coat texture changes dramatically based on products used. Avoid heavy conditioners that weigh down the coat or create a limp appearance. Blow dry on low heat while brushing to maintain the characteristic wavy texture.
commonIssues: Severe, hidden matting close to the skin
Coat texture changes with age (puppy coat transition is dramatic)
Protein-losing enteropathy and nephropathy (breed-specific)
Eye irritation from face fall
Dirty, stained coat around mouth and paws
Wheaten Terrier (Soft Coated).extendResults: Daily combing is not optional for this breed. Even 5 minutes of daily combing from the skin outward prevents the hidden mats that require shaving. Use a light leave-in conditioner spray before brushing. Keep the face fall trimmed so hair does not fall into the eyes. Wipe paws and mouth after meals and walks.
Wheaten Terrier (Soft Coated).seasonalCare: Wheaten Terriers do not shed seasonally but grow coat continuously. In Houston humidity, the coat may become limp and mat faster. Use a volumizing spray in humid conditions. The coat goes through a dramatic texture change between 12-24 months as the puppy coat transitions to the adult silky coat, requiring extra grooming attention during this period.
Wheaten Terrier (Soft Coated).maintenanceSchedule: Brush and comb daily. Bathe every 2-3 weeks. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks. Clean face daily. Verify coat is mat-free at skin level with a metal comb every session.

Old English Sheepdog.brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week minimum with a pin brush, slicker brush, and wide-tooth metal comb. This breed's shaggy double coat requires dedicated line brushing from the skin outward. Section the coat with clips and work systematically. The undercoat mats invisibly beneath the outer coat, so always verify with a comb at skin level.
Old English Sheepdog.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks using a moisturizing shampoo and heavy conditioner. The voluminous coat requires extended soak, rinse, and dry time. Budget 2-3 hours for a complete bath and dry. High-velocity drying is absolutely essential as the dense coat retains extraordinary amounts of water and can develop mildew if not fully dried.
commonIssues: Severe hidden matting requiring full shave-down if neglected
Pelting (mats fusing into felt-like sheets against skin)
Moisture retention leading to hot spots and skin infections
Eye irritation from face hair
Fecal matting in rear coat
Old English Sheepdog.extendResults: This breed requires the most consistent home maintenance of almost any breed. Daily brushing for 15-20 minutes is realistic for a full coat. Many OES owners and groomers opt for a practical 2-3 inch clip that preserves the breed look while dramatically reducing maintenance. Keep the rear and sanitary areas trimmed short for hygiene regardless of body length.
Old English Sheepdog.seasonalCare: Old English Sheepdogs shed their undercoat heavily in spring. In Houston heat, a shorter clip is strongly recommended for pet dogs. The full show coat in Houston humidity creates a high risk for skin infections and overheating. If maintaining length, professional grooming every 3 weeks with thorough drying is essential during summer.
Old English Sheepdog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly minimum (daily recommended). Professional groom every 3-4 weeks. Full drying mandatory at every bath. Sanitary trim biweekly. Eye area check daily.

Afghan Hound.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and wide-toothed comb -- this is non-negotiable for the Afghan's long, silky, flowing coat. Work in sections, misting with a detangling spray before brushing to prevent breakage. Never brush a dry Afghan coat. Use a slicker brush gently on any tangles, working from the ends upward.
Afghan Hound.bathingTips: Bathe weekly or every 10 days with a high-quality moisturizing or silk-protein shampoo, followed by a rich conditioner left on for 3-5 minutes. This coat requires the most bathing of nearly any breed to stay clean and mat-free. Use a force dryer on a warm, medium setting, drying in layers while brushing straight.
commonIssues: Severe matting that requires professional dematting or shaving if neglected
Coat breakage from improper brushing technique or dry brushing
Ear infections hidden under the heavily feathered ears
Hip dysplasia making extended grooming sessions uncomfortable
Eye irritation from long topknot hair
Afghan Hound.extendResults: Use a silk or satin snood to protect the topknot and ears during meals. Sleep on satin bedding or use a body suit to prevent friction matting. Tie up the topknot daily. Apply a leave-in conditioner or coat oil after every bath. The Afghan coat is a full-time commitment -- cutting corners leads to shaving.
Afghan Hound.seasonalCare: Afghans do not shed in the traditional sense but lose hair continuously like humans. In Houston's humidity, the long coat can develop a musty smell if not dried properly after baths. Summer may warrant a slightly shorter trim (with owner approval) for comfort, though show dogs maintain full coat year-round.
Afghan Hound.maintenanceSchedule: Daily brushing with pin brush (30-45 minutes), weekly bath with conditioner, daily topknot management, ear cleaning twice weekly, nail trims every 2-3 weeks, professional grooming weekly or bi-weekly.

Chow Chow.brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week with a pin brush, slicker brush, and undercoat rake. The immense double coat with its distinctive lion-like mane requires systematic section-by-section grooming. Focus heavily on the thick neck ruff, rear breeches, and behind the ears where matting is worst. Always brush the undercoat first with a long-tooth rake before finishing with a pin brush.
Chow Chow.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a de-shedding shampoo and follow with a heavy conditioner. The extraordinarily dense coat requires extended soak time to penetrate to the skin. High-velocity drying is mandatory and may take 60-90 minutes. An incompletely dried Chow coat will mat, develop hot spots, and produce a musty odor within hours.
commonIssues: Hidden matting under the dense outer coat, especially in mane and rear
Hot spots from moisture trapped in undercoat
Skin allergies and autoimmune skin conditions
Difficulty grooming due to breed temperament
Blue-black tongue pigment causing drool staining on light coats
Chow Chow.extendResults: Chow Chows often have strong opinions about grooming, so consistent, calm handling from puppyhood is essential. Use a high-velocity dryer at home between professional grooms to blow out loose undercoat weekly. Focus daily brushing on the mane and rear breeches where mats form fastest. A 10-minute daily session prevents the severe matting that requires sedated grooming.
Chow Chow.seasonalCare: Chow Chows blow their massive undercoat heavily in spring and moderately in fall. In Houston's heat, this breed is at extreme risk for overheating. Professional de-shedding every 3-4 weeks during summer is recommended. Never shave a Chow Chow as the dense coat provides critical insulation and sun protection for their sensitive skin.
Chow Chow.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Professional groom with full dry every 4-6 weeks. De-shedding treatment monthly in summer. Check skin under coat biweekly. Monitor for heat stress in warm weather.

Dalmatian.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a rubber curry brush, bristle brush, or grooming mitt. Despite the short coat, Dalmatians are surprisingly heavy shedders. Their short, stiff hairs embed in fabric and furniture, making regular brushing essential for household management. A rubber curry brush is most effective at lifting out loose hairs from the dense, short coat.
Dalmatian.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. The short coat dries quickly and requires minimal effort. Use a brightening shampoo to keep the white base coat crisp and prevent yellowing. Avoid harsh shampoos as Dalmatians can have sensitive skin. A thorough rubber curry session before bathing loosens dead hair for easier removal.
commonIssues: Constant heavy shedding of short, stiff hairs that embed in everything
Skin allergies and dermatitis
Deafness-related sensitivity during grooming (breed-predisposed)
Urinary stones requiring dietary management (affects skin health)
Dry skin and dandruff
Dalmatian.extendResults: Daily rubber curry sessions of just 2-3 minutes dramatically reduce the household hair problem. A damp rubber grooming glove after curry brushing picks up the remaining loose hairs. Supplement with omega-3 fatty acids to reduce shedding volume and improve skin health. A chamois cloth gives the spotted coat a beautiful shine.
Dalmatian.seasonalCare: Dalmatians shed heavily year-round with peaks in spring and fall. There is no low-shedding season for this breed. In Houston heat, their short coat provides good air circulation but minimal sun protection for the white-coated areas. Monitor for sunburn on the ears, nose, and any areas with thin hair coverage.
Dalmatian.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with rubber curry (daily is ideal). Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Check ears weekly for deafness-related anxiety during cleaning. Trim nails biweekly. Monitor skin for allergies monthly.

Corgi (Pembroke Welsh).brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The dense, weather-resistant double coat sheds heavily. Focus the undercoat rake on the thick rear breeches, neck ruff, and chest where the undercoat is densest. Their low-to-ground build means the belly coat picks up more debris than taller breeds.
Corgi (Pembroke Welsh).bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a de-shedding shampoo and conditioner formulated for double coats. Ensure water and shampoo penetrate the dense undercoat by massaging thoroughly. High-velocity drying is recommended as the thick coat retains moisture. Pay special attention to drying the belly and chest which stay damp longest due to ground proximity.
commonIssues: Heavy year-round shedding with seasonal blowouts
Belly and chest coat picking up ground-level dirt and moisture
Hot spots from retained moisture in dense undercoat
Intervertebral disc disease (IVDD) affecting grooming table positioning
Obesity contributing to skin fold issues
Corgi (Pembroke Welsh).extendResults: Daily brushing with a slicker brush for 5 minutes significantly reduces the hair tumbleweeds Corgis are famous for. Focus on the rear breeches and pantaloons where the most undercoat accumulates. Keep the belly trimmed slightly shorter for hygiene and to reduce debris pickup. A de-shedding tool used weekly between grooms makes a visible difference.
Corgi (Pembroke Welsh).seasonalCare: Corgis blow their undercoat heavily twice a year, typically in spring and fall, producing astounding volumes of loose fur. During the 2-3 week blow period, daily raking is essential. In Houston summers, professional de-shedding every 4-5 weeks helps manage the coat. Their low body and thick coat make them moderately heat-sensitive; monitor for overheating.
Corgi (Pembroke Welsh).maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks. De-shedding treatment during seasonal blows. Trim belly and paw pads monthly. Wipe belly after walks in wet conditions.

Cane Corso.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt to remove loose hair and distribute natural oils. During shedding season, increase to 2-3 times per week with a deshedding tool. Their short, dense double coat is low-maintenance but sheds more than most owners expect.
Cane Corso.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo formulated for short coats. Avoid over-bathing as it strips the natural oils that keep their coat water-resistant. A deodorizing spray between baths helps keep them fresh without drying out the skin.
commonIssues: Skin fold infections around the jowls and face
Excessive drooling causing chin and chest staining
Seasonal shedding heavier than expected for a short coat
Ear infections due to floppy ear structure trapping moisture
Dry, flaky skin from over-bathing
Cane Corso.extendResults: Wipe facial folds daily with a damp cloth or grooming wipe to prevent bacterial buildup. Keep a drool rag handy and clean the chin area after meals and water. Apply a light coat conditioner spray weekly to maintain shine between grooms.
Cane Corso.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding occurs in spring and fall as the double coat transitions. In Houston summers, ensure they stay cool -- their dark coat absorbs heat rapidly. Winter coat grows denser, requiring more frequent brushing to prevent loose undercoat buildup.
Cane Corso.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly full-body brushing with curry brush, daily face fold cleaning, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly, professional groom every 6-8 weeks. Check skin folds for redness or odor at each brushing session.

Belgian Malinois.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. Their short, dense double coat sheds year-round with heavy blowouts twice yearly. A rubber curry brush works well for quick maintenance sessions and stimulates blood flow to the skin.
Belgian Malinois.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a protein-enriched shampoo to support their active coat. These high-energy dogs get dirty frequently, so waterless shampoo or grooming wipes are excellent between baths. Rinse thoroughly -- residue in the dense undercoat causes irritation.
commonIssues: Year-round shedding that intensifies seasonally
Hot spots from moisture trapped in undercoat during humid weather
Dry skin from frequent bathing due to active outdoor lifestyle
Nail wear varies -- working dogs may self-maintain, pets need regular trims
Skin irritation from environmental allergens
Belgian Malinois.extendResults: Use a high-velocity dryer after any wet outdoor activity to prevent moisture from sitting in the undercoat. Apply an omega-3 supplement to support coat health and reduce shedding. Quick daily brush-throughs with a bristle brush keep the coat looking fresh between grooms.
Belgian Malinois.seasonalCare: Spring and fall bring intense coat blowouts lasting 2-3 weeks -- daily brushing with an undercoat rake is essential during these periods. Houston heat requires extra attention to hydration and preventing hot spots. Winter coat is significantly denser than summer coat.
Belgian Malinois.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly minimum, daily during coat blowouts. Nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear check weekly, professional deshedding treatment every 6-8 weeks. Paw pad check after outdoor activities.

Bloodhound.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or hound glove to remove loose hair and massage the skin. Their short, dense coat is relatively low-maintenance but requires consistent attention to skin folds. A bristle brush works well for finishing and distributing oils.
Bloodhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a mild, hypoallergenic shampoo -- their sensitive skin reacts to harsh chemicals. Pay special attention to cleaning all facial wrinkles and ear folds during bath time. Use a medicated shampoo if skin fold dermatitis is present.
commonIssues: Chronic ear infections due to long, heavy ears trapping moisture and debris
Skin fold infections in facial wrinkles requiring daily attention
Strong hound odor that builds quickly between baths
Excessive drooling causing skin irritation on chin and chest
Eye infections from droopy lower eyelids collecting debris
Bloodhound.extendResults: Clean facial wrinkles daily with antibacterial wipes and dry thoroughly. Flip ears back for 10-15 minutes daily to air out the ear canal. Use a deodorizing spray formulated for hound breeds between baths. Keep chin area dry to prevent drool-related skin breakdown.
Bloodhound.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round with slight increase in spring. Houston humidity amplifies their natural hound odor and increases risk of skin fold infections. Summer requires extra vigilance with ear cleaning and wrinkle care. Winter is their most comfortable season coat-wise.
Bloodhound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing, daily wrinkle cleaning, ear cleaning 2-3 times weekly, eye area cleaning daily, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks with thorough ear and wrinkle treatment.

English Springer Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a slicker brush and medium-toothed comb, focusing on feathering on ears, chest, legs, and belly. Use a pin brush for the body coat and detangling spray for stubborn mats. Pay extra attention behind the ears where mats form fastest.
English Springer Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a moisturizing shampoo and conditioner to keep feathering soft and tangle-free. Their medium-length coat benefits from a cream rinse that makes post-bath brushing easier. Dry ears thoroughly to prevent moisture-related infections.
commonIssues: Matting in ear feathering, armpits, and behind ears
Ear infections from long, heavy, furred ears
Burrs and debris caught in leg and belly feathering
Tear staining around eyes
Skin allergies causing itching and coat dullness
English Springer Spaniel.extendResults: Comb through feathering every other day to catch tangles before they become mats. Apply a leave-in conditioner spray to feathered areas after brushing. Keep ear hair trimmed to improve airflow and reduce infection risk. Wipe under eyes daily to prevent staining.
English Springer Spaniel.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round with heavier periods in spring and fall. Summer requires trimming the feathering shorter for comfort and to reduce debris pickup during outdoor play. Winter coat grows thicker and feathering becomes denser, requiring more frequent brushing.
English Springer Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush feathering 3-4 times weekly, full body brush twice weekly, ear cleaning weekly, eye area cleaning daily, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom with sanitary trim every 6-8 weeks.

Brittany.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush for the body and a medium comb for the light feathering on ears and legs. Their flat to wavy coat is relatively easy to maintain. Focus on the areas behind ears and where the legs meet the body where tangles develop.
Brittany.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, sport-dog formula shampoo that cleans without stripping natural oils. These active sporting dogs benefit from a quick rinse after field work or swimming. Use a light conditioner on feathered areas only.
commonIssues: Ear infections from floppy ears trapping moisture
Tangles in leg and ear feathering after outdoor activity
Seasonal shedding can be heavier than expected
Skin irritation from field debris and grass seeds
Dry nose and paw pads from active outdoor lifestyle
Brittany.extendResults: Check ears after every outdoor adventure and wipe with a pH-balanced ear cleaner. Spray a light detangler on feathering before outdoor play to prevent debris from embedding. A weekly coconut oil treatment on paw pads keeps them supple and crack-free.
Brittany.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding in spring and fall. Summer coat is lighter and shorter -- keep feathering trimmed for comfort in Houston heat. Winter coat grows denser. Active field dogs need more frequent post-adventure grooming regardless of season.
Brittany.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, ear cleaning weekly, paw pad check after outdoor activity, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks with ear trim and sanitary clip.

Papillon.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and fine-toothed comb. Focus on the long ear fringes, chest ruff, and pant feathering where tangles form. Despite their luxurious appearance, Papillons have a single coat without undercoat, making grooming easier than expected.
Papillon.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a gentle, silk-protein shampoo that enhances their fine, flowing coat. Use a lightweight conditioner to prevent tangles without weighing down the hair. Their single coat dries quickly compared to double-coated breeds.
commonIssues: Tear staining below eyes, especially in lighter-colored dogs
Tangles in ear fringes if not brushed regularly
Dental issues that affect overall health and coat condition
Patellar luxation causing gait changes that affect nail wear
Fine coat breaks easily if brushed while dry
Papillon.extendResults: Mist the coat lightly with water or detangling spray before brushing to prevent breakage. Clean under eyes daily with a tear stain remover pad. Keep ear fringes combed out after meals to prevent food debris from causing tangles. A silk pillowcase for their bed helps prevent coat friction.
Papillon.seasonalCare: Light shedding year-round with no major seasonal blowout due to the single coat. Houston heat is generally well-tolerated but sun protection for the ear tips is important -- the thin skin burns easily. Winter requires no significant coat changes.
Papillon.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with misting, daily eye area cleaning, ear fringe comb-out after meals, dental care daily, nail trim every 2-3 weeks. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks.

Miniature Pinscher.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a soft bristle brush or grooming mitt -- their smooth, short coat is among the easiest to maintain. A rubber curry brush during shedding season helps remove loose hair efficiently. Quick daily wipe-downs with a grooming cloth keep the coat gleaming.
Miniature Pinscher.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle shampoo for sensitive skin. Their thin coat provides minimal insulation, so use warm water and dry them quickly and thoroughly. Oatmeal-based shampoos work well for their occasionally sensitive skin.
commonIssues: Skin sensitivity and dryness, especially in winter
Patellar luxation affecting movement and nail wear patterns
Cold sensitivity due to minimal coat -- not a grooming issue but affects care
Color dilution alopecia in blue and fawn varieties
Dental disease that impacts overall health
Miniature Pinscher.extendResults: Apply a light moisturizing spray weekly to prevent dry skin. Their coat shows dirt quickly but also cleans up fast with a damp cloth between baths. Keep nails trimmed short -- Min Pins are prone to overgrown nails that affect their prancing gait.
Miniature Pinscher.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding year-round with a slight increase in spring. Houston summers are well-tolerated with their short coat, but provide sun protection for extended outdoor time. Winter requires clothing for outdoor walks -- their thin coat provides almost no insulation.
Miniature Pinscher.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing, daily wipe-down, nail trim every 2-3 weeks, ear cleaning biweekly, dental care daily. Professional groom every 8-10 weeks -- mainly for nail, ear, and sanitary maintenance.

Chinese Crested.brushingRoutine: For Hairless variety: no brushing needed on the body, but gently brush the crest, plume tail, and socks with a soft pin brush. For Powderpuff variety: brush daily with a pin brush and wide-toothed comb to prevent the fine, silky coat from matting. The Powderpuff's coat mats severely if neglected.
Chinese Crested.bathingTips: Hairless: bathe weekly with a gentle, non-comedogenic cleanser and apply a fragrance-free moisturizer or sunscreen afterward. Powderpuff: bathe every 2-3 weeks with a silk-protein shampoo and deep conditioner. Both varieties need careful skin attention -- the hairless variety is essentially a skincare regimen, not a coat care one.
commonIssues: Acne and blackheads on hairless body (Hairless variety)
Sunburn on exposed skin requiring SPF protection
Severe matting in Powderpuff coat if brushing is missed
Dry, flaky skin requiring regular moisturizing
Dental issues including missing or crowded teeth affecting health
Chinese Crested.extendResults: Hairless: apply a lightweight, non-comedogenic moisturizer daily and sunscreen before any sun exposure. Exfoliate gently weekly to prevent blackheads. Powderpuff: use a leave-in conditioner spray daily and keep the coat in a light wrap or bands to prevent tangling between grooms.
Chinese Crested.seasonalCare: Hairless variety needs heavy sun protection in Houston summers -- SPF clothing or pet-safe sunscreen is mandatory. Winter requires sweaters and skin moisturizing against dry air. Powderpuff sheds minimally year-round. Both varieties are hypoallergenic.
Chinese Crested.maintenanceSchedule: Hairless: weekly bath, daily moisturizing, sunscreen before outdoor time, weekly exfoliation. Powderpuff: daily brushing, bath every 2-3 weeks. Both: nail trim every 2-3 weeks, dental care daily. Professional groom every 3-4 weeks.

Italian Greyhound.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a soft rubber grooming mitt or chamois cloth -- their ultra-short, fine coat requires minimal brushing. The main purpose is removing loose hair and stimulating skin circulation. A soft bristle brush works for sensitive areas.
Italian Greyhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a hypoallergenic, moisturizing shampoo. Their thin skin and minimal coat mean they chill easily -- use warm water and towel-dry immediately, followed by a low-heat blow dryer. Avoid harsh shampoos that strip their already minimal skin oils.
commonIssues: Extremely thin skin that tears and bruises easily
Dental disease is chronic and severe in this breed
Cold sensitivity requiring clothing in cool weather
Skin allergies manifesting as rashes and dryness
Leg fractures from jumping affect mobility and nail maintenance
Italian Greyhound.extendResults: Apply a light skin moisturizer weekly to prevent dryness. A chamois cloth rubdown daily gives their coat a beautiful sheen with minimal effort. Keep nails extremely short -- their thin legs put extra stress on overgrown nails. Dental care is critical for overall health.
Italian Greyhound.seasonalCare: Almost no shedding regardless of season. Houston summers suit them well with their thin coat, but provide shade during peak sun hours. Winter requires insulated clothing for outdoor walks. Their thin skin sunburns -- apply pet-safe sunscreen for extended outdoor time.
Italian Greyhound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly grooming mitt rubdown, daily chamois cloth shine, nail trim every 2 weeks, dental care daily, ear cleaning biweekly. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks for thorough nail, dental, and skin check.

Whippet.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or hound glove to remove loose hair and promote skin health. Their short, dense coat is very low-maintenance. A chamois cloth after brushing adds shine. During shedding periods, increase to twice weekly.
Whippet.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, moisturizing shampoo. Their short coat dries quickly, but their lean body means they get cold fast -- towel dry promptly. A gentle oatmeal shampoo works well for their occasionally sensitive skin.
commonIssues: Thin skin that is prone to cuts, scrapes, and pressure sores
Dry skin in winter months
Sensitivity to anesthesia (important for any sedated grooming)
Calluses on elbows and hocks from lying on hard surfaces
Dental issues requiring attention
Whippet.extendResults: Use a vitamin E oil on any calluses forming on elbows and hocks. Provide soft bedding to prevent pressure sores. A daily chamois cloth wipe keeps the coat gleaming with almost no effort. Light coconut oil on dry patches keeps skin supple between baths.
Whippet.seasonalCare: Light shedding year-round with a slight spring increase. Houston heat is generally well-tolerated but they need shade during peak sun hours. Winter requires a fitted coat or sweater for outdoor walks -- they have almost no body fat for insulation.
Whippet.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with curry brush, daily chamois wipe optional, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning biweekly, dental care daily. Professional groom every 8-10 weeks.

Rhodesian Ridgeback.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or bristle brush to remove loose hair and distribute natural oils. Focus on the ridge area where the coat grows in the opposite direction -- this area can trap debris. A grooming mitt works well for quick maintenance sessions.
Rhodesian Ridgeback.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, wheat-protein shampoo that enhances their coat's natural luster. Their short, dense coat is naturally clean and odor-resistant. Avoid over-bathing -- their coat has natural oils that repel dirt.
commonIssues: Dermoid sinus along the ridge (congenital -- groomer should be aware)
Moderate shedding that surprises owners of short-coated breeds
Elbow calluses from lying on hard surfaces
Ear infections in warm, humid climates
Seasonal allergies causing skin irritation
Rhodesian Ridgeback.extendResults: Run your hands along the ridge regularly to check for any lumps or irregularities -- early detection of dermoid sinus issues is critical. A daily rubdown with a grooming mitt keeps the coat shiny and removes loose hair before it hits the furniture. Use a deodorizing spray monthly between baths.
Rhodesian Ridgeback.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding in spring and fall. Their short coat handles Houston heat relatively well, but provide plenty of shade and water. The ridge area can accumulate more dirt and debris in humid conditions. Winter coat thickens slightly.
Rhodesian Ridgeback.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing, ridge inspection at each session, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly in humid months, elbow callus treatment as needed. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks.

Alaskan Malamute.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly minimum with an undercoat rake and slicker brush. Their thick double coat requires serious commitment -- daily brushing during coat blowouts is non-negotiable. Use a long-toothed undercoat rake to reach the dense woolly undercoat, followed by a pin brush for the outer guard coat.
Alaskan Malamute.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a deshedding shampoo and conditioner system. Thoroughly soak the coat down to the skin -- their dense undercoat is nearly waterproof. A high-velocity dryer is essential for removing loose undercoat and ensuring complete drying. Never let a Malamute air-dry -- trapped moisture causes hot spots.
commonIssues: Extreme seasonal coat blowouts lasting 2-4 weeks twice yearly
Hot spots from moisture trapped in the dense undercoat
Heat stress in warm climates -- Houston requires serious management
Zinc-responsive dermatosis causing crusty, flaky skin
Matting in the dense undercoat if brushing is skipped
Alaskan Malamute.extendResults: A professional deshedding treatment every 6-8 weeks removes undercoat that home brushing misses. Use a high-velocity dryer at home after any water exposure. Apply a coat conditioning spray before brushing to reduce breakage and make undercoat removal easier. Never shave a Malamute -- the double coat provides sun and heat protection.
Alaskan Malamute.seasonalCare: Massive coat blowouts in spring and fall -- daily raking is essential for 2-4 weeks. Houston summers are dangerous for this arctic breed: keep them in air conditioning, limit outdoor time to early morning and evening, and maintain the coat for proper insulation. NEVER shave them thinking it helps with heat -- it makes it worse. Winter is their most comfortable season.
Alaskan Malamute.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during blowouts. Undercoat rake session twice weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional deshedding groom every 6-8 weeks -- this breed benefits enormously from professional blowout treatments.

Great Pyrenees.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a long-toothed slicker brush and undercoat rake. Their thick, white double coat mats easily behind the ears, in the armpits, and on the hindquarters. Use a wide-toothed comb to check for mats after brushing. A pin brush works well for the outer coat finishing.
Great Pyrenees.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a brightening or whitening shampoo to maintain their pristine white coat. Pre-soak with a degreasing shampoo for heavily soiled areas. Their coat is naturally dirt-resistant -- mud often dries and brushes right out. A thorough blow-dry with a high-velocity dryer is essential to prevent matting and hot spots.
commonIssues: Matting in armpits, behind ears, and hindquarter feathering
Staining on white coat from tears, saliva, and outdoor debris
Double dewclaws (breed standard) require careful nail maintenance
Hot spots in humid climates from moisture trapped in dense coat
Excessive shedding creating tumbleweeds of white fur
Great Pyrenees.extendResults: Apply a leave-in whitening conditioner after baths to keep the coat bright between grooms. Check for mats daily by running your fingers through coat at mat-prone areas -- 30 seconds of prevention saves hours of detangling. Clean around eyes daily to prevent tear staining on white fur.
Great Pyrenees.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding in spring as the winter undercoat releases -- daily raking required for 3-4 weeks. Houston summers demand vigilant coat care: maintain the coat for insulation but ensure thorough drying after any water exposure. Never shave -- the white coat reflects sun and provides UV protection. Winter coat is at its fullest and most magnificent.
Great Pyrenees.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily mat checks, nail trim every 3-4 weeks including double dewclaws, ear cleaning weekly, eye area cleaning daily. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks with deshedding and whitening treatment.

Keeshond.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush, slicker brush, and undercoat rake in rotation. Their plush double coat with the distinctive mane and plumed tail requires thorough, systematic brushing. Always brush in layers, lifting the top coat to reach the dense undercoat beneath.
Keeshond.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a volumizing shampoo that supports their standoff coat texture. Use a heavy conditioner on the body but avoid the mane area where you want maximum volume. High-velocity blow-drying is essential -- force-drying removes tremendous undercoat and prevents matting.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding with impressive coat blowouts
Matting in the dense undercoat especially around the ruff and hindquarters
Hot spots in humid climates from trapped moisture
Skin allergies causing excessive scratching and coat damage
Black skin disease (alopecia X) -- a breed-specific concern
Keeshond.extendResults: Use a line-brushing technique to ensure every layer of the coat is reached. Apply an anti-static finishing spray to the ruff and plumed tail for a show-quality look between grooms. A weekly undercoat raking session prevents mat formation in the dense underbelly and hindquarters.
Keeshond.seasonalCare: Major coat blowouts twice yearly in spring and fall -- daily brushing with undercoat tools is essential for 3-4 weeks. Houston heat requires coat maintenance for insulation but extra vigilance against hot spots. Never shave a Keeshond -- the coat regrows incorrectly and they lose thermal regulation.
Keeshond.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly using line-brushing technique, undercoat rake weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom with full blowout every 6-8 weeks.

American Eskimo Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. Their brilliant white double coat with a standoff texture tangles easily if neglected. Focus on the ruff, behind the ears, and the pantaloons on the hind legs. A slicker brush works for removing small tangles before they become mats.
American Eskimo Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a whitening shampoo followed by a lightweight conditioner. Their white coat shows every stain, so a pre-bath whitening treatment on tear-stained or discolored areas helps immensely. Blow-dry completely with a high-velocity dryer to maintain the standoff coat texture.
commonIssues: Tear staining highly visible on pure white coat
Matting in undercoat if brushing is inconsistent
Skin allergies and hot spots, especially in humid climates
Heavy seasonal shedding creating white fur everywhere
Dry, itchy skin if bathed too frequently or with harsh products
American Eskimo Dog.extendResults: Clean under eyes daily with a tear stain remover to prevent brown streaks on white fur. Spritz with a whitening maintenance spray between baths. Brush in sections using the line-brushing method to reach the dense undercoat. A humidifier in winter helps prevent the dry skin that leads to excessive scratching.
American Eskimo Dog.seasonalCare: Heavy coat blowouts twice yearly -- daily brushing required during these periods. Houston heat demands that you never shave the coat but keep it well-maintained for proper air circulation. Apply pet-safe sunscreen on the nose and ear tips in summer. Winter is their element -- the coat is at its most impressive.
American Eskimo Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily eye area cleaning, undercoat rake session twice weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional whitening groom every 4-6 weeks.

Tibetan Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush daily or every other day with a pin brush and wide-toothed comb. Their long, profuse double coat falls naturally and mats easily if not maintained. Use a detangling spray before brushing and work from the ends up to the roots. Part the coat and brush in sections to reach the dense undercoat.
Tibetan Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and deep conditioner designed for long coats. Their coat texture benefits from a cream rinse that provides slip for detangling. Always detangle completely before bathing -- water tightens existing mats into felt-like knots.
commonIssues: Severe matting if daily brushing is missed even briefly
Eye irritation from hair falling into eyes
Debris, twigs, and burrs trapped in the long coat
Ear infections from heavy ear hair restricting airflow
Interdigital mats between the large, flat paw pads
Tibetan Terrier.extendResults: Use a leave-in conditioner spray daily to maintain coat slip and prevent matting. Keep the hair above the eyes trimmed or banded to prevent irritation. Check between the toes daily for mats and debris -- their large, flat snowshoe-like feet are mat magnets. A weekly deep conditioning treatment keeps the coat manageable.
Tibetan Terrier.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding trapped within the coat (meaning mats, not furniture fur). Houston heat may warrant a puppy clip for comfort -- discuss with your groomer. The full coat provides some sun protection but can be uncomfortable in extreme humidity. Many owners opt for a shorter trim in summer months.
Tibetan Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily or every other day, full comb-through twice weekly, paw pad check daily, eye area trim as needed, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks.

Japanese Chin.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and fine-toothed comb. Their silky single coat is surprisingly easy to maintain despite its elegant appearance. Focus on behind the ears, the chest ruff, and the plumed tail where tangles develop. A slicker brush works for minor tangles.
Japanese Chin.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a silk-protein shampoo that enhances their fine, flowing coat. Use a lightweight conditioner to maintain slip and prevent tangling. Their flat face requires careful washing -- avoid getting water in the shallow nasal passages. Dry the facial folds thoroughly.
commonIssues: Tear staining around prominent eyes on lighter coat colors
Breathing difficulties during grooming due to brachycephalic face
Eye proptosis risk -- handle the head gently during grooming
Skin fold moisture around the flat face
Heat intolerance making grooming sessions stressful in warm environments
Japanese Chin.extendResults: Clean facial folds and under eyes daily with a gentle wipe. Keep grooming sessions short and cool -- their flat face makes breathing harder during stress. A light silk spray after brushing adds sheen to their glamorous coat. Ensure the grooming environment is well air-conditioned.
Japanese Chin.seasonalCare: Light to moderate shedding year-round with a slight seasonal increase. Houston heat is dangerous for this brachycephalic breed -- keep them in air conditioning and schedule grooming appointments during the coolest part of the day. Their single coat does not provide heavy insulation in winter.
Japanese Chin.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily eye and facial fold cleaning, nail trim every 2-3 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks in a cool, calm environment.

Pekingese.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and wide-toothed comb to prevent the profuse double coat from matting. Work in sections, lifting the long outer coat to reach the dense undercoat. Pay special attention to behind the ears, around the neck ruff, the skirt, and the feathered hindquarters.
Pekingese.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and heavy conditioner to maintain coat manageability. Thoroughly detangle before bathing -- wet mats become cement. Their flat face requires careful washing; clean facial wrinkles with a separate cloth. Complete blow-drying is essential to prevent matting and skin issues.
commonIssues: Severe matting throughout the dense double coat
Skin fold infections in deep facial wrinkles
Breathing difficulties during grooming due to extreme brachycephalic face
Tear staining around prominent eyes
Interdigital mats and debris in the feathered feet
Pekingese.extendResults: Daily facial wrinkle cleaning is non-negotiable -- use an antibacterial wipe and dry thoroughly. Apply cornstarch to wrinkles after cleaning to keep them dry. Use a detangling spray before every brushing session. Keep the coat slightly shorter if the owner cannot commit to daily brushing -- a lion clip is a popular, adorable option.
Pekingese.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding in spring and fall trapped within the long coat, causing severe matting if not brushed daily. Houston heat is extremely dangerous for Pekingese -- they are the most heat-sensitive of all breeds. Schedule grooming in early morning, keep sessions short, and ensure the environment is heavily air-conditioned.
Pekingese.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily, facial wrinkle cleaning twice daily, eye area cleaning daily, full comb-through every other day, nail trim every 2-3 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom every 3-4 weeks.

Brussels Griffon.brushingRoutine: Rough-coated variety: brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and hand-strip or clipper the wiry coat every 8-12 weeks. Smooth-coated variety: weekly brushing with a rubber curry brush or bristle brush. The rough coat requires more maintenance but sheds significantly less than the smooth.
Brussels Griffon.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle shampoo appropriate for the coat type. Rough coats benefit from a texturizing shampoo that maintains the wiry feel. Smooth coats do well with a basic moisturizing formula. Keep water away from the flat face and clean facial wrinkles separately.
commonIssues: Eye proptosis risk from the prominent, large eyes -- handle head area gently
Breathing difficulties due to brachycephalic face during grooming
Rough coat requires hand-stripping for proper texture maintenance
Tear staining around large, expressive eyes
Skin sensitivity and allergies
Brussels Griffon.extendResults: Clean around the eyes daily to prevent tear staining and debris buildup. For rough coats, maintain texture between grooms with a stripping stone on the beard and furnishings. Keep grooming sessions brief and in cool environments to accommodate their breathing limitations. A damp cloth wipe-down between baths keeps both coat types fresh.
Brussels Griffon.seasonalCare: Rough coats shed minimally year-round. Smooth coats have moderate seasonal shedding in spring and fall. Houston heat requires careful management for this brachycephalic breed -- schedule grooming during cool hours and keep sessions short. Both coat types are manageable in winter.
Brussels Griffon.maintenanceSchedule: Rough: brush 2-3 times weekly, hand-strip or clip every 8-12 weeks. Smooth: brush weekly. Both: daily eye cleaning, facial fold care, nail trim every 2-3 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks.

Silky Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and fine-toothed comb. Their long, silky coat parts down the spine and flows to the floor if kept in full coat. Work through the coat in sections, spraying with a light detangler before brushing. The fine texture tangles easily and breaks if brushed dry.
Silky Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a silk-protein shampoo and lightweight conditioner to maintain the signature glossy, flowing coat. Rinse thoroughly -- residue dulls the silky sheen. A cream rinse helps with post-bath combing. Blow-dry on low heat following the coat's natural fall direction.
commonIssues: Coat breakage from brushing without moisture or detangler
Tangles and mats forming rapidly if daily brushing is missed
Dental disease affecting overall health and coat quality
Patellar luxation affecting gait and nail wear
Tracheal collapse requiring gentle handling around the neck
Silky Terrier.extendResults: Never brush the coat dry -- always mist with water or detangling spray first. Use a silk-finish spray after brushing for an extra-glossy look. Wrap the coat in tissue paper bands if growing a show-length coat. For pet owners, a modified trim keeps the coat manageable while preserving the silky look.
Silky Terrier.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding year-round due to the single coat texture similar to human hair. Houston humidity can cause the coat to frizz -- use an anti-humidity finishing spray. No significant seasonal coat changes, making them one of the more consistent breeds for grooming year-round.
Silky Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with detangling spray, bath every 2-3 weeks, nail trim every 2-3 weeks, dental care daily, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks with trim maintenance.

Norwich Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and stripping knife or comb. Their wiry, weather-resistant double coat benefits from hand-stripping every 8-12 weeks to maintain proper texture and color. Clipping softens the coat over time. A greyhound comb helps check for tangles in the undercoat.
Norwich Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that maintains the wiry coat quality. Avoid heavy conditioners that soften the coat texture. If the coat is hand-stripped, wait at least two weeks after stripping before bathing. A dry shampoo between baths helps manage the terrier-typical odor.
commonIssues: Coat texture changes to soft and fluffy if clipped instead of hand-stripped
Skin irritation after hand-stripping sessions
Ear infections from small, erect ears collecting debris
Dental crowding in their small jaws
Tracheal sensitivity requiring gentle handling during grooming
Norwich Terrier.extendResults: Between hand-stripping sessions, use a stripping stone or Mars Coat King to maintain coat texture. Keep the furnishings (eyebrows and beard) combed and tidy between grooms. A light coat spray helps maintain the weather-resistant quality of the wiry outer coat.
Norwich Terrier.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding year-round when properly hand-stripped -- the dead coat is removed manually rather than shed naturally. Spring and fall are ideal times for full hand-stripping sessions. Houston heat is manageable with their wiry coat, which provides natural UV protection.
Norwich Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, stripping stone maintenance weekly, hand-strip or clip every 8-12 weeks, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly, dental care daily. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks.

Cairn Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush, working through the harsh, weather-resistant outer coat to reach the soft, dense undercoat. A wide-toothed comb through the furnishings keeps the eyebrows and beard looking tidy. Hand-stripping every 8-12 weeks maintains proper coat texture and color.
Cairn Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a clarifying or texturizing shampoo. Avoid conditioners that soften the naturally harsh coat texture. Their double coat takes longer to dry than expected -- use a high-velocity dryer to ensure complete drying down to the undercoat.
commonIssues: Coat softening and color fading when clipped instead of hand-stripped
Matting in the soft undercoat if brushing is inconsistent
Skin allergies causing itching and hot spots
Patellar luxation affecting gait
Flea allergy dermatitis -- terriers seem particularly reactive
Cairn Terrier.extendResults: Use a stripping stone between professional sessions to maintain coat texture. Keep the facial furnishings trimmed enough to see the eyes clearly but long enough for the classic terrier expression. A weekly undercoat comb-through prevents matting at the skin level.
Cairn Terrier.seasonalCare: Low shedding when hand-stripped -- the dead coat is manually removed. If clipped, moderate shedding occurs year-round. Houston summers are manageable with their natural coat. Their weather-resistant outer coat actually helps insulate against heat. Spring and fall are optimal for hand-stripping sessions.
Cairn Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, facial furnishing trim as needed, hand-strip or clip every 8-12 weeks, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning biweekly. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks.

Wire Fox Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 3 times weekly with a slicker brush and pin brush combination. Their dense, wiry coat with tight twists requires hand-stripping every 8-10 weeks to maintain the classic Wire Fox look. Use a stripping knife or stone between sessions to keep the coat in rolling rotation. Focus on the furnishings -- legs, beard, and eyebrows.
Wire Fox Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that preserves the wiry coat quality. Never use softening conditioners on the body coat -- save conditioner only for the leg and facial furnishings. Their white base coat benefits from a brightening shampoo to remove any yellowing.
commonIssues: Coat texture loss when clipped repeatedly instead of hand-stripped
Staining on white coat areas from food, tears, and outdoor debris
Skin irritation and bumps after hand-stripping
Furnishing tangles in the leg and beard hair
Atopic dermatitis and skin allergies
Wire Fox Terrier.extendResults: Maintain the leg furnishings with daily combing to prevent tangles. Clean the beard after every meal to prevent staining and food buildup. Use a whitening spray on the body coat between baths. Keep the coat in rolling strip rotation for the best texture and color year-round.
Wire Fox Terrier.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding when properly hand-stripped. Clipped coats shed moderately year-round. The wiry coat handles Houston heat reasonably well -- it provides UV protection while allowing airflow. Schedule full stripping sessions for spring and fall transitions.
Wire Fox Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3 times weekly, daily beard cleaning, leg furnishing comb-out every other day, hand-strip every 8-10 weeks, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks.

Bedlington Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Bedlington's unique linty, crisp coat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and comb. The coat is a distinctive mix of hard and soft hair that forms a thick, linty texture. Brush in the direction of growth, then fluff upward for the breed's characteristic rounded appearance. Focus on the topknot, ear tassels, and leg furnishings which define the breed silhouette.
Bedlington Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that maintains the coat's crisp linty feel. Avoid heavy conditioners that soften the coat too much and lose the breed-specific texture. Blow-dry while scissoring and shaping the coat into the distinctive lamb-like silhouette. The Bedlington's coat should feel firm to the touch, not silky.
commonIssues: Coat texture loss from improper products or over-conditioning
Topknot shaping challenges between professional grooms
Ear tassel tangling
Copper toxicosis affecting coat quality in some lines
Skin sensitivity requiring gentle products
Bedlington Terrier.extendResults: Use a texturizing spray between grooms to maintain the crisp coat feel. The Bedlington's trim requires skilled scissoring; spot-trim the face and ear tassels between full grooms to maintain the breed outline. A light chalking on the topknot can maintain shape between appointments.
Bedlington Terrier.seasonalCare: The Bedlington's coat does not shed seasonally but grows continuously. In humid summers, the coat may lose its crisp texture; use volumizing and texturizing products. In winter, the thick coat provides good insulation. Regular trimming every 6-8 weeks maintains the breed-specific shape regardless of season.
Bedlington Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Comb through ear tassels every other day. Spot-trim face between grooms. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming with full scissor trim every 6-8 weeks.

Kerry Blue Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush daily or every other day with a slicker brush and wide-toothed comb. Their soft, wavy, non-shedding coat mats quickly if neglected. Work through the coat in sections, starting from the skin outward. The distinctive blue-gray coat requires consistent maintenance to look its best. Use a detangling spray for stubborn areas.
Kerry Blue Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a color-enhancing shampoo formulated for gray or silver coats. A moisturizing conditioner keeps the soft, wavy coat manageable. Their coat absorbs and holds water, so thorough blow-drying is essential. Use a slicker brush while blow-drying to straighten and separate the coat.
commonIssues: Rapid matting if brushing is skipped even a few days
Coat color changes from black at birth through multiple shades to blue-gray
Ear infections from heavy ear hair blocking airflow
Skin cysts and growths common in the breed
Eye irritation from facial hair if not trimmed properly
Kerry Blue Terrier.extendResults: Maintain the facial trim between professional grooms by combing the beard after meals and keeping fall-away hair from the eyes trimmed. A weekly deep-combing session catches mats before they tighten. Use a leave-in conditioner spray daily to maintain coat slip and prevent tangles.
Kerry Blue Terrier.seasonalCare: No seasonal shedding -- the coat grows continuously and requires year-round trimming. Houston humidity can cause the wavy coat to frizz and mat faster. Use an anti-humidity spray during summer months. The dense coat provides insulation in both heat and cold but requires extra grooming attention in humid weather.
Kerry Blue Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily or every other day, beard cleaning after meals, full comb-through 3 times weekly, bath every 2-3 weeks, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly with ear hair removal. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks.

Irish Wolfhound.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and wide-toothed comb to work through the rough, wiry double coat. Hand-strip the coat 2-3 times per year to maintain the correct harsh texture. A stripping knife helps maintain the rough topcoat between full strips. Pay attention to the beard, eyebrows, and longer furnishings.
Irish Wolfhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a texturizing shampoo for wire coats. Their sheer size makes bathing a two-person job -- use a walk-in shower or professional tub. Avoid conditioners that soften the wiry coat. Towel dry and air-dry to preserve the rough, natural texture.
commonIssues: Beard matting and odor from food and water
Sheer size making home grooming extremely challenging
Joint issues (especially elbows and hips) requiring gentle handling and soft surfaces during grooming
Bloat risk requiring rest before and after grooming
Tail and ear tip injuries during the grooming process
Irish Wolfhound.extendResults: Clean the beard after every meal -- use a snood during feeding to keep it cleaner. Pull loose dead coat by hand between professional strip sessions. Keep the nails short since their giant frame puts significant pressure on the toes. Provide a non-slip surface for grooming since slippery floors stress their joints.
Irish Wolfhound.seasonalCare: Irish Wolfhounds shed moderately in spring and fall. Their rough coat provides good insulation in both heat and cold, so never shave them. In Houston summers, their giant size makes them heat-sensitive -- groom in air-conditioned spaces and ensure water access. Monitor for hot spots in humid weather.
Irish Wolfhound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard cleaning, hand-strip 2-3 times yearly, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 2-3 weeks (critical for joint health), professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Borzoi.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and long-toothed metal comb to work through the silky, medium-length coat. Focus on the feathering on the chest, legs, and tail where mats form most readily. A slicker brush helps with the denser areas behind the ears and in the pantaloons.
Borzoi.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and lightweight conditioner. The Borzoi's silky coat should feel smooth and flowing, not heavy. Use a force dryer on a warm setting to straighten and separate the coat while drying -- air-drying causes wave and potential matting.
commonIssues: Feathering matting on chest, legs, and hindquarters
Sensitivity to anesthesia affecting any sedated grooming procedures
Thin skin that tears easily during dematting
Bloat risk making post-groom rest important
Long tail tip injuries from enthusiastic wagging
Borzoi.extendResults: Mist feathering with detangling spray and comb through 2-3 times weekly to prevent mats from developing. Keep sanitary areas trimmed for cleanliness. Protect the long tail from door injuries. Apply a silk-protein leave-in spray after bathing to maintain the coat's signature flowing quality.
Borzoi.seasonalCare: Borzoi shed their undercoat heavily in spring and moderately in fall. During spring shedding, daily brushing is needed to remove the clumps of loose undercoat. In Houston summers, the silky coat provides some sun protection but limit outdoor time in extreme heat. Never shave a Borzoi -- the coat regulates temperature.
Borzoi.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly (daily during shedding), sanitary trims as needed, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, teeth brushing 3 times weekly, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks.

Saluki.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a soft pin brush. The smooth body coat requires minimal attention, but the feathered variety needs regular combing of the ear, tail, and leg feathering with a wide-toothed comb. Smooth-coated Salukis need only a grooming mitt weekly. Handle gently -- they are sensitive dogs.
Saluki.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. Their natural coat has very little doggy odor, so frequent bathing is unnecessary. For feathered varieties, use a light conditioner on the feathering only. Dry the feathering carefully to prevent tangling.
commonIssues: Feathering tangles in the eared, legged, and tailed variety
Thin, sensitive skin prone to scrapes and pressure sores
Anesthesia sensitivity requiring careful veterinary handling
Extremely lean body makes them feel cold easily
Anxiety-related coat issues -- Salukis are emotionally sensitive
Saluki.extendResults: For feathered Salukis, apply a light detangling spray to feathering after outdoor exercise. Provide soft bedding to prevent calluses on their thin-skinned, bony joints. A chamois cloth rubdown on the smooth body coat adds a beautiful sheen. Keep grooming sessions calm and quiet -- this breed stresses easily.
Saluki.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding year-round -- one of the cleanest, lowest-odor breeds. Houston heat is well-tolerated due to their desert heritage, but provide shade during peak sun hours. Winter requires a coat or sweater -- their thin build and minimal body fat offer no insulation.
Saluki.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly, feathering comb-out for feathered variety, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning biweekly. Professional groom every 8-10 weeks -- mainly for nail and ear maintenance.

Basenji.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a soft bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt. Their short, fine coat is exceptionally low-maintenance -- Basenjis famously groom themselves like cats. A quick rubdown with a grooming cloth between brushings keeps the coat gleaming. Shedding is minimal.
Basenji.bathingTips: Bathe only every 8-12 weeks -- or even less frequently. Basenjis are one of the cleanest dog breeds with almost no doggy odor. Their self-grooming habits keep them remarkably fresh. When bathing, use a gentle, fragrance-free shampoo and dry quickly -- they dislike water.
commonIssues: Fanconi syndrome affects overall health and coat condition
Skin sensitivity and allergies
Strong dislike of water making bathing challenging
Dry skin in winter months
Hypothyroidism causing coat changes in some lines
Basenji.extendResults: A weekly rubdown with a damp chamois cloth is often all that is needed between baths. Their natural cleanliness is remarkable -- encourage their self-grooming instincts. Apply a light moisturizer in dry weather to prevent skin flaking. Use a waterless shampoo if full baths are too stressful.
Basenji.seasonalCare: Very light shedding with a slight increase in spring. Houston heat is well-tolerated -- they are an African breed adapted to warm climates. Their short coat provides minimal insulation in winter, so provide a coat or sweater in cold weather. Sun exposure is generally fine with their pigmented skin.
Basenji.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly grooming mitt rubdown, bath only when necessary (every 8-12 weeks), nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning biweekly, dental care daily. Professional groom every 10-12 weeks -- one of the lowest-maintenance breeds.

Pharaoh Hound.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt. Their short, glossy coat is extremely low-maintenance and lies close to the body. A soft chamois cloth after brushing adds an impressive natural sheen. Shedding is minimal -- a quick weekly session is truly sufficient.
Pharaoh Hound.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo. Their coat is naturally clean and odor-free, requiring very infrequent bathing. Warm water is essential -- they are thin-skinned and feel the cold. Dry thoroughly and quickly.
commonIssues: Thin, sensitive skin that cuts and scrapes easily
Anesthesia sensitivity requiring specialized veterinary care
Sunburn on the nose and ear tips (their signature blushing shows skin sensitivity)
Cold intolerance due to minimal body fat and thin coat
Calluses on pressure points from their lean build
Pharaoh Hound.extendResults: Apply pet-safe sunscreen on the nose and ear tips before extended sun exposure. Their unique ability to blush means the skin is reactive -- watch for irritation signs during grooming. A chamois cloth rubdown every few days maintains their stunning natural sheen. Provide soft bedding to prevent calluses.
Pharaoh Hound.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding year-round. Houston summers are well-tolerated due to their Mediterranean heritage, but protect the nose from sunburn. Winter requires a well-fitted coat or jacket for outdoor walks. Their thin skin and minimal body fat make cold weather genuinely uncomfortable.
Pharaoh Hound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly grooming mitt rubdown, chamois cloth as desired, bath every 8-12 weeks, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning biweekly, sunscreen application for outdoor activities. Professional groom every 10-12 weeks.

Labradoodle.brushingRoutine: Brush daily to every other day with a slicker brush and metal comb, regardless of coat type (wool, fleece, or hair). Wool and fleece coats mat severely and quickly without consistent brushing. Part the coat and brush in sections from the skin outward. Use a dematting comb for any tangles caught early.
Labradoodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and conditioner appropriate for curly or wavy coats. A curl-defining conditioner helps maintain coat structure. Blow-dry completely using a slicker brush to stretch curls and check for hidden mats. Never air-dry a Labradoodle -- mats tighten as the coat dries naturally.
commonIssues: Severe matting throughout the coat if brushing is missed
Ear infections from heavy ear hair blocking the canal
Coat type varies widely -- even within a litter
Tear staining, especially in lighter-colored dogs
Skin allergies and hot spots, particularly in Houston's humidity
Labradoodle.extendResults: Commit to a consistent brushing schedule -- Labradoodle matting is the number one reason for coat shave-downs. Use a leave-in conditioner spray daily to maintain coat slip. Check behind the ears, in armpits, and between the hind legs daily -- these areas mat first. A humectant spray helps in dry weather.
Labradoodle.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding in wool and fleece coats, moderate in hair coats. Houston humidity can cause the coat to frizz and mat faster -- use an anti-humidity finishing spray. No significant seasonal blowout but the coat grows continuously, requiring regular trimming. Summer may warrant a shorter clip for comfort.
Labradoodle.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily to every other day, full comb-through 3 times weekly, ear cleaning and ear hair removal weekly, eye area cleaning daily for lighter colors, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks with full haircut.

Cockapoo.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a slicker brush and metal comb. Their coat ranges from loose waves to tight curls depending on the genetic mix, but all types mat without consistent grooming. Work through the coat in sections from the skin outward. A detangling spray before brushing makes the process smoother and reduces breakage.
Cockapoo.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a curl-enhancing shampoo and moisturizing conditioner. Their coat holds water and takes time to dry -- use a high-velocity or cage dryer for thorough drying. A deep conditioning treatment monthly helps prevent the dry, brittle coat that leads to matting.
commonIssues: Matting, especially around ears, armpits, collar area, and between hind legs
Chronic ear infections from heavy, curly ear hair
Tear staining visible on lighter coat colors
Coat texture changes significantly from puppy to adult
Skin allergies inherited from both parent breeds
Cockapoo.extendResults: Remove the collar or harness daily and brush the area underneath -- collar mats are extremely common in Cockapoos. Clean ears weekly and have ear hair removed during professional grooms. Apply a leave-in conditioner spray daily to high-friction areas. Keep facial hair trimmed enough to see the eyes clearly.
Cockapoo.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding in curlier coats, light shedding in wavier coats. Houston humidity causes curlier coats to tighten and mat faster. Use an anti-humidity spray in summer. The coat grows continuously year-round, requiring regular trimming regardless of season. Shorter clips in summer improve comfort.
Cockapoo.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily, ear cleaning weekly with ear hair removal, eye area cleaning daily, full comb-through every other day, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom with haircut every 6-8 weeks.

Bernedoodle.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a slicker brush, pin brush, and metal comb in rotation. Their thick, wavy to curly coat inherited from both the Bernese and Poodle parent is dense and prone to matting. Line-brush in sections, starting at the legs and working up. The areas behind the ears, chest, and the back of the thighs mat fastest.
Bernedoodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a rich moisturizing shampoo and heavy conditioner to maintain their thick, luxurious coat. Their dense coat holds a significant amount of water -- allow extra drying time with a high-velocity dryer. Ensure complete drying to the skin to prevent hot spots in the dense undercoat layers.
commonIssues: Dense coat mats severely and can require full shave-downs if neglected
Size variation (standard, mini, tiny) means grooming needs vary in scale
Hot spots in Houston's humidity from moisture trapped in dense coat
Ear infections from thick ear hair
Tri-color coats may show different textures in different color areas
Bernedoodle.extendResults: Establish a non-negotiable daily brushing routine from puppyhood -- Bernedoodles that are not trained to accept grooming early become extremely difficult as large adults. Use a leave-in conditioner spray before every brushing session. Focus 60% of brushing time on the mat-prone areas: behind ears, armpits, groin, and hind legs.
Bernedoodle.seasonalCare: Low shedding in curlier coats, moderate in wavier coats. Houston summers benefit from a shorter clip -- many owners prefer a 1-2 inch all-over cut for summer and grow the coat longer in winter. Their thick coat provides good insulation but traps humidity. Year-round coat growth requires regular trimming.
Bernedoodle.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily without exception, full comb-through every other day, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom with haircut every 6-8 weeks. Puppy grooming visits should start at 10-12 weeks for acclimation.

Aussiedoodle.brushingRoutine: Brush daily or every other day with a slicker brush and metal comb. Their coat ranges from wavy to curly depending on the generation, and both types require consistent maintenance. Use a pin brush for wavier coats and a slicker for curlier coats. Pay special attention to behind the ears, the chest, and the back of the hind legs.
Aussiedoodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and conditioner suited for curly or wavy coats. Their energetic, outdoorsy nature means they get dirty frequently -- waterless shampoo between baths is a lifesaver. Blow-dry completely, brushing through the coat during drying to prevent mat formation.
commonIssues: Matting in high-friction areas from their active lifestyle
Coat type varies dramatically based on generation and genetics
Ear infections from heavy ear hair
Merle coat patterns may have associated skin sensitivity
Herding instinct means they may resist standing still during grooming
Aussiedoodle.extendResults: Brush after every outdoor adventure to remove debris before it becomes embedded and causes mats. Apply a detangling spray before brushing. For merle-patterned dogs, monitor any skin changes in lighter pigmented areas. Start grooming training early -- their intelligence makes them quick learners when it is positive.
Aussiedoodle.seasonalCare: Low to minimal shedding in curlier coats, moderate in wavier or straighter coats. Houston heat warrants a shorter clip in summer -- their active nature makes heat management important. Coat grows continuously year-round. Spring is a good time for a fresh, shorter cut before summer heat arrives.
Aussiedoodle.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily or every other day, full comb-through 3 times weekly, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom with haircut every 6-8 weeks.

Cavapoo.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a slicker brush and fine-toothed comb. Their soft, wavy to curly coat is one of the most mat-prone of all doodle crosses due to the extremely fine hair texture from the Cavalier parent. Work gently but thoroughly, starting from the ends and working to the roots. A detangling spray is essential.
Cavapoo.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo and rich conditioner. Their fine coat benefits from a deep conditioning treatment to prevent the brittleness that leads to matting. Blow-dry completely on a low heat setting -- their fine coat is more susceptible to heat damage than coarser doodle coats.
commonIssues: Extremely fine coat mats quickly and severely
Tear staining is very common in this cross
Ear infections from heavy, furred ears
Mitral valve disease from the Cavalier parent affects overall health
Skin sensitivity and food allergies common in both parent breeds
Cavapoo.extendResults: Clean the eye area twice daily to manage tear staining -- this is the most visible grooming challenge in Cavapoos. Use a leave-in conditioner spray daily, focusing on behind the ears and the chest. Keep the facial hair trimmed to a manageable length. Consider a shorter overall trim if daily brushing is not realistic.
Cavapoo.seasonalCare: Low shedding in curlier coats, light shedding in wavier coats. Houston humidity makes their fine coat frizz and tangle faster. An anti-humidity finishing spray helps in summer. No seasonal blowout but continuous growth means regular trimming year-round. Shorter clips are recommended for Houston summers.
Cavapoo.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily, eye area cleaning twice daily, full comb-through every other day, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 2-3 weeks. Professional groom with haircut every 5-6 weeks -- they need more frequent trims than most doodle crosses.

Maltipoo.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and fine-toothed comb. Their soft, fluffy coat ranges from slightly wavy to curly and mats very easily due to the cotton-candy-like texture. Use a light detangling spray before every session. Brush gently in sections -- their small size means the coat is fine and breaks easily with aggressive brushing.
Maltipoo.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a gentle, whitening or brightening shampoo for lighter colors, or a moisturizing formula for darker coats. Use a lightweight conditioner that does not weigh down their fluffy coat. Blow-dry on low heat, using a slicker brush to fluff the coat. Their small size makes home bathing manageable.
commonIssues: Severe tear staining, especially in white and light-colored dogs
Fine coat mats rapidly, particularly around ears and legs
Dental disease is extremely common and affects coat health
Patellar luxation common in this cross
Skin allergies and sensitivities from both parent breeds
Maltipoo.extendResults: Tear stain management is often the biggest daily grooming task -- clean under eyes twice daily with a tear stain remover pad. Apply cornstarch under the eyes to absorb moisture and prevent staining. Keep the facial hair trimmed to a functional length. A topknot or bow keeps hair out of the eyes between grooms.
Maltipoo.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding year-round due to the Poodle influence. Houston humidity can make their fluffy coat droop and mat faster. Use a volumizing finishing spray after grooming. No significant seasonal coat changes. Their small size and light coat make them comfortable in most Houston weather with appropriate clothing in cooler months.
Maltipoo.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily, eye area cleaning twice daily, full comb-through every other day, dental care daily, nail trim every 2-3 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom with haircut every 4-6 weeks.

Yorkipoo.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and fine-toothed comb. Their coat can range from the silky straight Yorkie texture to the curly Poodle texture, with most falling somewhere in between as a soft wave. Use a light detangling spray before every session. Focus on the chest, behind the ears, and the leg furnishings.
Yorkipoo.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a silk-protein shampoo and lightweight conditioner. For silkier coats, a smoothing conditioner works best; for curlier coats, use a curl-defining formula. Their small size makes bathing easy but be thorough -- small dogs are often under-rinsed, leaving residue that causes irritation.
commonIssues: Fine coat tangles and mats easily regardless of texture type
Tear staining common in lighter-colored individuals
Dental disease from both parent breeds requiring vigilant oral care
Patellar luxation is common
Tracheal collapse risk means avoiding pressure on the neck during grooming
Yorkipoo.extendResults: Always use a harness rather than a collar to protect the trachea -- collar pulling contributes to tracheal issues. Clean eyes daily for tear stain prevention. Trim facial hair to keep vision clear and reduce tear contact with fur. A light finishing spray appropriate for the coat type adds shine and manageability.
Yorkipoo.seasonalCare: Minimal shedding year-round. Houston weather is generally manageable for Yorkipoos -- their small size means they adapt to indoor temperatures easily. Provide a sweater for cooler days. No significant seasonal coat changes. Continuous growth requires regular trimming.
Yorkipoo.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily, eye area cleaning daily, full comb-through every other day, dental care daily, nail trim every 2-3 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional groom with haircut every 4-6 weeks.

Schnoodle.brushingRoutine: Brush every other day with a slicker brush and metal comb. Their coat is typically wiry to wavy, inheriting the Schnauzer texture more than the Poodle curl in many cases. Use a stripping comb if the coat leans wiry, or a slicker for curlier coats. Focus on the beard, eyebrows, and leg furnishings which tangle and collect food debris.
Schnoodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a texturizing shampoo for wiry coats or a curl-enhancing formula for curlier coats. The Schnauzer influence means the beard and eyebrows may need a more frequent wash than the body. A light conditioner on furnishings only -- avoid softening the wiry body coat if present.
commonIssues: Beard staining and odor from food and water trapped in facial hair
Coat type varies widely depending on generation and genetics
Ear infections from ear hair
Skin allergies common from both parent breeds
Eyebrow hair growing into eyes if not trimmed
