Schnoodle.extendResults: Clean the beard after every meal and water session to prevent staining and bacterial buildup. Comb the eyebrows upward and trim to keep vision clear. Use a dry beard wash between full baths. For wiry coats, a stripping stone maintains texture between professional grooms.
Schnoodle.seasonalCare: Low shedding year-round in most coat types. Houston humidity can cause wiry coats to soften and curlier coats to frizz. Use a texturizing spray for wiry coats or an anti-humidity spray for curly coats. Continuous coat growth requires regular trimming regardless of season.
Schnoodle.maintenanceSchedule: Brush every other day, beard cleaning after meals, eyebrow trim as needed, full comb-through 3 times weekly, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom with haircut every 6-8 weeks.

Sheepadoodle.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a large slicker brush, pin brush, and metal comb. Their thick, dense coat inherited from the Old English Sheepdog parent is one of the heaviest of all doodle crosses and mats severely without daily attention. Line-brush in sections starting from the feet upward. A long-toothed undercoat rake may be needed for thicker coats.
Sheepadoodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a rich moisturizing shampoo and heavy conditioner. Their thick coat absorbs enormous amounts of water and takes a very long time to dry -- plan for extended blow-drying sessions. Professional bathing is recommended for standard-sized Sheepadoodles due to the sheer volume of coat.
commonIssues: Extremely dense coat that mats rapidly and severely throughout
Their large size makes grooming physically demanding for owners
Hot spots in Houston's humidity from trapped moisture
Eye hair blocking vision if facial trim is not maintained
Drooling can cause chin and beard staining
Sheepadoodle.extendResults: This breed requires an absolute commitment to daily grooming -- full shave-downs are extremely common when brushing lapses. Focus extra time on behind the ears, armpits, belly, and hind legs. Use a leave-in conditioner spray generously before every brushing session. Consider a shorter all-over clip if daily brushing is not sustainable.
Sheepadoodle.seasonalCare: Low to moderate shedding depending on coat type, but the volume of coat makes any shedding significant. Houston summers strongly warrant a shorter clip -- 1 to 1.5 inches all over for comfort. Their thick coat is a serious heat risk in Houston humidity. Winter allows for a longer, fuller coat if the owner can maintain it.
Sheepadoodle.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily without exception, full comb-through every other day, eye area trim as needed, ear cleaning weekly with ear hair removal, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom with haircut every 6-8 weeks -- schedule extra time for this breed.

Pomsky.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. Their double coat combines the Pomeranian's dense fluff with the Husky's thick undercoat, creating impressive shedding volume for a smaller dog. Use a pin brush for the outer coat and an undercoat rake to reach the dense under layer.
Pomsky.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a deshedding shampoo and conditioner system. Their double coat requires thorough soaking and even more thorough rinsing -- residue trapped in the undercoat causes irritation. High-velocity blow-drying is essential to remove loose undercoat and ensure complete drying.
commonIssues: Heavy shedding that surprises owners expecting a small, low-maintenance dog
Double coat blowouts twice yearly are significant
Matting in the undercoat if brushing is inconsistent
Dry skin from over-bathing or Houston's seasonal changes
Coat texture varies widely depending on which parent breed dominates
Pomsky.extendResults: Use a high-velocity dryer during home grooming sessions to blow out loose undercoat -- this reduces shedding between professional grooms significantly. Apply a coat conditioning spray before brushing. A professional deshedding treatment every 6-8 weeks makes a dramatic difference in household shedding levels.
Pomsky.seasonalCare: Heavy coat blowouts in spring and fall -- daily brushing required for 2-3 weeks during these transitions. Houston heat requires coat maintenance for insulation but never shave the double coat. Summer grooming sessions should include thorough undercoat removal. Winter coat is at its densest and most impressive.
Pomsky.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during blowouts, undercoat rake session twice weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, ear cleaning weekly. Professional deshedding groom every 6-8 weeks.

German Shorthaired Pointer.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or bristle brush. Their short, dense, water-repellent coat is remarkably low-maintenance. A grooming mitt works well for quick daily rubdowns that remove loose hair and distribute natural oils. During shedding season, increase to 2-3 times weekly with a deshedding tool.
German Shorthaired Pointer.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, sport-dog formula shampoo. Their coat is naturally water-repellent, so they stay relatively clean even with an active outdoor lifestyle. A deodorizing shampoo helps after particularly muddy field days. Rinse thoroughly -- their short coat makes residue obvious.
commonIssues: Ear infections from floppy ears trapping moisture after swimming or field work
Skin allergies and hot spots, especially in humid climates
Seasonal shedding heavier than expected for a short-coated breed
Tail injuries during hunting or active play
Calluses on elbows from lying on hard surfaces
German Shorthaired Pointer.extendResults: Clean ears after every swim or field outing -- this breed is extremely prone to ear infections from moisture. A quick rubdown with a grooming mitt after outdoor adventures removes loose hair and debris. Apply a light moisturizing spray weekly to maintain coat health and natural sheen.
German Shorthaired Pointer.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding in spring and fall. Houston heat is well-tolerated by these athletic dogs, but provide ample water and shade during exercise. Their short, dense coat provides good sun protection. Winter coat thickens slightly, with a denser undercoat developing.
German Shorthaired Pointer.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing, ear cleaning after water exposure and weekly otherwise, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, paw pad check after field work. Professional groom every 8-10 weeks.

English Pointer.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or hound glove. Their short, smooth, dense coat is one of the easiest to maintain among sporting breeds. A chamois cloth after brushing creates a polished, gleaming finish. Minimal effort yields maximum results with this coat type.
English Pointer.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle shampoo for short coats. Their smooth coat dries quickly and stays relatively clean between baths. A quick wipe with a damp cloth after field work is often sufficient. Use an oatmeal-based shampoo if dry skin is a concern.
commonIssues: Seasonal shedding with fine, short hairs that embed in upholstery
Ear infections from pendant-shaped ears
Thin skin on the tail prone to splitting from enthusiastic wagging
Calluses on elbows and sternum from their lean build
Skin allergies in some lines
English Pointer.extendResults: A daily grooming mitt rubdown takes under two minutes and significantly reduces loose hair in the home. Keep ears clean and dry, especially after outdoor exercise. Apply vitamin E oil to any calluses developing on pressure points. Their tail tip can split from hitting objects -- keep the environment clear.
English Pointer.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding in spring and fall. Houston heat suits their field heritage well, but provide water breaks during exercise. Their short coat offers good protection. Winter coat is slightly denser. This breed is generally comfortable in Houston's climate year-round.
English Pointer.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly grooming mitt rubdown, chamois wipe as desired, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, callus check at each session. Professional groom every 8-10 weeks.

Gordon Setter.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and medium-toothed comb. Their long, silky black and tan coat with feathering on the ears, chest, belly, legs, and tail requires focused attention on feathered areas. Use a slicker brush for minor tangles and a detangling spray for stubborn areas. The ear feathering mats fastest.
Gordon Setter.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a color-enhancing shampoo for dark coats and a silk conditioner to maintain the glossy sheen. Their black coat shows dandruff and product buildup easily, so rinse extremely thoroughly. A finishing rinse with cool water helps seal the cuticle and enhance shine.
commonIssues: Matting in ear, chest, and leg feathering
Ear infections from long, heavy, feathered ears
Black coat shows flaking and dandruff readily
Seasonal shedding more significant than other setters
Hip and elbow dysplasia affecting comfort during grooming sessions
Gordon Setter.extendResults: Comb through all feathering every other day to catch tangles before they mat. Apply a silk-finish spray to the feathering after brushing for a polished look. Clean inside the ears weekly -- their heavy, feathered ears are a perfect environment for infections. A leave-in conditioner on feathered areas prevents dryness and breakage.
Gordon Setter.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round with heavier periods in spring and fall. Houston heat can be uncomfortable for this dark-coated breed -- schedule outdoor activities for early morning and evening. Keep feathering trimmed shorter in summer for comfort. Winter coat is fuller with denser feathering.
Gordon Setter.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, feathering comb-through every other day, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, sanitary trim as needed. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks.

Flat-Coated Retriever.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and slicker brush. Their moderately long, flat-lying coat with feathering on the ears, chest, legs, and tail is less prone to matting than curly or wavy coats. Focus on the feathered areas where tangles develop. A medium-toothed comb checks for missed tangles after brushing.
Flat-Coated Retriever.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and light conditioner. Their coat has a natural water-resistant quality that should be preserved -- avoid stripping shampoos. A color-enhancing formula works well for their solid black or liver coats. Blow-dry on medium heat following the lay of the coat.
commonIssues: Feathering tangles after swimming or field work
Ear infections from water-loving activities
Seasonal shedding with feathering collecting debris
Their perpetual puppy energy makes them wiggly during grooming
Cancer prevalence in the breed can affect coat and skin health
Flat-Coated Retriever.extendResults: Rinse and brush feathering after every swim to prevent tangles and remove chlorine or natural water minerals. Apply a leave-in conditioner to feathered areas after water activities. Trim the feet and hock feathering to reduce debris pickup. Their happy temperament makes grooming enjoyable if you can channel the energy.
Flat-Coated Retriever.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round with increase in spring and fall. Houston heat is manageable for this water-loving breed -- provide swimming opportunities for exercise and cooling. Trim feathering slightly shorter in summer. Winter coat is fuller with more substantial feathering.
Flat-Coated Retriever.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, post-swim rinse and brush, ear cleaning weekly and after water activities, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks with feathering trim.

Chesapeake Bay Retriever.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry brush or bristle brush. Their unique double coat has a harsh, wavy outer coat over a dense, woolly, oily undercoat that provides exceptional waterproofing. Avoid over-brushing which strips the natural oils. An undercoat rake during shedding season helps manage the dense underlayer.
Chesapeake Bay Retriever.bathingTips: Bathe sparingly -- every 8-12 weeks -- to preserve the coat's essential natural oils and waterproofing. Use a gentle, non-stripping shampoo and avoid conditioners that can disrupt the coat's natural oily texture. Their coat is designed to repel water and resist dirt. Over-bathing is the most common grooming mistake with this breed.
commonIssues: Strong oily coat odor that owners must accept as normal and functional
Dense undercoat sheds heavily during seasonal transitions
Skin issues if the natural coat oils are stripped by over-bathing
Ear infections from frequent water work
Webbed feet require paw pad and nail attention
Chesapeake Bay Retriever.extendResults: Resist the urge to over-groom or over-bathe this breed -- their coat's oily quality is a feature, not a flaw. A weekly rubdown with a curry brush is truly sufficient for maintenance. Dry the ears thoroughly after every water activity. Check between the webbed toes for debris and cracking.
Chesapeake Bay Retriever.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding in spring and fall as the dense undercoat cycles. Daily undercoat raking during coat blowouts. Houston heat is manageable for this water breed -- they thrive with swimming opportunities. Never strip the natural coat oils thinking it will help with heat. Winter coat is impressively dense and water-resistant.
Chesapeake Bay Retriever.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly curry brush rubdown, daily during coat blowouts, ear cleaning after water exposure and weekly otherwise, paw pad check weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 8-12 weeks -- less is more with this breed.

Nova Scotia Duck Tolling Retriever.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and slicker brush. Their medium-length double coat with feathering is water-repellent and moderately easy to maintain. Focus on the ear feathering, chest ruff, and pantaloon feathering on the hind legs. An undercoat rake helps during shedding season.
Nova Scotia Duck Tolling Retriever.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, natural shampoo that preserves the coat's water-repellent properties. Their red-gold coat benefits from a color-enhancing formula. Use a light conditioner on feathered areas only -- the body coat should maintain its natural water resistance. Blow-dry completely to prevent matting in the undercoat.
commonIssues: Feathering tangles after water work and outdoor activities
Seasonal undercoat shedding can be substantial
Ear infections from frequent swimming
White markings show staining easily
Addison's disease and autoimmune conditions can affect coat health
Nova Scotia Duck Tolling Retriever.extendResults: Rinse the coat after every swim to remove minerals and debris from the feathering. Apply a leave-in conditioner to the ear feathering and pantaloons after outdoor activities. Trim foot feathering to prevent snowball formation in winter and debris pickup in summer. Their white chest and paw markings may benefit from brightening spray.
Nova Scotia Duck Tolling Retriever.seasonalCare: Moderate to heavy shedding in spring and fall -- daily brushing with an undercoat rake during these transitions. Houston heat is manageable with swimming opportunities for cooling. Trim feathering for summer comfort. Winter coat is fuller and denser with more pronounced undercoat and feathering.
Nova Scotia Duck Tolling Retriever.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, post-swim rinse, ear cleaning weekly and after water activities, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks with feathering maintenance.

Clumber Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a slicker brush and wide-toothed comb. Their dense, flat, silky coat with abundant feathering on the ears, chest, belly, and legs requires thorough, consistent attention. Focus on the heavy ear feathering and the profuse chest and belly hair where mats form. An undercoat rake helps manage the dense underlayer.
Clumber Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a whitening shampoo -- their predominantly white coat shows every stain. Use a heavy conditioner on the feathering to prevent tangles. Their substantial build means professional bathing is often easier than at home. Dry thoroughly, especially in the dense feathering areas.
commonIssues: Heavy drooling causing chin and chest staining on white coat
Ear infections from massive, heavy, low-set ears
Excessive shedding for a spaniel breed
Eye entropion and ectropion causing tear staining
Weight management issues affecting mobility during grooming
Clumber Spaniel.extendResults: Wipe the face and chin after every meal and water session to prevent drool staining on the white coat. Clean inside the massive ears twice weekly -- Clumber ears are among the most infection-prone of any breed. Apply a whitening maintenance spray to the coat between baths. Keep the belly and rear feathering trimmed for hygiene.
Clumber Spaniel.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding year-round with increased volume in spring and fall. Houston humidity amplifies their tendency for ear infections and skin fold issues. Keep feathering trimmed shorter in summer for comfort and hygiene. Winter coat is at its fullest.
Clumber Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily face wiping, ear cleaning twice weekly, eye area cleaning daily, feathering comb-through every other day, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 4-6 weeks.

Field Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and medium-toothed comb. Their moderately long, silky coat with feathering on the ears, chest, legs, and belly is less profuse than the Cocker but still requires consistent attention. Focus on behind the ears and the leg feathering where tangles develop.
Field Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and conditioner. Their silky coat benefits from a cream rinse that adds slip for easier post-bath detangling. Dark-colored Field Spaniels look best with a shine-enhancing formula. Blow-dry on medium heat, brushing the coat in sections as you dry.
commonIssues: Ear infections from moderately long, feathered ears
Feathering tangles after outdoor exercise
Hypothyroidism is common and affects coat quality
Seasonal shedding concentrated in the undercoat
Tear staining in lighter-colored individuals
Field Spaniel.extendResults: Comb through ear feathering and leg furnishings every other day to stay ahead of tangles. Keep the hair on the bottom of the feet trimmed for traction and cleanliness. A light leave-in conditioner on feathered areas extends the freshly groomed look. Clean ears weekly as a preventive measure.
Field Spaniel.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding in spring and fall. Houston heat is manageable for this moderately coated breed. Trim feathering slightly shorter in summer for comfort. Winter coat develops more undercoat. Their active outdoor nature means regular post-exercise grooming attention year-round.
Field Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, feathering comb-through every other day, ear cleaning weekly, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, foot hair trim as needed. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks.

Welsh Springer Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and slicker brush. Their flat, silky red and white coat with feathering on the ears, chest, belly, and legs requires focused attention on feathered areas. Use a fine-toothed comb through the ear feathering which mats fastest. A medium comb checks the body coat for tangles.
Welsh Springer Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle shampoo and silk conditioner. Their red and white coat benefits from a color-enhancing formula for red coats and a brightening rinse for the white areas. Rinse thoroughly -- residue is particularly visible on the white portions. Dry ears completely to prevent infections.
commonIssues: Matting in ear and leg feathering
Ear infections from long, vine-shaped, furred ears
Tear staining visible on white facial markings
Seasonal shedding, particularly from the undercoat
Skin allergies and sensitivities
Welsh Springer Spaniel.extendResults: Comb through ear feathering daily during heavy shedding periods and every other day normally. Clean under the eyes daily to prevent tear staining on white facial markings. Apply a leave-in conditioner to all feathered areas after brushing. Keep the feet trimmed neatly between the pads and around the toes for a tidy appearance.
Welsh Springer Spaniel.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding in spring and fall with undercoat release. Houston heat is manageable -- trim feathering shorter in summer for comfort during active outdoor play. Winter coat develops a denser undercoat. Year-round attention to ear health is important regardless of season.
Welsh Springer Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, ear feathering comb-out every other day, ear cleaning weekly, eye area cleaning daily for staining prevention, nail trim every 3-4 weeks. Professional groom every 5-7 weeks.

American Water Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and wide-toothed comb. Their distinctive tightly curled to wavy double coat is water-resistant and moderately prone to matting. Work through the curls gently without straightening them. An undercoat rake helps manage the dense underlayer during shedding season.
American Water Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, curl-enhancing shampoo that maintains the coat's natural wave pattern. Avoid heavy conditioners that weigh down the curls. Their oily, water-resistant coat has a natural odor that bathing manages but never fully eliminates. Scrunch-dry with a towel to maintain curl definition.
commonIssues: Natural oily coat odor that is characteristic of the breed
Matting in the dense undercoat if brushing is neglected
Ear infections from floppy, curly-haired ears
Coat pattern varies from marcel wave to tight curls
Seasonal shedding can be substantial from the undercoat
American Water Spaniel.extendResults: Scrunch rather than brush the curls when possible to maintain their natural pattern. Apply a leave-in curl-defining spray after baths. Clean ears weekly and after every water activity. Accept that some coat odor is normal and functional for this breed -- it is part of their water-resistant design.
American Water Spaniel.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding in spring and fall, primarily from the undercoat. Houston heat is well-tolerated by this water breed -- provide swimming for exercise and cooling. Their curly coat provides good insulation in both directions. Winter coat is denser with more pronounced undercoat. Humidity helps maintain curl definition naturally.
American Water Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, ear cleaning weekly and after water activities, nail trim every 3-4 weeks, paw pad check weekly. Professional groom every 6-8 weeks with curl-preserving technique.

Staffordshire Bull Terrier.brushingRoutine: Use a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt 2-3 times per week to remove loose hair and distribute natural oils across their short, smooth coat. A soft bristle brush works well for finishing and adding shine. Focus on the chest and hindquarters where dead hair tends to accumulate.
Staffordshire Bull Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo formulated for sensitive skin. Staffies are prone to skin allergies, so avoid heavily fragranced products. A colloidal oatmeal shampoo works well for soothing any irritation.
commonIssues: Skin allergies and contact dermatitis
Demodectic mange susceptibility
Seasonal shedding increases
Dry, flaky skin in winter
Interdigital cysts between toes
Staffordshire Bull Terrier.extendResults: Apply a light coat conditioning spray after grooming to maintain shine between baths. Wipe down with a damp microfiber cloth after outdoor play to remove allergens. Feed omega-3 fatty acid supplements to support coat health from the inside out.
Staffordshire Bull Terrier.seasonalCare: Staffies shed moderately year-round with increases in spring and fall. In summer, their short coat offers little sun protection on lighter-colored areas, so limit prolonged sun exposure. In winter, consider a light jacket for outdoor walks as they have minimal insulation.
Staffordshire Bull Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a rubber curry brush. Wipe coat with a damp cloth after walks. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Check skin folds around the face weekly. Clean ears biweekly and trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

American Pit Bull Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a rubber curry comb or natural bristle brush to manage their short, stiff coat. Work in circular motions with the curry comb to lift dead undercoat and stimulate blood flow to the skin. A chamois cloth gives a final polish.
American Pit Bull Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a mild, moisturizing shampoo. Pit Bulls can be prone to dry skin, so choose products with natural oils like coconut or aloe. Avoid over-bathing as it strips essential oils and can trigger skin issues.
commonIssues: Allergic dermatitis and hot spots
Grass and environmental allergies
Dry, cracked nose leather
Tear staining around eyes
Calluses on elbows from lying on hard surfaces
American Pit Bull Terrier.extendResults: Use a daily skin and coat supplement with omega fatty acids to maintain the natural sheen. Wipe the coat with unscented baby wipes or grooming wipes after outdoor activities to remove pollen and allergens. Apply nose balm weekly to prevent cracking.
American Pit Bull Terrier.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round with heavier periods in spring and fall. Summer heat can cause skin irritation, so keep them cool and watch for hot spots. In winter, their thin coat provides minimal protection, so use a fitted coat for extended outdoor time in cold weather.
American Pit Bull Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with rubber curry. Wipe down after outdoor play. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Apply nose balm weekly. Check ears weekly and clean as needed. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

American Staffordshire Terrier.brushingRoutine: Use a firm natural bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt 2-3 times weekly across their short, glossy coat. The rubber mitt is particularly effective at capturing loose hair during shedding periods. Brush in the direction of hair growth, paying extra attention to the neck and shoulder area.
American Staffordshire Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced dog shampoo. AmStaffs have sensitive skin, so hypoallergenic formulas without artificial dyes or fragrances work best. A finishing rinse with diluted apple cider vinegar can help maintain coat pH and add shine.
commonIssues: Atopic dermatitis and food allergies
Demodex mites
Seasonal flank alopecia
Skin fold irritation around muzzle
Zinc-responsive dermatosis
American Staffordshire Terrier.extendResults: A light misting of coat conditioning spray between baths keeps the coat glossy and reduces static. Regular wiping with a microfiber grooming cloth removes surface dirt and distributes oils. Ensure their bedding is washed frequently to prevent skin issues.
American Staffordshire Terrier.seasonalCare: Sheds moderately with seasonal increases in spring and fall. In Houston's summer heat, keep grooming consistent to remove dead hair that traps heat against the skin. Winter care should include moisturizing products as indoor heating dries out skin and coat.
American Staffordshire Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Wipe coat with damp cloth after walks. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean facial folds weekly. Check and clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Monitor skin for allergy flare-ups.

Bullmastiff.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a rubber curry brush followed by a soft bristle brush to maintain their short, dense coat. Pay special attention to the wrinkles and folds around the face, cleaning them with a damp cloth after brushing. A deshedding tool like a FURminator can help during heavy shedding periods.
Bullmastiff.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. Due to their size, a high-velocity dryer speeds up drying time significantly. Clean and dry all facial wrinkles thoroughly after bathing to prevent bacterial and yeast infections in the folds.
commonIssues: Skin fold dermatitis and yeast infections
Excessive drooling causing chin acne
Elbow calluses and hygromas
Seasonal allergies
Interdigital cysts
Bullmastiff.extendResults: Clean facial wrinkles daily with medicated wipes or a damp cloth followed by a light dusting of cornstarch to keep folds dry. Apply elbow balm regularly to prevent calluses. Keep a drool rag handy and wipe the chin area frequently to prevent acne.
Bullmastiff.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round with heavier blowing in spring and fall. Summer heat is dangerous for this breed, so keep them cool and groomed to reduce heat retention. In winter, their short coat needs supplemental warmth during extended outdoor exposure.
Bullmastiff.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Clean facial wrinkles daily. Wipe drool and chin area multiple times daily. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Apply elbow balm 2-3 times weekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Mastiff (English).brushingRoutine: Brush 3 times per week with a rubber curry brush and follow with a bristle brush to manage their short, double coat. During shedding season, daily brushing with a deshedding tool is necessary to control the significant volume of loose hair. Focus on the neck ruff, hindquarters, and tail where hair tends to collect.
Mastiff (English).bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a large-breed formulated shampoo with moisturizing properties. Due to their massive size, a walk-in shower or professional grooming facility with a raised tub is ideal. Thoroughly dry all wrinkles and skin folds to prevent moisture-related infections.
commonIssues: Deep skin fold infections
Chronic drool staining on chest and chin
Elbow and hock calluses
Hot spots in humid weather
Yeast overgrowth in wrinkles
Mastiff (English).extendResults: Daily wrinkle cleaning with veterinary-grade wipes is essential for this breed. Use a waterless shampoo spray between baths to freshen the coat and manage drool stains. Provide padded bedding to prevent callus formation on pressure points.
Mastiff (English).seasonalCare: Heavy seasonal shedding in spring and fall requires daily brushing. English Mastiffs are extremely heat-sensitive, so summer grooming should focus on removing dead undercoat to improve air circulation. Winter care includes monitoring for dry, cracked skin on the nose and paw pads.
Mastiff (English).maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Clean all wrinkles and folds daily. Wipe drool areas several times daily. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Treat calluses with balm 3 times weekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Neapolitan Mastiff.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt to manage their short, dense coat. The curry brush works best in circular motions to capture loose hair and stimulate the skin beneath their heavy folds. A soft bristle brush provides a finishing pass for shine.
Neapolitan Mastiff.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a medicated or antimicrobial shampoo, as this breed is prone to skin infections in their extensive wrinkles. Every single fold and wrinkle must be washed, rinsed, and completely dried. Use a high-velocity dryer to ensure no moisture remains trapped in folds.
commonIssues: Chronic skin fold pyoderma
Severe drool-related dermatitis
Cherry eye and eye fold irritation
Interdigital furunculosis
Demodex mange susceptibility
Neapolitan Mastiff.extendResults: Daily wrinkle maintenance is non-negotiable for this breed. Use medicated wipes in all folds, then apply a thin layer of antimicrobial powder to keep folds dry. Keep a dedicated drool towel available and wipe the face, jowls, and chest regularly throughout the day.
Neapolitan Mastiff.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round with slight seasonal increases. This breed is extremely heat-intolerant, so summer grooming should prioritize dead coat removal and wrinkle hygiene. In humid climates like Houston, increase wrinkle cleaning frequency to prevent yeast and bacterial overgrowth.
Neapolitan Mastiff.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Clean ALL wrinkles and folds at least twice daily. Wipe drool continuously throughout the day. Bathe every 4-6 weeks with antimicrobial shampoo. Monitor eyes daily for irritation. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Tibetan Mastiff.brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week with a pin brush and undercoat rake to manage their massive double coat. Work through the dense mane around the neck and the heavy feathering on the legs and tail with a long-toothed metal comb to prevent mats. During the annual coat blow, daily brushing with an undercoat rake is essential.
Tibetan Mastiff.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-10 weeks using a high-quality moisturizing shampoo and follow with a heavy conditioner to maintain coat texture. Their dense coat requires extended soaking time to penetrate to the skin. Use a high-velocity dryer extensively, as this coat can take hours to air dry and damp undercoat leads to hot spots.
commonIssues: Severe matting in the mane and behind ears
Hot spots from trapped moisture in undercoat
Hypothyroidism-related coat changes
Seasonal flank alopecia
Allergic dermatitis
Tibetan Mastiff.extendResults: Use a leave-in conditioning spray when brushing to reduce breakage and make detangling easier. Focus daily attention on the areas behind the ears, under the collar, and in the groin where mats form first. Keep the coat clean and dry to prevent the musty odor that develops in damp undercoat.
Tibetan Mastiff.seasonalCare: Tibetan Mastiffs blow their entire undercoat once per year, typically in late spring, producing enormous amounts of loose fur over 2-4 weeks. Daily raking sessions are required during this period. In summer, never shave this breed as the double coat provides insulation against heat. Winter is when their coat is at its fullest and most manageable.
Tibetan Mastiff.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly with pin brush and undercoat rake, daily during coat blow. Comb through mane and feathering with metal comb 2-3 times weekly. Bathe every 6-10 weeks. Check behind ears and under collar daily for mat formation. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Dogue de Bordeaux.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt across their short, fine coat. The curry brush effectively removes dead hair while massaging the skin beneath their loose folds. A soft chamois cloth or microfiber towel gives a polished finish.
Dogue de Bordeaux.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo. This breed's extensive facial wrinkles require meticulous cleaning and rinsing during every bath. Dry all folds completely with a towel and low-heat dryer to prevent bacterial growth in trapped moisture.
commonIssues: Deep facial fold infections
Chronic drool dermatitis on chin and chest
Demodectic mange
Ear infections due to heavy ear leather
Elbow hygromas
Dogue de Bordeaux.extendResults: Clean facial folds daily with antimicrobial wipes and apply a light, breathable barrier cream to prevent moisture buildup. Wipe drool from the chin and chest several times daily. Use a waterless shampoo spray on the chest area between baths to manage drool staining.
Dogue de Bordeaux.seasonalCare: Light to moderate shedding year-round. Extremely heat-sensitive, so summer grooming focuses on coat maintenance for cooling efficiency and vigilant wrinkle hygiene in humid conditions. Winter care includes nose and paw pad moisturizing to prevent cracking in dry air.
Dogue de Bordeaux.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Clean facial wrinkles twice daily minimum. Wipe drool continuously. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Check and clean ears twice weekly due to heavy ear leather. Apply elbow balm regularly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Leonberger.brushingRoutine: Brush daily or at minimum 4-5 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to manage their thick, medium-long double coat. Use a long-toothed metal comb through the mane, chest feathering, and leg furnishings where mats form quickly. Pay particular attention to behind the ears, under the armpits, and around the collar area.
Leonberger.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo formulated for double-coated breeds, followed by a detangling conditioner. Their dense coat requires thorough saturation and extended rinsing to remove all product. A high-velocity dryer is essential as this coat can retain moisture for extended periods.
commonIssues: Matting in mane, armpits, and behind ears
Hot spots from moisture trapped in undercoat
Excessive shedding creating household management challenges
Ear infections from heavy, pendulous ears
Webbed feet collecting debris between toes
Leonberger.extendResults: Use a detangling spray daily when brushing to reduce coat breakage and ease mat removal. Keep the hair between the paw pads trimmed to prevent debris collection and slipping. Trim sanitary areas regularly to maintain hygiene. A weekly deep-combing session prevents small tangles from becoming serious mats.
Leonberger.seasonalCare: Heavy seasonal shedding twice a year in spring and fall, with females often blowing coat after heat cycles as well. Daily brushing with an undercoat rake is mandatory during these periods. In summer, ensure the undercoat is thinned to prevent overheating. Never shave a Leonberger as their double coat regulates temperature.
Leonberger.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with slicker brush and undercoat rake. Deep comb through feathering and mane 2-3 times weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Trim paw pad hair and sanitary areas biweekly. Clean ears twice weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Greater Swiss Mountain Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a rubber curry brush and bristle brush combination to maintain their short, dense double coat. During shedding season, add an undercoat rake to the routine and brush daily. The topcoat is coarse and the undercoat is thick, so the curry brush works well to pull out dead undercoat.
Greater Swiss Mountain Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, all-purpose dog shampoo. Their double coat repels dirt naturally, so they stay cleaner than many breeds. Ensure thorough rinsing as residual shampoo trapped in the dense undercoat can cause irritation and flaking.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding creating large fur volume
Hot spots in summer humidity
Dry skin in winter from indoor heating
Lick granulomas on front legs
Splayed feet requiring nail maintenance
Greater Swiss Mountain Dog.extendResults: Regular use of a rubber curry brush between baths stimulates natural oil production and maintains coat sheen. A light coat conditioning spray helps manage static and keeps the topcoat smooth. Wipe down with a damp cloth after outdoor activities to remove allergens and debris.
Greater Swiss Mountain Dog.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding twice yearly in spring and fall with significant undercoat release. Daily brushing with an undercoat rake is required during these 2-3 week periods. Summer care involves ensuring the dead undercoat is removed to prevent heat stress. Winter coat is at its densest and most impressive.
Greater Swiss Mountain Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Check between toes for debris after walks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks as this breed's nails grow quickly.

Anatolian Shepherd.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to maintain their medium-length double coat. The outer coat is coarse and slightly wavy while the undercoat is dense and woolly. During the biannual coat blow, switch to daily sessions with a deshedding tool and rake to manage the massive volume of loose undercoat.
Anatolian Shepherd.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks as their coat has natural dirt-repelling properties. Use a mild, all-purpose shampoo and avoid over-bathing which strips the protective oils in their weatherproof coat. Their coat is designed for outdoor work and maintains itself well with regular brushing.
commonIssues: Massive seasonal coat blow creating extreme shedding
Matting in the neck ruff and behind ears
Dry skin from over-bathing
Burrs and debris collection in the feathering
Entropion causing eye irritation and tearing
Anatolian Shepherd.extendResults: This breed's coat is relatively self-maintaining between grooms. Focus on keeping the neck ruff and rear feathering tangle-free with a metal comb. Remove burrs and plant material promptly before they mat into the coat. A light detangling spray helps when working through the thicker areas.
Anatolian Shepherd.seasonalCare: Anatolian Shepherds blow their heavy undercoat dramatically in late spring, shedding enough fur to fill garbage bags over 2-3 weeks. Daily raking is essential during this period. Summer coat is noticeably thinner and lighter. Winter coat grows in dense and woolly, requiring less maintenance once fully established.
Anatolian Shepherd.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with slicker brush and rake, daily during spring coat blow. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Comb through neck ruff and feathering twice weekly. Check for burrs after outdoor time. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Bouvier des Flandres.brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush, pin brush, and metal comb to maintain their rough, tousled double coat. The outer coat is harsh and wiry while the undercoat is dense and fine. Work systematically from skin outward using line brushing technique to prevent hidden mats close to the skin.
Bouvier des Flandres.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a texturizing shampoo that maintains the coat's natural rough texture. Avoid heavy conditioners that soften the coat too much. A thorough blow-dry with a high-velocity dryer is mandatory as their dense coat retains water and can develop hot spots or mildew if left damp.
commonIssues: Matting in the beard, legs, and underbelly
Beard becoming dirty and smelly from food and water
Eye irritation from hair growing into eyes
Ear infections due to hair in ear canals
Hot spots from inadequate drying
Bouvier des Flandres.extendResults: Clean the beard daily after meals and water with a damp cloth or grooming wipe to prevent odor and staining. Keep hair trimmed away from the eyes for comfort and vision. Strip or trim ear canal hair regularly to improve air circulation. A waterproof beard bib during meals helps reduce staining.
Bouvier des Flandres.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding year-round as dead hair gets trapped in the rough outer coat rather than falling free. Regular brushing removes this trapped hair. Summer care involves keeping the coat trimmed shorter for comfort in heat. Winter coat grows thicker and benefits from the full rough texture for weather protection.
Bouvier des Flandres.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly with line brushing technique. Clean beard daily. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for trimming and shaping. Strip or trim ear hair monthly. Check eyes daily for hair irritation. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Briard.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and long-toothed metal comb to maintain their long, flowing double coat. The coat has a dry, slightly wavy texture that mats easily if neglected. Use line brushing technique, parting the coat and working from skin outward in small sections. Pay extra attention to behind the ears, armpits, groin, and leg feathering.
Briard.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo followed by a light conditioner to aid in detangling. Detangle the coat completely before bathing as water tightens existing mats. Use a high-velocity dryer while brushing through the coat simultaneously for the best results.
commonIssues: Severe matting throughout the coat if brushing is skipped
Coat collecting debris, leaves, and burrs
Eye irritation from the characteristic fall of hair over eyes
Ear infections from heavy, hair-covered ears
Tartar and odor in beard hair
Briard.extendResults: Apply a leave-in conditioning spray daily before brushing to reduce static and breakage. Band or clip the fall hair above the eyes for daily comfort while keeping the look for shows. Keep the beard clean by wiping after meals. Regular trimming of the paw pads prevents slipping and debris collection.
Briard.seasonalCare: Briards shed minimally but their long coat requires consistent maintenance year-round. Summer care may involve a shorter trim for comfort and easier management. Winter coat provides excellent insulation but can collect snow and ice balls, so trim the feet and leg furnishings to prevent ice accumulation.
Briard.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush and metal comb using line brushing technique. Clean beard after every meal. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for shaping and thinning. Check eyes daily. Clean ears weekly. Trim paw pads and sanitary areas biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Bearded Collie.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and a long-toothed metal comb to manage their long, flat, flowing double coat. Mist the coat lightly with water or detangling spray before brushing to prevent breakage. Use line brushing from the skin outward, working in sections from the rear forward. The undercoat is soft and dense, requiring thorough combing to prevent hidden mats.
Bearded Collie.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and follow with a quality conditioner to maintain the coat's silky drape. Always detangle thoroughly before bathing. Blow dry with a high-velocity dryer while brushing through each section to achieve the proper flat, flowing finish.
commonIssues: Rapid matting especially during coat change from puppy to adult
Debris and mud collection in the long coat
Eye staining from hair falling across eyes
Ear infections under heavy ear fringe
Urine staining on belly furnishings
Bearded Collie.extendResults: Daily misting and brushing prevents the small tangles that become major mats within days. Keep sanitary areas trimmed short for hygiene. Trim feet and paw pads regularly to reduce mud and debris tracking. Use a snood or band to keep hair out of food and water bowls during meals.
Bearded Collie.seasonalCare: Beardies go through a dramatic coat change between 9-18 months, shedding the puppy coat, which causes extreme matting if not managed with daily, thorough brushing. Seasonal shedding is moderate but ongoing. Summer often means a shorter utility trim for active dogs. Winter coat grows fuller and provides good weather protection.
Bearded Collie.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with misting and line brushing. Comb through entire coat with metal comb 3-4 times weekly. Bathe every 3-4 weeks. Trim sanitary areas, paw pads, and feet biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Check eyes daily. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Rough Collie.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a pin brush and undercoat rake, focusing on the abundant mane, chest frill, and rear feathering where mats develop fastest. Use a long-toothed metal comb behind the ears, under the armpits, and in the groin. Brush in layers using line brushing technique to reach the dense undercoat beneath the long, harsh outer coat.
Rough Collie.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle shampoo followed by a light conditioner to maintain the coat's natural texture and stand-off quality. Always detangle before bathing. Blow dry completely with a high-velocity dryer while brushing to achieve the characteristic full, flowing appearance.
commonIssues: Matting behind ears, in armpits, and groin
Heavy seasonal shedding creating large fur volumes
Hot spots from trapped moisture in undercoat
Collie nose (nasal solar dermatitis)
Dry, brittle coat from over-conditioning
Rough Collie.extendResults: Focus daily attention on the three critical mat zones: behind ears, armpits, and groin. A light detangling spray before brushing reduces breakage in the long outer coat. Keep the feet trimmed neatly and the sanitary area clipped for hygiene. Avoid heavy conditioners that flatten the coat's natural stand-off texture.
Rough Collie.seasonalCare: Rough Collies blow their undercoat dramatically twice a year, with females often blowing coat after heat cycles as well. During these 3-4 week periods, daily undercoat raking is mandatory. Summer care involves ensuring dead undercoat is removed for cooling. Never shave a Rough Collie as the coat protects against sunburn and heat.
Rough Collie.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly with pin brush and undercoat rake. Comb critical mat zones daily. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Trim feet, sanitary areas, and hock feathering biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Apply nose protection for sun-sensitive Collies. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Smooth Collie.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to maintain their short, dense double coat. The coat lies flat and close to the body with a dense undercoat that sheds seasonally. Use a rubber curry brush to capture loose hair from the smooth outer coat, then follow with an undercoat rake to thin the dense underlayer.
Smooth Collie.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, all-purpose shampoo. The Smooth Collie's coat is relatively easy to wash and dry compared to the Rough variety. Ensure thorough rinsing as shampoo trapped in the dense undercoat causes irritation. Towel dry and finish with a low-heat dryer.
commonIssues: Heavier seasonal shedding than appearance suggests
Collie nose (nasal solar dermatitis)
Dry skin in winter
Undercoat matting if not regularly raked
Allergic skin reactions
Smooth Collie.extendResults: Regular rubber curry brushing between grooms maintains shine and controls loose hair in the home. A light coat conditioning spray reduces static and adds sheen. The Smooth Collie's coat is relatively low-maintenance but the dense undercoat should not be neglected during seasonal changes.
Smooth Collie.seasonalCare: Despite their smooth appearance, these dogs shed heavily twice a year when the dense undercoat releases. Daily raking during the 2-3 week blow period manages the volume. Summer coat is noticeably thinner. Winter undercoat grows in dense and requires regular maintenance to prevent matting close to the skin.
Smooth Collie.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with slicker brush and undercoat rake. Daily during seasonal shedding. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Apply nose protection for sun-sensitive individuals. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Cardigan Welsh Corgi.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to manage their medium-length, dense double coat. The outer coat is slightly harsh and weather-resistant while the undercoat is thick and plush. Focus on the ruff around the neck, the pants on the rear, and the chest where the coat is longest and densest.
Cardigan Welsh Corgi.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, moisturizing shampoo. Their low stature means the belly picks up ground moisture and dirt easily, so spot-clean the underside between baths. Dry thoroughly with a high-velocity dryer, paying special attention to the dense undercoat which retains water.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding disproportionate to body size
Belly and chest getting dirty from low ground clearance
Hot spots in humid weather
Dry, flaky skin in winter
Nail overgrowth from limited natural wear on short legs
Cardigan Welsh Corgi.extendResults: Daily belly wipes keep the underside clean between baths. Use a deshedding tool weekly to thin the undercoat and prevent loose hair from accumulating. Keep the pants trimmed for hygiene and the paw pads trimmed for traction. A light coat spray maintains texture between grooms.
Cardigan Welsh Corgi.seasonalCare: Cardigans blow their undercoat twice yearly in spring and fall, shedding surprising volumes for their size. Daily raking is needed during these periods. Summer grooming focuses on removing dead undercoat for cooling. Winter coat grows in thick and lush, requiring regular maintenance to stay mat-free.
Cardigan Welsh Corgi.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Wipe belly after walks in wet conditions. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Trim paw pads and sanitary areas biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks as short legs reduce natural wear.

Australian Cattle Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to maintain their smooth, dense double coat. The outer coat is short and straight while the undercoat is dense. A rubber curry brush works well for the initial pass to remove loose topcoat, followed by the undercoat rake during shedding season.
Australian Cattle Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, all-purpose shampoo. ACDs have a naturally clean, weather-resistant coat that repels dirt effectively. Avoid over-bathing as it compromises the coat's natural protective properties. Towel dry thoroughly and let the coat air-dry or use a low-heat dryer.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal undercoat shedding
Dry skin from over-bathing
Allergic dermatitis
Deafness-related handling sensitivity during grooming
Calluses on elbows from hard surface rest
Australian Cattle Dog.extendResults: The ACD coat is relatively self-maintaining between professional grooms. Regular rubber curry brushing distributes natural oils and removes loose hair. A weekly undercoat raking session prevents buildup that leads to shedding explosions. Feed omega-3 supplements for optimal coat health.
Australian Cattle Dog.seasonalCare: ACDs shed their dense undercoat twice yearly in spring and fall, producing significant fur volume over 2-3 weeks. Daily brushing with an undercoat rake manages this period. Summer coat is thinner and easier to maintain. Winter undercoat grows in dense and weather-resistant.
Australian Cattle Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Check ears weekly, especially in blue dogs prone to deafness. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Wipe coat with damp cloth after working in dirty conditions.

Canaan Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to maintain their medium-length, harsh double coat. The outer coat is straight and flat-lying while the undercoat varies in density by season. A pin brush works well for the outer coat while the rake removes loose undercoat efficiently.
Canaan Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks as the Canaan Dog has a naturally clean coat that repels dirt and debris. Use a mild, natural-ingredient shampoo that preserves the coat's protective qualities. Their desert-origin coat is designed to be low-maintenance and over-bathing strips essential oils.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal undercoat shed
Dry skin from over-grooming or over-bathing
Static in dry winter conditions
Sensitivity to chemical grooming products
Seasonal allergies in humid climates
Canaan Dog.extendResults: This breed's coat is one of the most self-maintaining among double-coated breeds. Regular brushing is more important than bathing. A natural-ingredient coat spray can help with static in dry conditions. Allow the coat's natural oils to protect and condition the hair between baths.
Canaan Dog.seasonalCare: Canaan Dogs blow their undercoat once or twice a year, with the heaviest shed occurring in spring. Daily raking during the 2-3 week blow period manages shedding. Summer coat is light and close-lying. Winter coat grows in a thick, insulating undercoat. This breed handles heat well thanks to their desert heritage.
Canaan Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during seasonal coat blow. Bathe every 8-12 weeks or as needed. Check ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Minimal professional grooming needed for this naturally clean breed.

Finnish Spitz.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a pin brush and undercoat rake to maintain their beautiful, golden-red double coat. The outer coat is long and harsh while the undercoat is short, soft, and dense. Brush with the grain of the coat to maintain the natural stand-off quality. Use a metal comb through the neck ruff and rear pants.
Finnish Spitz.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a gentle shampoo that preserves the coat's natural red-gold color. Avoid whitening or brightening shampoos that can alter the distinctive color. Use a light conditioner on the feathering only. Blow dry with a high-velocity dryer while brushing to achieve the proper full, standing coat.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal undercoat shedding
Color fading from sun exposure or improper products
Matting in the neck ruff and behind ears
Dry skin in arid conditions
Static buildup in winter
Finnish Spitz.extendResults: The Finnish Spitz coat should look naturally full and radiant. A light misting of coat conditioning spray before brushing prevents static and maintains shine. Avoid products that alter coat texture or flatten the natural stand-off quality. Regular undercoat raking keeps the coat looking full without matting underneath.
Finnish Spitz.seasonalCare: Heavy seasonal shedding occurs twice a year, with a dramatic undercoat blow in spring that requires daily raking for 2-3 weeks. The summer coat is thinner but maintains the golden color. Winter coat grows in at its fullest and most lustrous. Never shave this breed as the double coat provides essential temperature regulation.
Finnish Spitz.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly with pin brush and undercoat rake. Daily during seasonal shedding. Comb through ruff and pants 2-3 times weekly. Bathe every 8-10 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim paw pads biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Norwegian Elkhound.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to manage their thick, weather-resistant double coat. The silver-gray outer coat is coarse and straight while the undercoat is dense and woolly. Work systematically through the heavy neck ruff, chest, and rear pants where the coat is thickest. Use a metal comb for final detangling.
Norwegian Elkhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a mild, clarifying shampoo as the coat tends to hold natural oils. The Elkhound coat has a distinctive lack of doggy odor when properly maintained. Rinse extremely thoroughly as trapped shampoo in the dense undercoat causes flaking. Blow dry completely with a high-velocity dryer.
commonIssues: Extremely heavy seasonal shedding
Undercoat matting if not regularly raked
Hot spots in humid climates
Sebaceous cysts
Coat odor if undercoat stays damp
Norwegian Elkhound.extendResults: Regular undercoat raking between grooms prevents the buildup that causes the annual shed to become unmanageable. The Elkhound coat is relatively self-cleaning when properly maintained. Avoid conditioners that soften the naturally harsh outer coat. A quick daily brush through the ruff maintains the breed's distinctive profile.
Norwegian Elkhound.seasonalCare: Norwegian Elkhounds undergo one of the heaviest seasonal sheds of any breed, blowing their entire undercoat in spring over 2-4 weeks. Daily, intensive raking sessions are mandatory during this period. Some Elkhounds also blow coat in fall. Summer coat is noticeably thinner. Never shave this breed as the coat protects against both heat and cold.
Norwegian Elkhound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during the heavy spring coat blow. Rake undercoat twice weekly year-round. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Comb through ruff, chest, and pants with metal comb weekly. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Swedish Vallhund.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to maintain their medium-length, dense double coat. The outer coat is harsh and tight while the undercoat is soft and thick. Focus on the neck ruff, chest, and the rear feathering. A rubber curry brush works well for the shorter body coat.
Swedish Vallhund.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, all-purpose shampoo. The Vallhund's coat is naturally weather-resistant and stays relatively clean. Ensure complete drying of the dense undercoat with a high-velocity dryer to prevent dampness-related skin issues.
commonIssues: Seasonal undercoat shedding heavier than size suggests
Belly and chest collecting dirt from low ground clearance
Dry skin in winter
Matting in the neck ruff during coat blow
Nail overgrowth from limited natural wear on short legs
Swedish Vallhund.extendResults: Weekly undercoat raking prevents buildup between professional grooms. Wipe the belly after walks in wet or muddy conditions since their low stature picks up ground moisture. A light conditioning spray maintains coat texture and reduces static between baths.
Swedish Vallhund.seasonalCare: Vallhunds blow their undercoat twice yearly with particularly heavy shedding in spring. Daily raking is required during these 2-3 week periods. Summer coat is lighter and lower-maintenance. Winter coat grows dense and weatherproof, requiring regular maintenance to stay tangle-free.
Swedish Vallhund.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during seasonal coat blow. Wipe belly after wet walks. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks due to short-legged limited wear. Trim paw pads biweekly.

Schipperke.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to manage their thick, standoff double coat. The outer coat is harsh with a distinctive ruff around the neck, cape across the shoulders, and culottes on the rear. Use a pin brush on the longer ruff and culottes, and a rubber curry on the shorter body coat.
Schipperke.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that maintains the coat's natural harsh, standoff quality. Avoid heavy conditioners that flatten the coat. Blow dry with a high-velocity dryer to achieve the characteristic full, puffed-out profile of the breed.
commonIssues: Dramatic seasonal coat blow producing enormous fur volume for their size
Undercoat matting in the ruff and culottes
Static in dry conditions
Skin irritation if dead undercoat is not removed
Dry, itchy skin in winter
Schipperke.extendResults: Regular undercoat raking between grooms prevents the characteristic Schipperke blow from becoming overwhelming. The coat should maintain its sharp, fox-like silhouette between grooms. A light texturizing spray before brushing helps maintain the standoff quality. Avoid over-conditioning which flattens the coat.
Schipperke.seasonalCare: Schipperkes are notorious for their dramatic coat blow, which can happen once to three times per year and strips the entire undercoat in a matter of days. During this period, daily brushing removes handfuls of undercoat. The summer coat is noticeably thinner. Winter coat grows in at maximum density with the full ruff and cape.
Schipperke.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Rake undercoat weekly to prevent buildup. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Comb through ruff and culottes with metal comb twice weekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Belgian Tervuren.brushingRoutine: Brush daily or at minimum 4-5 times per week with a pin brush, slicker brush, and metal comb to maintain their long, abundant double coat. The mahogany-colored outer coat is straight and moderately harsh. Use line brushing technique through the heavy mane, chest frill, and leg feathering. Focus special attention on behind the ears, under the collar, and in the groin.
Belgian Tervuren.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, color-enhancing shampoo that preserves the rich mahogany tones. Follow with a light conditioner on the feathering and mane. Always detangle completely before bathing. Blow dry thoroughly with a high-velocity dryer while brushing to prevent matting and achieve the proper full coat profile.
commonIssues: Matting in mane, behind ears, and leg feathering
Color fading from sun exposure
Heavy seasonal shedding
Hot spots from moisture trapped in undercoat
Allergic dermatitis
Belgian Tervuren.extendResults: Daily attention to the three critical mat zones (behind ears, armpits, groin) prevents serious tangles. Use a leave-in conditioning spray when brushing to protect the coat and enhance the mahogany color. Keep sanitary areas trimmed for hygiene. Trim paw pad hair for traction and cleanliness.
Belgian Tervuren.seasonalCare: Tervurens shed their undercoat heavily twice a year, with females often blowing coat after heat cycles. Daily brushing with an undercoat rake is mandatory during these periods. Summer care may involve thinning the undercoat for comfort in heat. The winter coat reaches its fullest, most impressive state with the dramatic mane and frill.
Belgian Tervuren.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush and metal comb. Deep line brushing session 3 times weekly. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Trim sanitary areas, paw pads, and feet biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for shaping. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Belgian Sheepdog.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and metal comb to maintain their long, solid black double coat. The outer coat is abundant, straight, and moderately harsh with a dense undercoat. Use line brushing through the mane, chest frill, leg feathering, and tail plume. A slicker brush helps remove loose undercoat from the denser areas.
Belgian Sheepdog.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, color-safe shampoo that maintains the deep black without fading. A light conditioner on the feathering prevents breakage. Their black coat shows water spots and residue easily, so rinse extremely thoroughly. Blow dry completely while brushing through the coat.
commonIssues: Matting in the dense mane and behind ears
Sun-bleached coat turning rusty brown
Heavy seasonal shedding
Dandruff visible against the black coat
Static electricity in dry weather
Belgian Sheepdog.extendResults: The black coat shows every flaw, so regular maintenance is visible in this breed. Daily brushing keeps the coat gleaming and prevents the dull, unkempt look that develops quickly. Use a coat polish spray designed for black coats to enhance shine. Limit prolonged sun exposure to prevent rusty discoloration.
Belgian Sheepdog.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding twice a year with additional coat loss in females after heat cycles. During the blow, daily raking removes large volumes of loose undercoat. Summer sun can bleach the coat to a rusty brown, so limit sun exposure during peak hours. The winter coat reaches maximum density and looks most impressive.
Belgian Sheepdog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush and metal comb. Line brush through all feathering 3-4 times weekly. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Trim paw pads, sanitary areas, and feet biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Dutch Shepherd.brushingRoutine: Brushing varies by coat variety. Short-haired: brush 2-3 times weekly with a rubber curry and bristle brush. Long-haired: brush 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake, focusing on feathering and ruff. Wire-haired: hand strip quarterly and brush weekly with a slicker brush. All varieties have a dense undercoat requiring regular raking.
Dutch Shepherd.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild shampoo appropriate for the coat type. Short-haired varieties need basic maintenance. Long-haired varieties benefit from a light conditioner on the feathering. Wire-haired varieties should be bathed minimally to maintain the harsh texture, and never conditioned.
commonIssues: Seasonal undercoat shedding across all coat types
Wire-haired variety requires hand stripping to maintain texture
Long-haired variety mats in feathering
Brindle pattern can mask skin conditions
Dry skin if over-bathed
Dutch Shepherd.extendResults: Maintenance needs vary significantly by coat type. Short-haired is the lowest maintenance with regular curry brushing. Long-haired needs daily attention to feathering. Wire-haired requires the most specialized care with quarterly hand stripping to maintain proper texture and brindle pattern clarity.
Dutch Shepherd.seasonalCare: All three coat varieties shed their undercoat seasonally, with the heaviest blow in spring. Short-haired varieties shed surprisingly heavily for their coat length. Long-haired varieties require daily brushing during the blow. Wire-haired varieties shed less dramatically but still need increased brushing in spring.
Dutch Shepherd.maintenanceSchedule: Short: brush 2-3 times weekly. Long: brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during shedding. Wire: weekly brushing, hand stripping every 3-4 months. All varieties: bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Bergamasco.brushingRoutine: The Bergamasco does NOT require traditional brushing once their signature flocks (mats) have formed, typically between ages 1-3. Before flock formation, gently separate the developing mats by hand into flat, fan-shaped sections. After flock formation, check flocks regularly to ensure they are properly separated and not fusing into large, unmanageable masses.
Bergamasco.bathingTips: Bathe only 1-3 times per year unless truly dirty. Use a diluted gentle shampoo worked into the base of the flocks near the skin. The flocks must be thoroughly rinsed and then dried completely, which can take 24+ hours with fans and low heat. Never use conditioner as it softens the coat and prevents proper flock formation.
commonIssues: Flocks fusing together into massive, unmanageable plates
Trapped moisture causing skin infections or mildew in flocks
Difficulty checking skin beneath formed flocks
Extended drying time creating logistical challenges
Misunderstanding of coat care leading to improper shaving
Bergamasco.extendResults: Between baths, inspect the skin at the base of flocks regularly for redness, odor, or irritation. Ensure flocks hang freely and are separated to their full width. The coat naturally repels dirt and debris once properly formed. Keep the belly and sanitary areas trimmed short for hygiene since the flocks do not cover these areas.
Bergamasco.seasonalCare: The Bergamasco coat is naturally temperature-regulating year-round. In summer, the flocks provide insulation from heat and sun protection. In winter, the coat provides warmth. Never shave a Bergamasco as the flocks take years to reform. The coat requires minimal seasonal adjustment once properly formed.
Bergamasco.maintenanceSchedule: Hand-separate flocks as needed, checking weekly. Bathe 1-3 times per year with complete drying afterward. Trim sanitary areas and belly monthly. Check skin at flock bases weekly for issues. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Professional grooming only with breed-experienced groomers.

Komondor.brushingRoutine: The Komondor coat is NEVER brushed in the traditional sense. The characteristic cords must be separated by hand as they develop, typically starting between 8-12 months of age. Once formed, regularly pull cords apart from base to tip to prevent them from fusing into large, plate-like mats. Check the coat weekly by running your hands through to identify any cords that are joining together.
Komondor.bathingTips: Bathe only when necessary, typically 3-4 times per year, using a diluted gentle or whitening shampoo squeezed through each cord. Rinsing is critical and time-consuming since shampoo trapped in cords causes buildup and odor. Drying takes 24-48 hours with high-velocity dryers and fans. Never leave cords damp as this causes mildew and dangerous skin infections.
commonIssues: Cords fusing into large plates if not regularly separated
Mildew and musty odor from inadequate drying
Skin infections hidden beneath the cord mass
Extended drying time as a management challenge
Urine and fecal staining on rear cords
Komondor.extendResults: Regular cord separation is the primary maintenance between baths. Keep sanitary area cords trimmed shorter for hygiene. The white coat shows staining easily, so blot any dirty areas promptly. Ensure the dog sleeps in a clean, dry area to prevent cord contamination. Good ventilation is essential for coat health.
Komondor.seasonalCare: The Komondor's corded coat provides natural insulation in both heat and cold. Summer care involves ensuring the skin beneath the cords stays dry and aired out, which is more challenging in humid climates. Never shave the cords as they take years to regrow. Rainy seasons require vigilant drying after exposure.
Komondor.maintenanceSchedule: Separate cords by hand weekly from base to tip. Bathe 3-4 times yearly with complete 24-48 hour drying protocol. Trim sanitary area cords monthly. Check skin at cord bases weekly for odor or irritation. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Only use breed-experienced groomers.

Puli.brushingRoutine: Like the Komondor, the Puli coat is NEVER traditionally brushed. Cords are separated by hand as they form, starting around 9-12 months of age. Pull individual cords apart from base to tip regularly to maintain uniform, pencil-thin cords rather than flat mats. Work through the entire coat weekly, paying extra attention to the areas behind the ears and under the legs where cords tend to fuse.
Puli.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a diluted gentle shampoo worked through each cord. The Puli's smaller size makes bathing more manageable than the Komondor. Squeeze shampoo through cords rather than rubbing. Rinse thoroughly as product buildup creates weight and odor. Drying takes several hours with a high-velocity dryer and additional fan time.
commonIssues: Cords joining together into flat mats rather than round cords
Musty odor from inadequately dried cords
Debris trapped in cords during outdoor activity
Eye irritation from corded hair falling across eyes
Difficulty assessing skin condition under cords
Puli.extendResults: Weekly cord separation keeps the coat in proper form between baths. The corded coat naturally repels dirt and debris when properly maintained. Keep face cords banded or trimmed away from the eyes for comfort and vision. Trim sanitary area cords shorter for hygiene. Blot any wet or dirty spots promptly.
Puli.seasonalCare: The Puli's corded coat provides natural temperature regulation year-round. The coat grows continuously and cords lengthen over time, eventually reaching the ground in mature dogs. Summer care involves ensuring adequate airflow at the skin level beneath cords. Winter cords provide excellent insulation. Never shave unless medically necessary.
Puli.maintenanceSchedule: Separate cords by hand weekly, more frequently for developing coats. Bathe every 4-6 weeks with complete drying protocol (several hours). Trim sanitary cords monthly. Band or trim face cords for eye comfort. Check skin at cord bases weekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Polish Lowland Sheepdog.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and metal comb to maintain their long, thick, shaggy double coat. The outer coat is crisp and slightly wavy while the undercoat is soft and dense. Use thorough line brushing technique from the skin outward to prevent hidden mats. Pay special attention to behind the ears, under the armpits, in the groin, and on the leg furnishings.
Polish Lowland Sheepdog.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo followed by a light detangling conditioner. Always detangle the coat completely before wetting as water tightens existing mats. Blow dry with a high-velocity dryer while brushing through each section to prevent mat formation during drying.
commonIssues: Rapid matting throughout the coat if daily brushing is missed
Debris collection in the long, shaggy coat
Beard becoming dirty and smelly from food and water
Eye irritation from hair falling over the face
Ear infections beneath heavy ear fringe
Polish Lowland Sheepdog.extendResults: This breed requires significant daily commitment to coat maintenance. Use a detangling spray before every brushing session to reduce breakage and ease mat removal. Clean the beard after every meal. Keep the hair trimmed between paw pads for traction and cleanliness. Trim sanitary areas regularly for hygiene.
Polish Lowland Sheepdog.seasonalCare: The PON sheds minimally as dead hair gets trapped in the coat rather than falling free, which contributes to matting. Regular daily brushing removes this trapped hair. Summer grooming may involve a shorter utility clip for comfort and easier maintenance. Winter coat grows in thick and requires maximum grooming effort.
Polish Lowland Sheepdog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush and metal comb using line brushing. Clean beard after every meal. Bathe every 3-4 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for shaping. Trim paw pads and sanitary areas biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Check eyes daily for hair irritation. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Miniature American Shepherd.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to manage their medium-length double coat. The outer coat is straight to wavy and weather-resistant, while the undercoat is dense and soft. Focus on the mane around the neck, the pants on the hindquarters, and the leg feathering. Use a metal comb behind the ears and in the groin.
Miniature American Shepherd.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo followed by a light conditioner on the feathering. Their moderate coat length makes bathing manageable. Ensure thorough drying of the dense undercoat with a high-velocity dryer to prevent dampness-related skin issues.
commonIssues: Seasonal shedding heavier than expected for their size
Matting behind ears and in the groin
Tangling in the pants and leg feathering
Allergic skin reactions
Tear staining in lighter-colored dogs
Miniature American Shepherd.extendResults: Focus daily attention on the areas behind the ears and in the groin where mats form first. A detangling spray before brushing reduces breakage in the feathering. Keep paw pad hair trimmed for traction and cleanliness. The coat maintains its appearance well between grooms with consistent brushing.
Miniature American Shepherd.seasonalCare: Mini Americans blow their undercoat twice a year in spring and fall. Daily brushing with an undercoat rake manages the shed over 2-3 weeks. Females may blow coat after heat cycles. Summer coat is lighter and easier to maintain. Winter coat grows in full with the characteristic mane and pants.
Miniature American Shepherd.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during seasonal shedding. Comb through feathering with metal comb twice weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Trim paw pads and sanitary areas biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.
