Entlebucher Mountain Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a rubber curry brush and bristle brush to maintain their short, hard, close-fitting double coat. The coat is shiny and dense with a tight undercoat. A rubber grooming mitt is effective at capturing loose hair and stimulating skin circulation. Follow with a bristle brush for finishing shine.
Entlebucher Mountain Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, all-purpose shampoo. The Entlebucher's short coat is naturally clean and low-maintenance. Their tricolor coat maintains its shine well between baths. Towel dry thoroughly and allow to air dry or use a low-heat dryer.
commonIssues: Moderate seasonal shedding
Dry skin in winter from indoor heating
Allergic skin reactions
Hot spots in humid conditions
Calluses on elbows from hard surface rest
Entlebucher Mountain Dog.extendResults: Regular rubber curry grooming between baths keeps the coat gleaming and controls loose hair. A light coat conditioning spray adds sheen and reduces static. Omega-3 fatty acid supplements support the coat's natural shine from within. The short coat is one of the easier Swiss breeds to maintain.
Entlebucher Mountain Dog.seasonalCare: Moderate shedding with seasonal increases in spring and fall as the undercoat cycles. Daily curry brushing during these periods manages the increased hair loss. Summer coat is thinner and sleeker. Winter undercoat grows in denser for insulation. The hard outer coat provides natural weather resistance.
Entlebucher Mountain Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with rubber curry and bristle brush. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Wipe coat with damp cloth after outdoor activities as needed.

Appenzeller Sennenhund.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake to manage their thick, double coat. The outer coat is dense, glossy, and close-fitting while the undercoat is thick and black, gray, or brown. A rubber curry brush for the initial pass removes loose topcoat, followed by an undercoat rake during shedding periods.
Appenzeller Sennenhund.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, clarifying shampoo that maintains the coat's natural shine. The Appenzeller's coat has a natural sheen when healthy. Rinse thoroughly to avoid residue in the dense undercoat. Towel dry and finish with a medium-heat dryer.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal undercoat shedding
Undercoat matting if neglected during shed periods
Dry skin from over-bathing
Hot spots in warm, humid climates
Static buildup in dry conditions
Appenzeller Sennenhund.extendResults: Regular undercoat raking between professional grooms prevents the shed season from becoming overwhelming. A light coat conditioning spray maintains the breed's characteristic glossy appearance. The coat is relatively self-maintaining when undercoat management is consistent. Omega fatty acid supplements enhance the natural coat sheen.
Appenzeller Sennenhund.seasonalCare: Heavy shedding twice yearly in spring and fall with a significant undercoat release. Daily raking during these 2-3 week periods is essential. Summer coat is lighter and easier to maintain. Winter coat grows in thick and dense, providing natural weather protection for this mountain breed.
Appenzeller Sennenhund.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during seasonal shedding. Rake undercoat weekly to prevent buildup. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Kuvasz.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a pin brush, slicker brush, and undercoat rake to maintain their medium-long, white double coat. The outer coat is wavy with a dense, woolly undercoat. Use line brushing through the neck mane, chest frill, and rear feathering. A long-toothed metal comb catches hidden tangles close to the skin.
Kuvasz.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a whitening or brightening shampoo to maintain the brilliant white coat. Follow with a light conditioner to prevent breakage. The white coat shows discoloration easily, so thorough rinsing and complete drying are essential. Use a high-velocity dryer as the dense coat retains moisture.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding of large volumes of white fur
Tear staining on the white face
Matting in the neck ruff and behind ears
Yellowing or staining of the white coat
Hot spots from trapped moisture in thick undercoat
Kuvasz.extendResults: The bright white coat requires regular attention to staining prevention. Wipe the face daily to prevent tear staining. Use a waterless whitening spray between baths to maintain brightness. Regular undercoat raking prevents the dead undercoat from trapping moisture and causing odor. Keep the coat clean and dry.
Kuvasz.seasonalCare: Kuvasz blow their heavy undercoat once or twice a year, producing enormous volumes of white fur over 2-4 weeks. Daily raking with an undercoat rake is mandatory during this period. Summer coat is significantly thinner. Winter coat grows in at maximum density with a full mane and feathering. Never shave as the white coat protects against sunburn.
Kuvasz.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Wipe face daily for tear stain prevention. Comb through mane and feathering with metal comb twice weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks with whitening shampoo. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Maremma Sheepdog.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a pin brush and undercoat rake to maintain their long, thick, white double coat. The outer coat is slightly harsh and wavy while the undercoat is dense and woolly. Focus on the heavy neck ruff, behind the ears, and the rear feathering where mats form quickly. Use a long-toothed metal comb for final detangling.
Maremma Sheepdog.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-10 weeks with a whitening shampoo to maintain the bright white coat. The Maremma's coat has natural dirt-repelling properties and often shakes clean after drying. Follow with a light conditioner on the feathering. Ensure complete drying with a high-velocity dryer as the dense coat holds moisture for extended periods.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding of the white undercoat
Matting in the neck ruff, behind ears, and rear feathering
Yellowing or staining of the white coat
Hot spots from trapped moisture
Mud and debris staining on the white coat
Maremma Sheepdog.extendResults: The Maremma coat has a remarkable self-cleaning quality. When dried mud is left to dry naturally, it often brushes out easily. Avoid excessive bathing which strips the natural oils that give this coat its weather-resistant properties. Regular undercoat raking keeps the coat healthy and prevents odor from trapped dead coat.
Maremma Sheepdog.seasonalCare: Maremmas blow their undercoat heavily once or twice a year, producing substantial volumes of white fur. Daily raking for 2-4 weeks manages this period. Summer coat is noticeably lighter and shorter. Winter coat grows in thick and full, providing the breed's characteristic lion-like appearance. Never shave as the white coat protects against sunburn.
Maremma Sheepdog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly with pin brush and rake, daily during seasonal blow. Comb through mane and feathering with metal comb twice weekly. Bathe every 6-10 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim paw pads biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Black Russian Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush, pin brush, and metal comb to maintain their thick, coarse, slightly wavy double coat. The coat grows 4-6 inches in length and has a dense undercoat. Use line brushing through the full body coat, paying special attention to the leg furnishings, chest, and the signature beard and mustache. A stripping comb helps maintain proper coat texture.
Black Russian Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that maintains the coat's harsh, weather-resistant quality. Avoid conditioners that soften the coat excessively. The beard requires separate cleaning with every bath and between baths as it collects food and water. Blow dry completely with a high-velocity dryer.
commonIssues: Matting throughout the long, dense coat
Beard becoming smelly and dirty from food and water
Eye irritation from overgrown fall
Ear infections from hair in ear canals
Hot spots under matted areas
Black Russian Terrier.extendResults: Daily beard cleaning is essential for this breed. Wipe the beard with grooming wipes after every meal and water session. Regular trimming of the fall above the eyes maintains vision and comfort. Strip excess hair from ear canals monthly to improve ventilation. Keep feet trimmed between the paw pads.
Black Russian Terrier.seasonalCare: The BRT sheds minimally as dead hair gets trapped in the dense coat, requiring regular removal through brushing. Summer grooming may involve a shorter clip for comfort, particularly in humid climates like Houston. Winter coat grows in thick and provides excellent cold-weather protection. Hand stripping twice a year maintains optimal coat texture.
Black Russian Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly with line brushing. Clean beard daily. Bathe every 3-4 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for trimming and shaping. Hand strip or clipper trim quarterly. Strip ear hair monthly. Trim paw pads biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Giant Schnauzer.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush and metal comb to maintain their dense, wiry double coat. The outer coat is hard and wiry while the undercoat is soft and dense. Use a stripping knife or stripping stone quarterly to maintain the proper wire texture. The leg furnishings, beard, and eyebrows need daily combing to prevent mats.
Giant Schnauzer.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a texturizing shampoo designed for wire-coated breeds. Never use conditioner on the body coat as it softens the wire texture. A light conditioner can be used sparingly on leg furnishings only. Blow dry while brushing through the coat to maintain proper texture and lay.
commonIssues: Beard becoming dirty and smelly from food and water
Matting in leg furnishings
Coat softening if not properly hand stripped
Eye irritation from overgrown eyebrow hair
Ear infections from hair in ear canals
Giant Schnauzer.extendResults: Daily beard cleaning after meals and water is mandatory for this breed. Use a drip-catching water bowl or beard bib to reduce staining. Regular hand stripping every 8-12 weeks maintains the characteristic wire texture and color intensity. Clipper cutting is faster but softens coat texture over time.
Giant Schnauzer.seasonalCare: Wire-coated Giants shed minimally when properly hand stripped, making them more manageable than many large breeds for indoor living. Without stripping, dead coat builds up and causes skin irritation. The coat provides good weather protection year-round. Summer may warrant a shorter trim for comfort, especially in humid climates.
Giant Schnauzer.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly. Comb beard and furnishings daily. Clean beard after every meal. Bathe every 3-4 weeks. Hand strip every 8-12 weeks (or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks). Strip ear hair monthly. Trim paw pads biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Standard Schnauzer.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and metal comb to maintain their dense, wiry double coat. The wire outer coat is hard and close-fitting while the undercoat is soft. Hand strip with a stripping knife every 8-12 weeks to maintain proper wire texture and color. Comb through the beard, eyebrows, and leg furnishings daily to prevent tangles.
Standard Schnauzer.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo for wire-coated breeds. Avoid conditioner on the body coat to maintain the hard, wiry texture. The beard needs extra attention during bathing. Blow dry while combing through the furnishings. The body coat can be toweled dry.
commonIssues: Beard staining and odor from food and water
Leg furnishing tangles and mats
Coat softening from clipper cutting versus hand stripping
Eye irritation from untrimmed eyebrows
Skin bumps if dead coat is not stripped
Standard Schnauzer.extendResults: Clean the beard after every meal with a grooming wipe or damp cloth to prevent odor and staining. Keep eyebrows trimmed to a neat arch that allows clear vision. Regular hand stripping maintains the hard wire texture, rich color, and natural weather resistance that clipper cutting gradually destroys.
Standard Schnauzer.seasonalCare: Properly hand-stripped Schnauzers shed very little year-round, making them excellent for indoor living. The wire coat provides good insulation in both heat and cold. Without regular stripping, dead coat accumulates and causes itching and skin irritation. Seasonal adjustments are minimal for this well-insulated breed.
Standard Schnauzer.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Comb beard, eyebrows, and furnishings daily. Clean beard after meals. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Hand strip every 8-12 weeks or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks. Strip ear hair monthly. Trim paw pads biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Bouvier des Flandres (139).brushingRoutine: Brush thoroughly 3-4 times per week with a slicker brush, pin brush, and metal comb to maintain their rough, tousled double coat. Work through the dense coat using line brushing technique to prevent mats forming close to the skin. The coarse outer coat traps dead hair rather than shedding it freely, requiring thorough weekly removal. Pay special attention to the heavily furnished legs and the beard area.
Bouvier des Flandres (139).bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a texturizing shampoo that preserves the rough coat quality. Avoid heavy conditioners that soften the coat beyond its natural harsh texture. Ensure thorough blow drying with a high-velocity dryer as the dense undercoat retains water for extended periods and wet coat leads to skin problems.
commonIssues: Matting in beard, legs, and underbelly
Beard odor from trapped food and water
Eye irritation from facial hair
Ear canal hair causing infections
Hot spots from inadequate drying after baths or rain
Bouvier des Flandres (139).extendResults: Daily beard cleaning is the single most impactful maintenance habit for this breed. Wipe the beard after every meal and water session. Keep hair trimmed away from the eyes for comfort. Strip or trim ear canal hair regularly to prevent ear infections. Regular line brushing prevents the dead coat buildup that causes matting.
Bouvier des Flandres (139).seasonalCare: The Bouvier sheds minimally because dead hair stays trapped in the rough outer coat, requiring manual removal through regular brushing. Summer care involves keeping the coat at a manageable length for heat management. Winter coat provides excellent protection and can be left longer. The rough texture naturally repels water and dirt.
Bouvier des Flandres (139).maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly using line brushing technique. Clean beard daily. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for trimming and shaping. Strip ear hair monthly. Check eyes daily. Trim paw pads biweekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Lakeland Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and metal comb to maintain their hard, wiry double coat. Hand stripping every 8-12 weeks is ideal for maintaining proper texture and vibrant color. Comb through the leg furnishings, beard, and eyebrows daily to prevent tangling. The body coat should be tight and close-lying.
Lakeland Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo for wire coats. Avoid conditioners on the body that soften the wire texture. A light conditioner on leg furnishings only can help with detangling. Clean the beard thoroughly during each bath session.
commonIssues: Beard staining and odor from food and water
Leg furnishing mats
Coat softening from excessive clipper work
Skin irritation under matted furnishings
Tear staining near eyes
Lakeland Terrier.extendResults: Daily beard wiping after meals and water keeps this breed presentable between grooms. Hand stripping maintains the characteristic hard jacket that repels water and dirt naturally. Keep eyebrows shaped to frame the eyes without obstructing vision. Regular combing of furnishings prevents small tangles from becoming serious mats.
Lakeland Terrier.seasonalCare: Properly stripped Lakelands shed very little year-round, making them excellent for indoor living. The wire coat provides natural insulation in both seasons. Dead coat must be stripped regularly regardless of season to prevent skin problems. The coat requires minimal seasonal adjustment when properly maintained.
Lakeland Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Comb beard and furnishings daily. Clean beard after meals. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Hand strip every 8-12 weeks or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Welsh Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and metal comb. The Welsh Terrier has a hard, wiry, close-fitting outer coat with a short, soft undercoat. Hand stripping every 8-12 weeks maintains the proper tight, jacket-like appearance and the rich black and tan coloring. Comb the beard, eyebrows, and leg furnishings daily.
Welsh Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that preserves the wire coat's hard quality. The compact size makes bathing manageable. Clean the beard area thoroughly as it tends to collect food and water. Blow dry while combing through furnishings to set them properly.
commonIssues: Beard becoming dirty and smelly
Furnishing tangles on legs
Coat fading if clipped instead of stripped
Skin irritation from retained dead coat
Ear wax buildup
Welsh Terrier.extendResults: Daily beard cleaning is essential for the Welsh Terrier's neat appearance. Hand stripping preserves the characteristic hard coat texture and vivid tan-and-black color that fades with clipper cutting. Keep the eyebrows trimmed to a forward-pointing shape for the breed's distinctive expression.
Welsh Terrier.seasonalCare: Hand-stripped Welsh Terriers shed minimally and are well-suited for year-round indoor living. The wire coat provides natural weather protection. Without stripping, dead coat becomes trapped and can cause skin bumps and irritation. The coat requires consistent maintenance rather than dramatic seasonal changes.
Welsh Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Comb beard, eyebrows, and furnishings daily. Clean beard after meals. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Hand strip every 8-12 weeks or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Irish Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and metal comb to maintain their dense, wiry coat that lies close to the body. The wire coat is one of the hardest and flattest among terrier breeds. Hand strip with a stripping knife every 8-12 weeks to maintain the tight, broken texture and vibrant red color. Comb through the beard and leg furnishings daily.
Irish Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo. The Irish Terrier's coat should remain hard and close-fitting. Avoid conditioners on the body coat. The characteristic red color is maintained best through hand stripping rather than clipper cutting. Clean the beard thoroughly during each bath.
commonIssues: Beard staining and odor
Color fading to a lighter, washed-out red if clipped
Skin irritation from trapped dead coat
Furnishing tangles on legs
Foot pad injuries from their active nature
Irish Terrier.extendResults: The Irish Terrier's brilliant red coat color depends on proper hand stripping. Clipper cutting causes the coat to lighten and soften over generations of cuts. Clean the beard after meals to prevent the dark staining that shows prominently against the red coat. Keep feet trimmed neatly between the pads.
Irish Terrier.seasonalCare: Hand-stripped Irish Terriers shed minimally year-round. The tight wire coat provides excellent weather protection in rain and cold without seasonal coat changes. Summer care in hot climates may involve keeping the coat shorter but still properly stripped. The coat naturally repels water and dries quickly.
Irish Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Comb beard and furnishings daily. Clean beard after meals. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Hand strip every 8-12 weeks or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears biweekly. Trim paw pads biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Glen of Imaal Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and metal comb to maintain their medium-length, harsh double coat. The outer coat is rough and slightly wavy while the undercoat is soft. Hand stripping twice a year is recommended to maintain proper coat texture. The coat is less demanding than many terrier breeds but still needs regular attention to the chest, legs, and underbelly.
Glen of Imaal Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle shampoo that maintains the coat's natural rough texture. Avoid heavy conditioning products. The Glen's moderate coat length makes bathing straightforward. Towel dry and finish with a medium-heat dryer while brushing through the coat.
commonIssues: Coat softening without regular stripping
Matting in the softer undercoat areas
Ear infections from heavy ear leather
Skin irritation from trapped dead coat
Excessive hair between paw pads
Glen of Imaal Terrier.extendResults: The Glen's coat is more forgiving than many terrier breeds but benefits from regular hand stripping to maintain proper texture and color. Keep the hair between paw pads trimmed as their broad feet collect debris. Regular combing through the chest and underbelly prevents hidden mats.
Glen of Imaal Terrier.seasonalCare: Glen of Imaals shed lightly when properly stripped. Without stripping, dead coat accumulates and can cause skin issues. The rough coat provides good weather protection in all seasons. Summer care may involve a tidying trim for neatness. The breed handles both warm and cool temperatures well.
Glen of Imaal Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Hand strip twice yearly for proper coat maintenance. Trim between paw pads biweekly. Clean ears weekly due to drop ear shape. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks for tidying.

Dandie Dinmont Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Dandie Dinmont's unique coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and medium comb. The coat is a crisp mixture of hard and soft hair, with a distinctive silky topknot. Hand-strip the body coat to maintain proper texture. Use a soft brush on the topknot to avoid breaking the fine silky hair. Comb through the leg furnishings and underbody where the softer coat mats.
Dandie Dinmont Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a mild shampoo. Use a light conditioner only on the topknot and furnishings, not on the body coat which should remain crisp. The body coat benefits from a texturizing shampoo. Pat dry the topknot carefully and blow-dry on low to preserve the silky texture separately from the body coat.
commonIssues: Topknot breakage from rough handling
Body coat softening if not properly hand-stripped
Mat formation in softer undercarriage and leg furnishings
Back problems (IVDD) requiring careful positioning on grooming table
Ear hair growth and potential infections
Dandie Dinmont Terrier.extendResults: Regular hand-stripping every 8-10 weeks maintains the proper coat texture that clipping cannot replicate. Between strips, pull loose dead hairs by hand to maintain the coat's crisp feel. Apply a light silk spray to the topknot to keep it flowing and prevent breakage.
Dandie Dinmont Terrier.seasonalCare: The hand-stripped coat sheds minimally. Clipped coats will shed more as the coat texture changes. In summer, the crisp outer coat protects from sun. In winter, the double-layered coat provides warmth. The topknot requires year-round maintenance regardless of season.
Dandie Dinmont Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly. Groom topknot daily. Check and comb furnishings every other day. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional hand-stripping every 8-10 weeks or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks.

Sealyham Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Sealyham's wiry, weather-resistant double coat 3-4 times weekly with a slicker brush and stripping knife. The hard outer coat and soft undercoat require regular hand-stripping to maintain proper texture. Focus on the beard, leg furnishings, and the skirt along the body. Use a pin brush on the softer furnishings to prevent breakage.
Sealyham Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that preserves the wiry coat's harsh feel. Avoid conditioners on the body coat; apply only to the beard and leg furnishings. The white coat benefits from a whitening shampoo on the legs and beard. Blow-dry while hand-shaping the coat into the breed's rectangular silhouette.
commonIssues: Beard staining from food and water
Body coat softening from clipping instead of stripping
White coat yellowing and environmental staining
Mat formation in the soft undercoat
Retinal dysplasia requiring gentle handling around the face
Sealyham Terrier.extendResults: Rinse and comb the beard after every meal to prevent staining. Apply a whitening product weekly to keep the coat bright. Between professional grooms, pull loose dead coat by hand to maintain texture. A waterproof bib during feeding protects the beard from food stains.
Sealyham Terrier.seasonalCare: Hand-stripped coats shed minimally regardless of season. The wiry coat is naturally weather-resistant and provides insulation year-round. In humid Houston summers, the beard may develop odor; increase face washing frequency. In winter, the dense double coat is well-suited for the breed's natural protection.
Sealyham Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly. Clean and comb beard daily. Hand-strip body coat every 8-10 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Skye Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush daily with a pin brush and long-toothed metal comb to maintain their spectacularly long, flowing double coat. The outer coat is hard, straight, and flat-lying, reaching 5-6 inches in length, while the undercoat is short, soft, and woolly. Part the coat down the center of the back and brush from the skin outward in sections. Pay special attention to the characteristic veil of hair covering the face.
Skye Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo followed by a light conditioner to prevent tangling of the long coat. Always detangle completely before wetting. The extremely long coat takes time to wash and rinse thoroughly. Blow dry with a cool to medium-heat dryer while brushing flat to maintain the straight, flowing appearance.
commonIssues: Matting underneath the long, flat-lying coat
Ground-level coat dragging in dirt and moisture
Eye irritation from the facial veil
Long ears collecting debris
Coat breakage from improper brushing technique
Skye Terrier.extendResults: The Skye Terrier's floor-length coat demands daily commitment. Use a leave-in conditioning spray before every brushing session to prevent breakage. Keep the coat clean and dry as the long hair acts like a mop at ground level. Trim the coat length if the dog is not being shown to improve quality of life and reduce maintenance.
Skye Terrier.seasonalCare: The Skye Terrier sheds minimally with dead hair staying trapped in the long coat. Year-round daily brushing is non-negotiable regardless of season. Rainy and muddy seasons are particularly challenging as the low-hanging coat picks up everything from the ground. Summer care may involve shortening the coat for comfort in heat.
Skye Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush and metal comb. Bathe every 3-4 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for trimming and shaping. Check eyes daily under the facial veil. Clean ears weekly. Trim ground-sweeping coat as needed for hygiene. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks.

Cesky Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Cesky Terrier's soft, silky coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and comb. Unlike most terriers, the Cesky is groomed with clippers rather than hand-stripped, giving the coat a softer feel. Brush through the longer furnishings on the legs, belly, and beard thoroughly. Use a small comb around the distinctive facial furnishings including the bushy eyebrows and long beard.
Cesky Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. The silky coat benefits from a light conditioner on the furnishings. The Cesky's coat colors range from grey-blue to light coffee, and a color-enhancing shampoo maintains vibrancy. Blow-dry on medium heat while brushing the furnishings smooth.
commonIssues: Beard tangling and staining from food and water
Furnishing mats on legs and underbody
Coat color changes over the dog's lifetime complicating color matching
Eyebrow grooming challenges
Scottie cramp-like syndrome affecting some lines
Cesky Terrier.extendResults: Clean the beard after meals with a damp cloth to prevent staining and tangling. Apply a leave-in conditioner to furnishings between baths to maintain softness. Spot-trim the body coat between full grooms to maintain the breed's clean outline. A silicone-free detangling spray keeps furnishings tangle-free.
Cesky Terrier.seasonalCare: The Cesky's clipped coat sheds minimally year-round. In summer, a shorter clip keeps the dog comfortable in Houston's heat. In winter, allow slightly more length for warmth. The soft coat texture is consistent across seasons. Beard care is a year-round commitment regardless of season.
Cesky Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly. Clean beard daily. Comb through furnishings every other day. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional clipping every 6-8 weeks.

Rat Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times per week with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt to maintain their short, smooth, dense coat. A soft bristle brush provides a finishing pass for shine. The coat is close-lying and low-maintenance, making grooming quick and simple. Focus on the chest and thighs where the coat is slightly denser.
Rat Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, all-purpose shampoo. The Rat Terrier's short coat dries quickly and stays relatively clean between baths. Avoid over-bathing as it strips the natural oils that keep the coat smooth and shiny. Towel dry and allow to air dry.
commonIssues: Seasonal shedding more than expected for their coat length
Allergic dermatitis
Dry skin in winter
Sensitivity to cold due to thin coat
Demodectic mange susceptibility in some lines
Rat Terrier.extendResults: Regular rubber curry brushing distributes natural oils and maintains the coat's characteristic smooth shine. A light coat conditioning spray reduces static and adds luster between baths. Omega-3 supplements support coat health from within. The Rat Terrier is one of the easier breeds to maintain between professional grooms.
Rat Terrier.seasonalCare: Rat Terriers shed moderately year-round with seasonal increases in spring and fall. Daily curry brushing during shed periods controls loose hair. The thin coat provides minimal cold-weather protection, so a jacket or sweater is needed for winter walks. Summer heat is generally well-tolerated.
Rat Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry or grooming mitt. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears biweekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Provide winter coat protection for cold weather outings.

American Hairless Terrier.brushingRoutine: No traditional brushing is needed as this breed is hairless (or has a very short, sparse coat in the coated variety). For hairless dogs, use a soft, damp cloth or gentle grooming wipe to clean the skin 2-3 times per week. For the rare coated variety, a soft bristle brush once weekly is sufficient.
American Hairless Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 1-2 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo formulated for sensitive skin. Hairless dogs accumulate skin oils, dirt, and environmental debris directly on their skin. Follow every bath with an unscented, hypoallergenic moisturizing lotion to prevent dry, flaky skin. Avoid harsh exfoliants or acne products not designed for dogs.
commonIssues: Sunburn on exposed skin requiring daily sunscreen application
Acne and blackheads from oil buildup
Dry, cracked skin without proper moisturizing
Skin cuts and scrapes from lack of protective coat
Sensitivity to extreme temperatures, both hot and cold
American Hairless Terrier.extendResults: Apply pet-safe sunscreen (SPF 30+) before any outdoor exposure, reapplying every 2 hours. Moisturize the skin daily with a gentle, fragrance-free lotion designed for dogs. Wipe the skin with gentle grooming wipes between baths to control oil buildup. Clothing provides both sun protection and warmth depending on season.
American Hairless Terrier.seasonalCare: Summer care is critical due to sunburn risk. Always apply sunscreen and consider UV-protective clothing for extended outdoor time. The skin tans and can darken with sun exposure. Winter requires insulating clothing for all outdoor activities as they have zero protection from cold. Spring and fall are the most comfortable seasons for this breed outdoors.
American Hairless Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Wipe skin with gentle cloth or wipes 2-3 times weekly. Bathe every 1-2 weeks with moisturizing shampoo. Apply moisturizer daily. Apply sunscreen before every outdoor exposure. Provide clothing for sun and cold protection. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Monitor skin for acne, dryness, or irritation weekly.

Toy Fox Terrier.brushingRoutine: Use a soft bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt 1-2 times per week to remove loose hair and distribute natural oils. Their short, smooth coat is low-maintenance but benefits from regular attention to keep it sleek and shiny. Focus on the chest and belly where the coat is finest.
Toy Fox Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a mild, moisturizing shampoo formulated for short-coated breeds. Avoid over-bathing as their skin can dry out quickly. A quick wipe-down with a damp cloth or grooming wipe between baths keeps them fresh.
commonIssues: Dry, flaky skin from over-bathing
Tear staining around the eyes
Patellar luxation affecting gait and grooming comfort
Thin coat providing little cold-weather protection
Ear wax buildup in upright ears
Toy Fox Terrier.extendResults: Apply a light coat conditioner spray after grooming to maintain shine for 2-3 weeks. Keep nails trimmed every 2 weeks since their small paws are sensitive to overgrowth. Use a microfiber cloth to wipe down the coat daily to remove dust and dander.
Toy Fox Terrier.seasonalCare: Toy Fox Terriers shed lightly year-round with a slight increase in spring and fall. In winter, they need sweaters or coats for outdoor walks due to their thin coat. In Houston summers, avoid midday walks and keep them cool -- their small size makes them vulnerable to heat.
Toy Fox Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with a soft bristle brush, nail trims every 2 weeks, ear checks weekly, teeth brushing 3-4 times per week, and professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for a bath, nail trim, and sanitary clip.

Jack Russell Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times per week with a firm bristle brush for smooth coats or a slicker brush for rough/broken coats. For rough-coated Jacks, hand-stripping every 8-12 weeks preserves the wiry texture. A stripping knife or pumice stone works well on the harsh outer coat.
Jack Russell Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks using a deodorizing shampoo since these active dogs tend to get dirty quickly. For rough coats, use a texturizing shampoo to maintain the wiry feel. Rinse thoroughly to prevent residue buildup in the dense undercoat.
commonIssues: Heavy shedding despite short coat length
Skin irritation from outdoor allergens and grass
Rough coat matting if not hand-stripped regularly
Muddy paws and belly from high-energy outdoor play
Ear infections from moisture after swimming or play
Jack Russell Terrier.extendResults: Use a rubber curry comb between professional grooming sessions to pull loose undercoat hair and reduce shedding. Wipe paws and belly after every outdoor session. For rough-coated Jacks, avoid clipping -- hand-stripping maintains proper coat texture and weather resistance.
Jack Russell Terrier.seasonalCare: Jack Russells blow their undercoat heavily in spring and fall, requiring daily brushing during these periods. In Houston summers, keep them hydrated during outdoor play and rinse off after swimming to prevent hot spots. Winter care is minimal as their double coat provides good insulation.
Jack Russell Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, paw wipes daily, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, hand-stripping (rough coats) every 8-12 weeks, and professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Parson Russell Terrier.brushingRoutine: Use a slicker brush and stripping knife 2-3 times per week for the wiry double coat. Hand-stripping every 8-10 weeks is essential to maintain the correct harsh texture. A fine-toothed comb helps work through the furnishings on the face and legs.
Parson Russell Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-6 weeks with a texturizing or whitening shampoo (most Parsons have predominantly white coats). Avoid cream rinses or heavy conditioners that soften the wire coat. Use a natural bristle dryer or air-dry to preserve coat texture.
commonIssues: Loss of wire coat texture from clipping instead of stripping
Skin allergies causing hot spots and itching
White coat staining on legs and face
Dense undercoat trapping moisture and causing odor
Tear staining on the predominantly white face
Parson Russell Terrier.extendResults: Maintain hand-stripped coat with weekly stripping knife touch-ups on the shoulders and back. Apply coat oil sparingly to preserve the wiry texture between grooms. Use a whitening spray on stained areas as needed, focusing on the chest and muzzle.
Parson Russell Terrier.seasonalCare: Parsons shed moderately year-round with heavier shedding in spring and fall when the undercoat turns over. In Houston's humid summers, check for hot spots weekly since the dense undercoat traps moisture. Keep the belly and sanitary areas trimmed shorter in summer for comfort.
Parson Russell Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with slicker brush, hand-strip every 8-10 weeks, weekly ear cleaning, nail trims every 3 weeks, teeth brushing 3 times per week, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Border Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a slicker brush and follow with a fine comb through the dense, wiry double coat. Hand-stripping twice a year (spring and fall) is essential to maintain the correct hard outer coat texture. Between stripping sessions, use a stripping knife weekly on areas that grow fastest like the neck and shoulders.
Border Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe only every 6-8 weeks since the Border Terrier's harsh coat naturally repels dirt. Use a mild, pH-balanced shampoo -- avoid softening conditioners that compromise the wire texture. Their coat should feel rough and slightly oily after bathing, not soft and fluffy.
commonIssues: Coat softening and color change if clipped instead of stripped
Undercoat impaction if not properly stripped
Skin allergies common in the breed
Ear infections from narrow ear canals
Dental issues requiring regular attention
Border Terrier.extendResults: Roll the coat weekly with a stripping knife to keep dead hair pulled and the coat looking tidy between professional strips. Avoid all clipping -- once clipped, the wire coat may never return to proper texture. Wipe the beard after meals to prevent staining and odor.
Border Terrier.seasonalCare: Border Terriers blow their undercoat in spring and fall, which is the ideal time for full hand-stripping. In Houston summers, keep the coat stripped short but never shave it -- the wire coat provides sun protection. In winter, the dense undercoat provides excellent insulation.
Border Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly slicker brush and comb session, daily beard wipes, weekly ear cleaning, nail trims every 3 weeks, full hand-strip every 4-6 months, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Norfolk Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush followed by a metal comb to work through the wiry, close-lying coat. Hand-strip every 8-12 weeks to maintain the hard topcoat texture. Use a stripping knife to pull dead coat from the neck, shoulders, and back where it grows thickest.
Norfolk Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a texturizing shampoo designed for wire coats. The Norfolk's coat has natural dirt-resistant properties, so over-bathing strips beneficial oils. Towel dry and let the coat air-dry to maintain its natural rough texture.
commonIssues: Matting in the longer furnishings on legs and belly
Soft, cottony coat development if clipped instead of stripped
Ear hair buildup blocking airflow in drop ears
Tear staining on lighter-colored faces
Skin irritation from environmental allergens
Norfolk Terrier.extendResults: Comb through leg furnishings daily to prevent mats from forming. Pluck excess ear hair monthly to maintain airflow. Between professional strips, roll the coat weekly with your fingers or a stripping stone to pull loose dead hair before it mats into the undercoat.
Norfolk Terrier.seasonalCare: Norfolk Terriers shed minimally but blow their undercoat in spring and fall, requiring more frequent stripping during transitions. In Houston's humid summers, keep the belly trimmed shorter and check ears weekly for moisture-related infections. Their wiry coat actually insulates against heat.
Norfolk Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily leg furnishing combing, ear hair plucking monthly, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, hand-strip every 8-12 weeks, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Manchester Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush once weekly with a rubber curry comb or hound glove to remove loose hair and stimulate the skin. Their tight, glossy coat is one of the easiest to maintain. A chamois cloth rubbed over the coat after brushing adds a brilliant shine.
Manchester Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. Manchester Terriers have sensitive skin, so avoid harsh formulas. A light leave-in conditioner spray helps maintain the coat's natural gloss between baths.
commonIssues: Dry skin and flaking in low-humidity environments
Thin coat providing minimal protection from sun and cold
Skin sensitivities and allergic reactions
Nail quicks that grow long if not trimmed regularly
Heat sensitivity despite dark coat color
Manchester Terrier.extendResults: Use a grooming mitt daily to maintain the sleek, polished appearance. Apply a light coat oil or shine spray weekly to keep the coat glossy. Keep nails trimmed very short since Manchester Terriers have hare-like feet where long nails affect gait.
Manchester Terrier.seasonalCare: Light, consistent shedding year-round with minimal seasonal variation. In Houston summers, their black and tan coat absorbs heat -- walk early morning or evening and apply pet-safe sunscreen on thin-coated areas. In winter, they need a coat or sweater for outdoor time.
Manchester Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly rubber curry brush session, daily grooming mitt wipe, nail trims every 2 weeks, ear checks weekly, teeth brushing 3 times weekly, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Smooth Fox Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a firm bristle brush or rubber curry comb. Their dense, flat-lying smooth coat sheds more than expected for its length. Use a deshedding tool like a Furminator during heavy shedding periods to remove loose undercoat.
Smooth Fox Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a whitening shampoo for the predominantly white coat. Use a deodorizing shampoo if the dog is particularly active outdoors. Rinse thoroughly since residue on the white coat causes yellowing and irritation.
commonIssues: Heavier shedding than expected from a smooth coat
White coat staining especially on legs and chest
Skin allergies and contact dermatitis
Tear staining around the eyes
Dry, itchy skin in winter months
Smooth Fox Terrier.extendResults: Use a rubber grooming mitt between brushings to capture loose hair before it hits furniture. Apply a whitening spray to stained areas between baths. Wipe down with a damp microfiber cloth after outdoor play to prevent dirt from setting into the white coat.
Smooth Fox Terrier.seasonalCare: Smooth Fox Terriers shed moderately year-round with heavier blows in spring and fall. In Houston's heat, their white coat reflects sunlight but thin-coated belly areas can sunburn. During spring allergy season, wipe paws and belly after outdoor time to reduce allergen contact.
Smooth Fox Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly, daily wipe-downs for white coat maintenance, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, teeth brushing 3 times weekly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Greyhound.brushingRoutine: Brush once weekly with a soft rubber curry comb or hound glove. Their ultra-short, fine coat requires minimal brushing but benefits from regular skin stimulation. A chamois cloth after brushing produces a beautiful shine on their sleek coat.
Greyhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks or as needed with a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo. Greyhounds have thin, sensitive skin that dries out easily -- use an oatmeal-based or aloe shampoo. Apply a light moisturizing conditioner since their skin lacks the oil production of thicker-coated breeds.
commonIssues: Thin skin that tears and scrapes easily
Pressure sores on bony prominences from lying on hard surfaces
Cold sensitivity requiring indoor warmth and clothing
Dental disease common in the breed
Tail tip injuries that bleed heavily
Greyhound.extendResults: Apply a light skin moisturizer or coconut oil weekly to prevent dry, flaky skin. Provide soft bedding to prevent pressure calluses on elbows and hocks. Use a snood during meals to keep ears clean if they drag in food bowls.
Greyhound.seasonalCare: Greyhounds shed lightly year-round with minimal seasonal variation. In Houston summers, their thin coat and lean body make them heat-sensitive -- limit outdoor time in peak heat. In winter, they absolutely need coats and sweaters since they have almost no body fat or undercoat for insulation.
Greyhound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly rubber curry brush, daily skin checks for scrapes, moisturizer application 1-2 times weekly, nail trims every 2-3 weeks, dental care 4-5 times weekly, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Scottish Deerhound.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and wide-toothed comb to work through the 3-4 inch wiry coat. Hand-strip loose dead coat every few months rather than clipping to maintain the correct harsh texture. Pay special attention to the beard, eyebrows, and leg furnishings where tangles form.
Scottish Deerhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a texturizing shampoo for wire coats. Their rough coat naturally repels dirt and mud, so baths are needed less often than you might think. Towel dry and air-dry to preserve the natural crisp texture -- blow-drying softens the coat.
commonIssues: Beard and mustache matting from food and water
Leg furnishing tangles between grooming sessions
Large size makes home grooming challenging
Bloat risk affecting post-groom comfort
Joint issues in older dogs requiring gentle handling during grooming
Scottish Deerhound.extendResults: Comb through the beard daily after meals and water to prevent matting and odor. Keep sanitary areas trimmed for hygiene. Pull loose dead coat by hand between professional grooming sessions to maintain the tidy, natural Deerhound silhouette.
Scottish Deerhound.seasonalCare: Deerhounds shed moderately in spring and fall when the undercoat loosens. In Houston summers, the wiry coat actually provides insulation from heat -- never shave it. Trim the belly and inner thighs slightly shorter in summer for comfort. In winter, their coat provides good protection.
Scottish Deerhound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard maintenance, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3-4 weeks, hand-strip loose coat monthly, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Ibizan Hound.brushingRoutine: For smooth-coated Ibizans, brush weekly with a rubber curry comb or hound glove. For wire-coated Ibizans, use a slicker brush and stripping knife 2-3 times weekly. Both coat types are relatively low-maintenance but benefit from regular grooming to distribute oils.
Ibizan Hound.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, fragrance-free shampoo. Ibizans have sensitive skin, so hypoallergenic formulas work best. Their naturally clean coat rarely develops odor, so baths are more about skin health than cleanliness.
commonIssues: Thin skin that cuts and tears easily during play
Large, sensitive ears prone to cold and sunburn
Sensitivity to anesthesia affecting veterinary grooming procedures
Dry skin in low-humidity environments
Allergic reactions to flea bites and environmental allergens
Ibizan Hound.extendResults: Wipe down with a damp chamois cloth between baths to maintain the coat's clean appearance. Apply pet-safe sunscreen to ears and nose, especially the pink-skinned areas. Keep nails short since their hare feet are sensitive to overgrown nails.
Ibizan Hound.seasonalCare: Ibizans shed lightly year-round with a slight increase in spring. In Houston summers, protect their large, thin ears from sun exposure and provide shade. Their lean build and thin coat make them heat-sensitive despite their Mediterranean origins. In winter, they need a coat for outdoor time.
Ibizan Hound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing (smooth) or 2-3 times weekly (wire), ear checks twice weekly, nail trims every 2-3 weeks, sunscreen application as needed, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Petit Basset Griffon Vendeen.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and wide-toothed comb to work through the rough, tousled double coat. The PBGV's coat should look naturally unkempt -- never over-groom into a polished look. Use a mat splitter on any tangles that form behind the ears and in the leg furnishings.
Petit Basset Griffon Vendeen.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that preserves the coat's rough, casual texture. Avoid heavy conditioners that weigh down the coat and make it look flat. The PBGV should look like they just had a romp through the French countryside, not a day at the salon.
commonIssues: Ear infections due to long, pendulous ears trapping moisture
Matting in the dense undercoat especially behind ears and in armpits
Beard and mustache staining and odor from food
Debris collection in the rough coat after outdoor activity
Eye irritation from long eyebrow hair
Petit Basset Griffon Vendeen.extendResults: Clean ears twice weekly with a drying ear solution to prevent infections in those long, heavy ear flaps. Comb the beard after every meal. Keep the hair between foot pads trimmed to prevent slipping and debris accumulation. Maintain the casual, tousled look by avoiding over-brushing.
Petit Basset Griffon Vendeen.seasonalCare: PBGVs shed moderately in spring and fall as the undercoat turns over. In Houston's humid climate, their long ears are especially prone to yeast infections -- increase ear cleaning to every other day in summer. The rough coat provides some heat insulation but keep outdoor time limited in extreme heat.
Petit Basset Griffon Vendeen.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard cleaning, ear cleaning twice weekly (more in summer), nail trims every 3 weeks, foot pad hair trimming bi-weekly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Otterhound.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a large slicker brush and wide-toothed comb to work through the dense, rough, waterproof double coat. The outer coat is 2-4 inches long and naturally oily -- use a pin brush on the longer sections. Never strip or clip the coat flat; the Otterhound should retain its shaggy, rustic appearance.
Otterhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a deodorizing shampoo since the oily coat can develop a distinctive hound odor. Use lukewarm water and work the shampoo deep into the dense undercoat. A second lather is usually needed. Blow-dry on a low, cool setting to prevent matting while drying.
commonIssues: Strong hound odor from the naturally oily, waterproof coat
Matting in the dense undercoat if not brushed regularly
Beard dripping and staining after drinking
Ear infections from long, folded ears trapping moisture
Webbed feet collecting debris between toes
Otterhound.extendResults: Use a waterless deodorizing spray between baths to manage the natural hound scent. Clean the beard after every meal and water session. Check and clean between the webbed toes after outdoor activity. Keep ear flaps lifted and aired out daily to prevent moisture buildup.
Otterhound.seasonalCare: Otterhounds shed heavily in spring as they lose the dense winter undercoat. In Houston's humid summers, the oily coat can intensify odor -- increase bathing frequency to every 3-4 weeks. Their love of water means extra attention to ear drying after any swim or play in humidity.
Otterhound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard cleaning, ear cleaning twice weekly, between-toe checks after walks, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks including deodorizing treatment.

Black and Tan Coonhound.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a rubber curry comb or hound mitt to remove loose hair and distribute the natural oils that give the coat its distinctive sheen. A bristle brush works well for a finishing pass. During shedding season, increase to 3-4 times weekly with a deshedding tool.
Black and Tan Coonhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a deodorizing shampoo formulated for hound breeds -- they have a natural oily coat that produces a distinctive scent. Use a deep-cleaning shampoo on the long, velvety ears where oil and odor accumulate. Always dry ears thoroughly after bathing.
commonIssues: Strong hound odor from oily skin
Chronic ear infections in the long, pendulous ears
Drool and jowl staining
Heavy shedding during seasonal coat blows
Skin fold irritation around the neck and jowls
Black and Tan Coonhound.extendResults: Clean ears every 3 days with a drying ear solution to prevent the infections these long-eared hounds are prone to. Wipe facial folds and jowls daily. Use a deodorizing spray between baths to manage the natural hound scent. Feed from elevated bowls to keep ears out of food.
Black and Tan Coonhound.seasonalCare: Black and Tan Coonhounds shed moderately year-round with heavy blows in spring and fall. In Houston summers, their dark coat absorbs significant heat -- exercise only in early morning or evening. The oily coat intensifies in humidity, so increase bathing frequency in summer months.
Black and Tan Coonhound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly (more during shedding), ear cleaning every 3 days, jowl wiping daily, nail trims every 3 weeks, teeth brushing 2-3 times weekly, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks with ear treatment.

Treeing Walker Coonhound.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry comb or bristle brush to remove loose hair from the short, dense coat. A hound glove is excellent for daily quick grooming that also serves as bonding time. Use a deshedding tool during spring and fall coat transitions.
Treeing Walker Coonhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a deodorizing, deep-cleaning shampoo to address the natural hound odor. Pay special attention to the long ears -- wash them separately with a gentle cleanser. Rinse completely; residue on the tricolor coat causes dullness.
commonIssues: Hound odor that intensifies in warm, humid weather
Ear infections from long, folded ears with poor air circulation
Scratches and scrapes from outdoor activity
Shedding that is more significant than the short coat suggests
Toe nail splits from high-energy running on hard surfaces
Treeing Walker Coonhound.extendResults: Use a grooming mitt daily to control shedding and keep the coat gleaming. Apply ear cleaning solution every 3-4 days, especially after outdoor activity. Use a deodorizing coat spray between baths. Check paw pads weekly for cracks from their active lifestyle.
Treeing Walker Coonhound.seasonalCare: Treeing Walkers shed moderately year-round with heavier seasonal shedding in spring and fall. In Houston's heat, their high energy level makes overheating a concern -- ensure access to shade and water. Keep ears especially dry in humid months to prevent yeast infections.
Treeing Walker Coonhound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with curry comb, daily grooming mitt wipe, ear cleaning every 3-4 days, nail trims every 3 weeks, paw pad checks weekly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Redbone Coonhound.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a rubber curry comb to remove loose hair and bring out the rich, mahogany-red coat's natural luster. A boar bristle brush provides an excellent finishing pass. The coat is short but dense, so a deshedding tool helps during seasonal changes.
Redbone Coonhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a color-enhancing red/mahogany shampoo to maintain the breed's stunning coat color. A deodorizing formula helps with the natural hound scent. The ears need individual attention -- clean inside the ear flaps during every bath.
commonIssues: Strong hound odor, especially in warm weather
Ear infections from the long, low-set ear flaps
Drooling causing chin and chest staining
Red coat fading from sun exposure
Oily skin that attracts dirt and debris
Redbone Coonhound.extendResults: Use a coat-enhancing spray with UV protection to prevent the beautiful red coat from sun-fading. Clean ears every 3-4 days with an enzymatic ear cleaner. Wipe drool from the chin and chest daily. A weekly rub-down with a chamois cloth maintains the coat's distinctive glow.
Redbone Coonhound.seasonalCare: Redbones shed moderately year-round with heavier shedding in spring and fall. In Houston's intense sun, the red coat can fade -- limit prolonged sun exposure and use UV-protective sprays. In humid months, increase ear cleaning frequency and watch for hot spots on the oily skin.
Redbone Coonhound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly, daily drool wiping, ear cleaning every 3-4 days, nail trims every 3 weeks, UV protection spray as needed, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks with color-enhancing treatment.

Bluetick Coonhound.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a firm bristle brush or rubber curry comb to manage the short, dense, glossy coat. The ticked blue pattern looks best when regularly brushed to distribute oils evenly. Use a deshedding tool during seasonal coat transitions to control the surprisingly heavy shedding.
Bluetick Coonhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-5 weeks with a deodorizing hound shampoo -- Blueticks have one of the stronger hound odors. Double-lather if they have been hunting or in dense brush. Clean the pendulous ears separately with a medicated ear wash during every bath.
commonIssues: Pronounced hound odor requiring more frequent bathing than most short-coated breeds
Chronic ear infections in the long, thin ear leather
Heavy drooling causing skin fold irritation
Skin allergies and hot spots in humid climates
Coat thinning on elbows from lying on hard surfaces
Bluetick Coonhound.extendResults: Apply deodorizing spray every 2-3 days between baths to manage the hound scent. Use an enzymatic ear cleaner every 3 days -- Bluetick ears are among the most infection-prone of all breeds. Provide soft bedding to prevent callus formation on elbows. Wipe facial folds daily.
Bluetick Coonhound.seasonalCare: Blueticks shed moderately year-round with significant blows in spring and fall. Houston's humidity intensifies their natural odor -- plan on bathing every 3 weeks in summer. Keep ears meticulously dry in humid months. The dense coat provides some heat protection but ensure shade access.
Bluetick Coonhound.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly, ear cleaning every 3 days, daily facial fold wiping, deodorizing spray every 2-3 days, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 3-5 weeks.

Plott Hound.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry comb or firm bristle brush. The Plott's fine, glossy coat is shorter and smoother than other coonhound breeds, making it one of the easier hound coats to maintain. A grooming mitt used daily provides adequate maintenance between brushings.
Plott Hound.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-6 weeks with a mild, moisturizing shampoo. Plotts have less hound odor than other coonhound breeds thanks to their finer coat, so they can go longer between baths. Use a gentle ear cleanser during baths to address their moderate ear infection risk.
commonIssues: Less odor than other coonhounds but still present in humid conditions
Ear infections from folded ears, though less severe than longer-eared hounds
Scratches and scrapes from brush and bramble during outdoor activity
Shedding that increases significantly in spring and fall
Dry skin in winter from indoor heating
Plott Hound.extendResults: A daily wipe with a grooming mitt keeps the fine coat sleek and reduces shedding onto furniture. Apply a light coat conditioner spray weekly to maintain sheen. Check and clean ears weekly -- less intensive than other coonhounds but still important. Moisturize skin in dry months.
Plott Hound.seasonalCare: Plotts shed moderately year-round with heavier shedding in spring and fall transitions. In Houston summers, their brindle coat absorbs heat -- schedule outdoor time for cooler hours. The fine coat offers less insulation than other hounds, so they may appreciate a light coat in rare Houston cold snaps.
Plott Hound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly bristle brush session, daily grooming mitt wipe, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, coat conditioner spray weekly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Xoloitzcuintli.brushingRoutine: For hairless varieties, no brushing is needed -- instead, use a soft washcloth or gentle exfoliating mitt 2-3 times weekly to remove dead skin cells and prevent blackheads. For the coated variety, brush weekly with a soft bristle brush. The hairless Xolo's skin is their coat, and it requires more attention than fur.
Xoloitzcuintli.bathingTips: Bathe hairless Xolos every 1-2 weeks with a gentle, non-comedogenic body wash or mild dog shampoo. Avoid over-bathing which strips natural oils and causes overproduction of sebum. Follow every bath with a lightweight, fragrance-free moisturizer. Coated Xolos bathe every 4-6 weeks with standard dog shampoo.
commonIssues: Acne and blackheads on hairless skin
Sunburn on exposed skin requiring daily sun protection
Dry, flaky skin if not properly moisturized
Skin sensitivities and allergic reactions to products
Temperature sensitivity in both heat and cold
Xoloitzcuintli.extendResults: Apply pet-safe sunscreen (SPF 30+) before any outdoor exposure for hairless varieties. Moisturize daily with a lightweight, non-comedogenic lotion. Exfoliate gently 2-3 times weekly to prevent clogged pores. Use a humidifier indoors during dry months. The skin should feel smooth and supple, not oily or flaky.
Xoloitzcuintli.seasonalCare: Hairless Xolos need sun protection year-round in Houston -- their exposed skin burns easily. In summer, apply sunscreen every 2 hours during outdoor time and provide shade. In winter, they need sweaters and coats since they have no insulation. Skin tends to dry out more in winter, requiring heavier moisturizer.
Xoloitzcuintli.maintenanceSchedule: Exfoliation 2-3 times weekly, daily moisturizer application, sunscreen before outdoor time, bath every 1-2 weeks (hairless) or 4-6 weeks (coated), weekly skin inspection for acne or irritation, professional grooming/skin treatment every 4-6 weeks.

Peruvian Inca Orchid.brushingRoutine: For hairless varieties, skip brushing entirely and focus on skin care -- use a soft, damp cloth or gentle exfoliating pad 2-3 times weekly. For coated varieties, brush weekly with a soft bristle brush. Some hairless PIOs have tufts of hair on the head, feet, and tail that need gentle combing with a fine-toothed comb.
Peruvian Inca Orchid.bathingTips: Bathe hairless PIOs every 1-2 weeks with a mild, hypoallergenic, fragrance-free cleanser. Their sensitive skin reacts to harsh chemicals, so choose products carefully. Follow with an unscented, lightweight moisturizer. Coated varieties bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle dog shampoo.
commonIssues: Severe sunburn on unpigmented skin areas
Acne, blackheads, and clogged pores on hairless skin
Skin lesions and dry patches
Dental issues including missing premolars common in hairless varieties
Extreme temperature sensitivity requiring climate management
Peruvian Inca Orchid.extendResults: Apply pet-safe, mineral-based sunscreen daily on exposed skin. Moisturize morning and evening with a lightweight, non-comedogenic lotion. Keep the skin clean but not over-washed. For PIOs with skin tufts, comb gently to prevent tangles. Monitor for any new skin growths or changes at every grooming session.
Peruvian Inca Orchid.seasonalCare: Houston summers are high-risk for hairless PIOs -- they need sunscreen, shade, and limited direct sun exposure. Schedule outdoor time for early morning or evening only. In winter, they require protective clothing for any outdoor time. Their skin may need a richer moisturizer in winter months when indoor air is drier.
Peruvian Inca Orchid.maintenanceSchedule: Gentle exfoliation 2-3 times weekly, daily sunscreen and moisturizer, bath every 1-2 weeks (hairless) or 4-6 weeks (coated), weekly full skin inspection, tuft combing as needed, professional skin care treatment every 4-6 weeks.

Thai Ridgeback.brushingRoutine: Brush once weekly with a rubber curry comb or short-bristle brush. The Thai Ridgeback's short, smooth coat is extremely low-maintenance. A grooming mitt used a few times weekly keeps the coat sleek and removes loose hair. Pay attention to the distinctive ridge along the back, ensuring the hair there stays clean and flat.
Thai Ridgeback.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. Thai Ridgebacks are naturally clean dogs with minimal odor, so they need less bathing than most breeds. Their sensitive skin responds best to hypoallergenic, fragrance-free formulas. Rinse thoroughly to prevent any residue irritation.
commonIssues: Dermoid sinus along the ridge (congenital condition requiring veterinary attention)
Skin sensitivity to chemicals and fragrances
Minimal cold tolerance due to single coat with no undercoat
Thin skin that scrapes easily
Limited socialization tolerance making grooming stressful if not started early
Thai Ridgeback.extendResults: Inspect the ridge line at every grooming session for any signs of dermoid sinus (swelling, warmth, or pain). Use a microfiber cloth for quick daily wipe-downs. Apply a light coat conditioner spray to maintain sheen. Start grooming routines early and keep sessions positive since this breed can be resistant to handling.
Thai Ridgeback.seasonalCare: Thai Ridgebacks shed lightly year-round with minimal seasonal variation thanks to their single coat. In Houston summers, their short coat provides little sun protection -- apply sunscreen to thinly-coated areas. In winter cold snaps, they need a warm coat since they have zero undercoat insulation.
Thai Ridgeback.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly rubber curry brush, daily microfiber wipe, ridge inspection at every grooming, ear checks weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Azawakh.brushingRoutine: Brush once weekly with a soft rubber curry comb or hound glove. The Azawakh's ultra-short, fine coat is among the easiest to maintain but benefits from regular skin stimulation. A chamois cloth rubbed over the coat after brushing adds shine to their taut, close-fitting coat.
Azawakh.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. Their thin, fine coat and lean body mean they lose heat quickly during baths -- use warm water and dry immediately. A mild oatmeal shampoo works well for their sensitive skin. Avoid heavy products that weigh down the naturally sleek coat.
commonIssues: Extremely thin skin that tears and bruises easily
Sensitivity to cold requiring warm bathing and drying environments
Pressure sores on bony prominences from minimal body fat
Skin allergies and sensitivities to grooming products
Anxiety during grooming if not properly socialized
Azawakh.extendResults: Apply a light skin moisturizer or coconut oil weekly, especially during dry months. Provide thick, cushioned bedding to prevent pressure calluses on elbows and hips. Handle gently during grooming -- their lean build means there is minimal padding between skin and bone. Keep grooming sessions calm and efficient.
Azawakh.seasonalCare: Azawakhs shed minimally year-round. Despite their Saharan origins, they are sensitive to Houston's humid heat -- provide shade and limit exercise in peak temperatures. In winter, they absolutely need coats and sweaters since they have virtually no body fat or undercoat. Keep them warm during grooming.
Azawakh.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly soft rubber curry brush, daily chamois cloth wipe, skin moisturizer 1-2 times weekly, nail trims every 2-3 weeks, ear checks weekly, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Sloughi.brushingRoutine: Brush once weekly with a soft hound glove or rubber mitt. The Sloughi's short, fine, close-fitting coat requires minimal grooming but appreciates gentle, regular attention. A soft-bristle brush works well for removing the small amount of loose hair. Their coat should feel smooth and satiny to the touch.
Sloughi.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks or less frequently -- Sloughis are naturally clean dogs with very little odor. Use a mild, hypoallergenic shampoo and warm water. Their thin skin is sensitive to harsh chemicals, so choose products carefully. Dry thoroughly and quickly since their lean body chills easily.
commonIssues: Paper-thin skin that tears on branches, fences, or during play
Sensitivity to anesthesia and certain grooming products
Minimal body fat making them uncomfortable on hard grooming surfaces
Cold sensitivity requiring warm grooming environments
Sensitive temperament requiring calm, gentle handling
Sloughi.extendResults: Wipe down with a damp chamois cloth between baths to maintain the sleek, satin-like coat. Apply a light skin moisturizer in dry weather. Provide a padded surface for grooming since their bony frame is uncomfortable on hard tables. Keep grooming sessions short, positive, and calm.
Sloughi.seasonalCare: Sloughis shed minimally year-round with almost no seasonal variation. In Houston summers, their thin coat offers little sun protection -- limit direct sun exposure and provide shade. In winter, they need insulating coats for outdoor time. Their lean, thin-skinned build makes temperature regulation their primary grooming concern.
Sloughi.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly soft brush or hound glove, daily chamois wipe, skin check for cuts and tears at every session, nail trims every 2-3 weeks, ear checks weekly, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Cirneco dell'Etna.brushingRoutine: Brush once weekly with a soft bristle brush or rubber mitt. The Cirneco's short, glossy coat lies flat and close to the body, requiring minimal maintenance. A grooming mitt used 2-3 times weekly removes loose hair and stimulates the skin. The coat should gleam with health after a light brushing session.
Cirneco dell'Etna.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, gentle shampoo. Cirnecos are naturally clean dogs that rarely develop odor. Their coat repels dirt well, so spot cleaning with a damp cloth is often sufficient between baths. Use warm water and a light conditioner if the skin feels dry.
commonIssues: Thin skin that is prone to scrapes and cuts
Large, upright ears that are sensitive to cold and sun
Dry skin in low-humidity environments
Minimal cold tolerance with virtually no undercoat
Sensitivity to chemical-heavy grooming products
Cirneco dell'Etna.extendResults: Wipe down with a microfiber cloth after outdoor activity to remove dust and pollen. Apply pet-safe sunscreen to the large, exposed ear tips and nose. Keep nails short since their compact, hare-like feet are sensitive to overgrown nails. The coat should always look sleek and close-fitting.
Cirneco dell'Etna.seasonalCare: Cirnecos shed lightly year-round with minimal seasonal change. In Houston summers, protect their large ears from sunburn and provide shade. Their short coat offers some heat tolerance but limit extreme exertion. In winter, a light jacket is sufficient since Houston rarely gets cold enough to stress this Mediterranean breed.
Cirneco dell'Etna.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with soft bristle brush, grooming mitt 2-3 times weekly, ear tip sunscreen as needed, nail trims every 2-3 weeks, ear checks weekly, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Portuguese Podengo.brushingRoutine: For smooth-coated Podengos, brush weekly with a rubber curry comb or bristle brush. For wire-coated varieties, brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and hand-strip every 8-12 weeks to maintain the rough coat texture. Use a stripping knife on the wiry topcoat, focusing on the back and shoulders.
Portuguese Podengo.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with an appropriate shampoo -- mild moisturizing for smooth coats, texturizing for wire coats. Both coat types are naturally clean and dirt-resistant, so over-bathing is unnecessary. For wire coats, avoid conditioners that soften the harsh texture.
commonIssues: Wire coat losing texture if clipped instead of stripped
Ear infections in wire-coated varieties with more ear furnishing
Skin allergies from environmental triggers
Small size (Pequeno) making handling during grooming delicate
Active lifestyle leading to coat tangles in the wire variety
Portuguese Podengo.extendResults: For wire coats, roll the coat weekly with fingers or a stripping stone to maintain texture. Smooth coats benefit from a daily grooming mitt pass. Keep ear furnishings trimmed to allow airflow. This naturally clean breed requires less intervention than most -- focus on maintaining natural coat qualities.
Portuguese Podengo.seasonalCare: Podengos shed lightly to moderately depending on coat type, with wire coats shedding less. In Houston summers, their Mediterranean heritage provides good heat tolerance but provide shade during peak hours. Wire coats should never be shaved in summer. Both varieties handle winter well with minimal cold protection needed.
Portuguese Podengo.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing (smooth) or 2-3 times weekly (wire), hand-stripping every 8-12 weeks (wire), ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Lagotto Romagnolo.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a wide-toothed comb or specialized curly coat rake, working through the dense, woolly, curly coat in sections. Never use a slicker brush, which breaks the curls and creates frizz. Mist the coat with water or detangling spray before combing to prevent breakage. The coat should form tight, ring-shaped curls.
Lagotto Romagnolo.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing, curl-enhancing shampoo. Apply a rich conditioner and leave it in for 5 minutes to hydrate the woolly curls. Scrunch the coat with a towel rather than rubbing, which disrupts curl pattern. Diffuse-dry on low heat or air-dry for the best curl definition.
commonIssues: Matting deep in the woolly undercoat if not combed regularly
Ear hair growth blocking ear canals and trapping moisture
Coat becoming too long and heavy between clips
Loss of curl definition from improper brushing or products
Eye staining hidden under curly facial hair
Lagotto Romagnolo.extendResults: Trim the coat every 2-3 months to maintain a workable length of 1-2 inches -- longer coat mats more readily. Pluck ear hair monthly to maintain airflow. Keep facial hair trimmed to allow visibility and prevent eye irritation. Apply a leave-in curl cream after bathing to define and protect the curls.
Lagotto Romagnolo.seasonalCare: Lagottos do not shed seasonally since their coat grows continuously like human hair. In Houston's humid summers, the dense woolly coat can trap heat -- keep it clipped shorter (about 1 inch). In winter, the coat can be kept slightly longer for warmth. Humidity actually helps their curls maintain definition.
Lagotto Romagnolo.maintenanceSchedule: Comb 2-3 times weekly with wide-toothed comb, ear hair plucking monthly, facial hair trimming as needed, full body clip every 2-3 months, bath every 3-4 weeks, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Spanish Water Dog.brushingRoutine: Do NOT brush the Spanish Water Dog's coat -- this is one of the few breeds where brushing is prohibited. Their coat forms natural cords or curls that should never be brushed out. Instead, separate any clumping cords by hand every few days. Use your fingers to feel for mats near the skin and gently pull them apart.
Spanish Water Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks by soaking the coat thoroughly and applying a moisturizing shampoo, squeezing it through the curls rather than rubbing. Rinse completely -- trapped shampoo causes skin irritation under the cords. Squeeze water out with towels and air-dry only. Never blow-dry, which destroys the cord formation.
commonIssues: Matting at the skin level hidden beneath the cords
Trapped moisture and mildew in dense cords if not dried properly
Ear infections from excess ear hair and moisture
Skin issues invisible beneath the thick corded coat
Coat becoming too heavy and pulling on the skin if not clipped regularly
Spanish Water Dog.extendResults: Clip the entire coat to 1-2 inches every 3-4 months to reset the cord formation and prevent excessive weight. Check the skin under the cords weekly by parting them with your fingers. Keep ears clean and pluck excess hair from ear canals. Ensure the coat dries completely after baths or swimming -- trapped moisture is the biggest risk.
Spanish Water Dog.seasonalCare: Spanish Water Dogs do not shed in the traditional sense. In Houston's humid climate, the corded coat is at high risk for mildew and musty odor -- ensure complete drying after any water exposure. In summer, keep the coat clipped shorter to prevent overheating. The coat provides sun protection, so never shave to the skin.
Spanish Water Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Hand-separate cords every few days (NO brushing), bath every 4-6 weeks with complete drying, ear cleaning weekly with hair plucking monthly, full body clip every 3-4 months, skin checks under cords weekly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Barbet.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a wide-toothed comb or long-pin slicker brush, working through the thick, curly, woolly coat section by section.
