Always mist with water before combing to prevent curl breakage. The coat mats quickly, especially behind the ears, in the armpits, and on the belly. A dematting comb helps with any tangles found.
Barbet.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a hydrating, curl-defining shampoo followed by a rich conditioner. The Barbet's coat absorbs water like a sponge, so be prepared for extended drying time. Squeeze (never rub) water out with towels, then diffuse-dry on low heat or air-dry. The coat should form defined, loose curls.
commonIssues: Rapid matting in the dense, woolly coat -- can mat overnight if damp
Ear infections from heavy ear furnishings trapping moisture
Debris and burrs embedding in the curly coat during outdoor activity
Coat odor from the woolly texture retaining moisture and oils
Eye irritation from facial curls growing into the eyes
Barbet.extendResults: Comb through the entire coat at least every other day during humid months. Trim facial hair to keep eyes clear. Pluck ear hair monthly and clean ears twice weekly. After any water activity, dry the coat completely -- a damp Barbet coat will mat within hours. Keep the coat at 3-4 inches maximum between clips.
Barbet.seasonalCare: Barbets do not shed seasonally since their coat grows continuously. In Houston's humid summers, the woolly coat retains moisture and can develop odor -- clip shorter and increase bathing frequency. The coat actually provides insulation from heat, so maintain at least 1 inch. In winter, the coat can be kept longer for warmth.
Barbet.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly minimum, ear cleaning twice weekly, ear hair plucking monthly, facial trimming as needed, full clip every 2-3 months, bath every 2-3 weeks, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks.

Boykin Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and medium-toothed comb to maintain the moderately curly to wavy coat. Focus on the longer feathering on the ears, chest, belly, and legs where mats develop. A pin brush works well on the body coat, while a fine comb detangles the ear feathering.
Boykin Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and conditioner, especially if the dog is active in water. Boykins are water dogs, so frequent swimming means more frequent bathing to remove lake or pool chemicals. Use an ear drying solution after every water exposure to prevent infections.
commonIssues: Ear infections from heavy, feathered ear flaps -- one of the most ear-infection-prone breeds
Matting in feathering on ears, belly, and legs
Hot spots from moisture trapped in the curly coat
Skin allergies and exercise-induced collapse (EIC) affecting grooming tolerance
Coat collecting burrs, sticks, and debris during fieldwork
Boykin Spaniel.extendResults: Dry ears thoroughly after every water exposure -- keep a drying ear solution on hand at all times. Comb ear feathering daily to prevent mats. Trim foot pads and sanitary areas between professional grooming sessions. Apply a detangling spray before brushing to protect the curly coat from breakage.
Boykin Spaniel.seasonalCare: Boykins shed moderately year-round with increased shedding in spring. In Houston's hot summers, keep the coat trimmed to a manageable length and dry thoroughly after swimming. Their rich brown coat provides some sun protection. In winter, the coat can be kept slightly fuller for warmth during waterfowl season.
Boykin Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily ear feathering combing, ear cleaning after every water exposure plus weekly minimum, foot pad trimming bi-weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks.

Sussex Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and wide-toothed comb to maintain the flat to slightly wavy, abundant golden-liver coat. Pay special attention to the heavy feathering on the ears, chest, legs, and belly. A mat splitter helps with tangles that form in the dense undercoat. Brush in the direction of coat growth.
Sussex Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo and conditioner to maintain the rich golden-liver coat color. A color-enhancing formula designed for liver/brown coats helps preserve the breed's distinctive shade. Clean the long, heavy ears separately during every bath with a gentle ear wash.
commonIssues: Ear infections from the heaviest, longest ears in the spaniel family
Significant matting in the abundant body and ear feathering
Interdigital cysts between the toes
Coat weight causing skin pulling if not properly maintained
Drooling causing chest and beard staining
Sussex Spaniel.extendResults: Clean and dry ears every 2-3 days -- Sussex ears are among the most infection-prone in dogdom. Comb through feathering daily, especially on the ears. Trim excess hair between the toes to prevent interdigital irritation. Use a conditioning spray on the coat between grooming sessions to prevent dryness and tangling.
Sussex Spaniel.seasonalCare: Sussex Spaniels shed moderately year-round with heavier shedding in spring when the undercoat loosens. In Houston's humid summers, the heavy coat and low-slung body are prone to hot spots -- keep the belly trimmed shorter and check for moisture-related skin issues. Their low energy helps prevent overheating.
Sussex Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily ear feathering combing, ear cleaning every 2-3 days, between-toe checks weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, sanitary and belly trims as needed, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks.

Irish Water Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a wide-toothed comb and long-pin slicker brush to work through the dense, tightly curled, waterproof coat. Always mist the coat with water or detangling spray before combing -- dry brushing breaks the curls. The face and tail (rat tail) are naturally smooth and need only wiping. Focus on the body, legs, and topknot.
Irish Water Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a moisturizing, curl-enhancing shampoo followed by conditioner. The dense, curly coat takes a long time to saturate and an equally long time to dry. Squeeze water out with towels, then diffuse-dry or air-dry. The coat should form tight, defined ringlets when properly maintained.
commonIssues: Dense coat matting deeply if not combed regularly, especially behind ears and in armpits
Ear infections from the curly ear flaps trapping moisture
Slow drying time leading to trapped moisture and potential skin infections
Coat losing curl definition from improper brushing technique
Topknot tangles obscuring vision
Irish Water Spaniel.extendResults: Ensure the coat dries completely after every bath and swim -- trapped moisture in the dense curls causes skin infections and mildew. Trim the topknot to keep eyes clear. Keep the rat tail clean and free of debris. Apply a leave-in conditioner to maintain curl definition. Clip the body coat every 6-8 weeks to a manageable length.
Irish Water Spaniel.seasonalCare: Irish Water Spaniels do not shed seasonally, as their coat grows continuously. In Houston's humid summers, the dense curly coat retains heat -- keep it clipped to 1-2 inches for comfort. The waterproof coat handles rain and swimming well but must be dried thoroughly afterward. In winter, the coat provides excellent insulation at full length.
Irish Water Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Comb 2-3 times weekly, topknot management daily, ear cleaning after water exposure plus weekly, full body clip every 6-8 weeks, bath every 2-3 weeks, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks.

Curly-Coated Retriever.brushingRoutine: Do NOT brush the Curly-Coated Retriever's coat with conventional brushes -- this destroys the tight, crisp curls. Instead, use your fingers or a wide-toothed comb to gently separate any tangles. After bathing, scrunch the coat with your hands to encourage curl formation. A light misting with water and hand-scrunching between baths refreshes the curls.
Curly-Coated Retriever.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a curl-enhancing or lightweight moisturizing shampoo. Apply conditioner only to the ears and furnishings, not the body curls -- conditioner weighs them down and loosens the tight pattern. Towel-dry by scrunching, then air-dry completely. Blow-drying frizzes the coat and ruins curl definition.
commonIssues: Loss of curl pattern from brushing, blow-drying, or heavy products
Patchiness and bald spots (pattern baldness is a breed issue)
Frizzy coat from humidity or improper drying
Ear infections from curly ear flaps trapping moisture
Slow drying time due to dense, water-resistant curls
Curly-Coated Retriever.extendResults: Mist the coat lightly and scrunch with hands to refresh curls between baths. Apply a light curl-defining spray after bathing. Do not over-groom -- the Curly-Coat should look naturally tidy with minimal fuss. Monitor for any pattern baldness on the thighs and flanks and consult your vet if it appears.
Curly-Coated Retriever.seasonalCare: Curly-Coated Retrievers shed minimally year-round with a more noticeable shed in spring and fall. In Houston's humidity, the curls may frizz -- a light anti-humidity spray helps. In summer, the tight curls provide sun protection but ensure the dog has access to shade and water. Never shave the coat as curls may not grow back correctly.
Curly-Coated Retriever.maintenanceSchedule: Finger-comb or wide-toothed comb weekly (NO brushing), bath every 4-6 weeks with curl-enhancing shampoo, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, curl refreshing with water mist as needed, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Wirehaired Pointing Griffon.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush followed by a medium comb to maintain the harsh, bristly double coat. Hand-strip every 8-12 weeks to preserve the correct wire texture -- never clip this coat. A stripping knife helps maintain the rough topcoat between full strips. Focus on the longer furnishings on the face, legs, and underbody.
Wirehaired Pointing Griffon.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a texturizing shampoo for wire coats. Avoid conditioners that soften the deliberately harsh coat texture. The Griff's coat should feel rough and bristly, not soft. Towel dry and air-dry to preserve the natural wire texture. Their waterproof undercoat takes time to dry thoroughly.
commonIssues: Soft, flat coat developing if clipped instead of hand-stripped
Beard and mustache matting and odor from food and water
Dense undercoat trapping moisture after water work
Debris collection in the harsh coat during fieldwork
Ear infections from hairy, pendulous ears
Wirehaired Pointing Griffon.extendResults: Clean the beard after every meal and water break. Pluck excess ear hair monthly. Roll the coat weekly with a stripping stone to maintain wire texture between full strips. Trim foot pad hair and sanitary areas as needed. The coat should look rough and natural, never groomed into a polished appearance.
Wirehaired Pointing Griffon.seasonalCare: Griffs shed their undercoat in spring and fall -- daily brushing during these transitions removes the loose undercoat. In Houston's humid summers, the dense coat can trap heat -- keep it hand-stripped to a working length. The waterproof undercoat serves them well in rain but must dry completely to prevent hot spots.
Wirehaired Pointing Griffon.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard cleaning, ear hair plucking monthly, hand-strip every 8-12 weeks, foot pad trimming bi-weekly, ear cleaning weekly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Spinone Italiano.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and wide-toothed comb to maintain the dense, wiry, close-fitting coat. Hand-strip every 8-12 weeks to preserve the harsh, flat-lying coat texture. The distinctive eyebrows, beard, and mustache need daily combing with a fine-toothed comb to prevent matting and food debris buildup.
Spinone Italiano.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing wire coat shampoo. Avoid heavy conditioners that soften the naturally tough, weather-resistant coat. The Spinone's coat should feel coarse and dense, providing protection during fieldwork. Dry the thick beard separately and thoroughly to prevent odor.
commonIssues: Beard and mustache odor from food, water, and drool
Lip fold dermatitis from moisture trapped in facial wrinkles
Dense coat trapping field debris and burrs
Ear infections from pendulous, hairy ears
Drooling causing chest staining and skin irritation
Spinone Italiano.extendResults: Clean the beard and lip folds after every meal and multiple times daily. Keep facial furnishings trimmed to a manageable length. Use a drying ear solution weekly. Wipe drool from the chest and chin regularly. Hand-strip the body coat every few months to maintain the correct harsh texture.
Spinone Italiano.seasonalCare: Spinoni shed moderately in spring and fall when the undercoat loosens. In Houston's humid summers, the dense coat and drooling tendency increase the risk of skin fold infections -- keep facial folds dry and clean. Trim the belly slightly shorter in summer for comfort. The wiry coat insulates against heat.
Spinone Italiano.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard and lip fold cleaning multiple times, ear cleaning weekly with hair plucking monthly, hand-strip every 8-12 weeks, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

German Wirehaired Pointer.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a firm slicker brush and follow with a stripping knife to maintain the flat-lying, harsh, water-repellent wire coat. Hand-strip every 8-10 weeks -- critical for maintaining the correct dense, wiry texture that protects in the field. Use a comb on the bushy eyebrows and beard.
German Wirehaired Pointer.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a texturizing shampoo for wire coats. Their coat is naturally dirt-resistant and self-cleaning, so frequent bathing is unnecessary and counterproductive. Never use softening conditioners. The coat should feel rough and bristly after bathing, not silky.
commonIssues: Loss of weather-resistant wire texture if clipped instead of stripped
Beard collecting food, water, and field debris
Dense undercoat matting if not regularly stripped
Ear infections from hairy ear canals
Hot spots under the dense coat in humid conditions
German Wirehaired Pointer.extendResults: Wipe the beard after every meal and water session. Roll the coat weekly with a stripping knife to pull dead hair and maintain texture. Pluck ear hair monthly. Trim between foot pads to prevent debris accumulation. The GWP coat should always feel rough and bristly -- any softness indicates it needs stripping.
German Wirehaired Pointer.seasonalCare: GWPs blow their undercoat heavily in spring, requiring daily brushing and possibly additional stripping sessions. In Houston summers, the dense undercoat traps heat -- keep it stripped to a working length but never shave. The wire topcoat provides UV protection. In winter, the full coat provides excellent weather protection.
German Wirehaired Pointer.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard cleaning, weekly coat rolling with stripping knife, full hand-strip every 8-10 weeks, ear cleaning weekly, ear hair plucking monthly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Wirehaired Vizsla.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and stripping comb to maintain the dense, wiry, golden-rust coat. Hand-strip every 8-10 weeks to preserve the correct harsh texture. The Wirehaired Vizsla has a more prominent beard and eyebrows than the smooth Vizsla, which need daily combing to prevent matting.
Wirehaired Vizsla.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a texturizing shampoo. Their wire coat is naturally dirt-repellent and rarely develops odor. Avoid conditioners that soften the harsh coat. A color-enhancing shampoo in warm golden-rust tones helps maintain the breed's distinctive rich color.
commonIssues: Wire coat softening or changing color if clipped instead of stripped
Beard matting and odor from food and water
Skin sensitivity -- Wirehaired Vizslas are sensitive despite their tough appearance
Dense undercoat trapping moisture after water activities
Separation anxiety making grooming stressful when owner leaves the room
Wirehaired Vizsla.extendResults: Clean the beard after meals. Roll the coat weekly with a stripping knife between full strips. Apply a coat-enhancing spray with UV protection to prevent the golden-rust color from fading in sunlight. Keep the owner in view during grooming since these dogs become anxious when separated.
Wirehaired Vizsla.seasonalCare: Wirehaired Vizslas shed their undercoat in spring and fall, requiring more frequent brushing during transitions. In Houston summers, the wire coat provides sun protection -- never shave it. Keep the coat stripped to a working length for heat management. These active dogs need extra hydration during summer grooming.
Wirehaired Vizsla.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard combing, weekly coat rolling, hand-strip every 8-10 weeks, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Pudelpointer.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and medium comb to maintain the dense, harsh, wire coat that lies close to the body. Hand-strip every 8-12 weeks to preserve the proper weather-resistant texture. The Pudelpointer should look rough and ready, not polished. Use a stripping knife on the shoulders, back, and sides.
Pudelpointer.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a mild, texturizing shampoo. The Pudelpointer's self-cleaning coat rarely needs frequent bathing. Avoid any products that soften the wire texture. Towel dry and air-dry to maintain the natural, harsh coat quality. Their coat is designed to shed water and resist dirt.
commonIssues: Wire coat texture loss from clipping instead of stripping
Beard and furnishing matting
Dense undercoat impaction if not properly maintained
Ear infections from moderate ear hair growth
Field debris lodging in the harsh coat
Pudelpointer.extendResults: Roll the coat weekly with a stripping knife to maintain texture between full strips. Clean facial furnishings after meals. Trim foot pad hair to prevent slipping and debris collection. The Pudelpointer's coat is functional first -- maintain its weather-resistant, harsh quality above all aesthetic concerns.
Pudelpointer.seasonalCare: Pudelpointers shed moderately in spring and fall when the undercoat turns over. In Houston's humid summers, the dense coat can trap heat -- keep it hand-stripped to an appropriate working length. The wire coat provides excellent protection from sun and brush. Winter care is minimal as the coat provides natural insulation.
Pudelpointer.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily facial furnishing maintenance, weekly coat rolling, hand-strip every 8-12 weeks, ear cleaning weekly, foot pad trimming bi-weekly, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Bracco Italiano.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a rubber curry comb or soft bristle brush. The Bracco's short, dense, glossy coat is low-maintenance but benefits from regular grooming to distribute natural oils and reduce shedding. A grooming mitt provides a daily quick-groom that the breed enjoys. Focus on the longer hair on the ears.
Bracco Italiano.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo that enhances the coat's natural sheen. The Bracco's coat comes in white-orange or white-chestnut -- a color-enhancing shampoo helps maintain vibrancy. Clean the long, pendulous ears thoroughly during every bath with a medicated ear wash.
commonIssues: Ear infections from long, heavy ears with poor air circulation
Drooling causing chin and chest staining
Loose facial skin trapping moisture and developing irritation
Shedding that is heavier than expected for a short coat
Joint issues (hip and elbow dysplasia) requiring careful handling on grooming tables
Bracco Italiano.extendResults: Clean ears every 3-4 days with a drying ear solution. Wipe facial folds and jowls daily to prevent moisture-related irritation. Use a drool bib during meals if staining is severe. A chamois cloth rubbed over the coat between baths maintains the glossy appearance. Provide a non-slip grooming surface for their large, heavy frame.
Bracco Italiano.seasonalCare: Braccos shed moderately year-round with heavier shedding in spring and fall. In Houston summers, their short coat manages heat relatively well, but limit strenuous exercise in peak heat. The pendulous ears are more infection-prone in humid months -- increase cleaning frequency. Winter care is minimal.
Bracco Italiano.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with curry comb, daily grooming mitt wipe, ear cleaning every 3-4 days, facial fold wiping daily, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Treeing Tennessee Brindle.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a rubber curry comb or firm bristle brush. Their short, dense coat is naturally low-maintenance but benefits from regular brushing to remove loose hair and distribute oils. A grooming mitt used 2-3 times weekly provides adequate between-brushing maintenance. The brindle pattern looks best when the coat gleams with health.
Treeing Tennessee Brindle.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a mild deodorizing shampoo. As a hound breed, they can develop a moderate hound scent, though less intense than larger coonhounds. Use warm water and rinse thoroughly. A light moisturizing conditioner helps if the skin tends toward dryness.
commonIssues: Moderate hound odor in warm, humid weather
Ear infections from folded ear flaps
Scratches and scrapes from treeing and outdoor activity
Shedding that increases notably in spring
Tough, calloused foot pads from active outdoor lifestyle
Treeing Tennessee Brindle.extendResults: Use a grooming mitt daily to control shedding and maintain the coat's natural sheen. Clean ears weekly with a drying ear solution. Check paw pads for cracks after heavy outdoor activity. Apply a deodorizing coat spray between baths as needed. The brindle coat should look rich and defined.
Treeing Tennessee Brindle.seasonalCare: Treeing Tennessee Brindles shed moderately year-round with heavier shedding in spring. In Houston's heat, their active lifestyle means extra attention to hydration and post-exercise cool-down. The short coat manages heat well but provide shade during peak hours. Winter care is minimal with their dense coat.
Treeing Tennessee Brindle.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with curry comb, grooming mitt 2-3 times weekly, ear cleaning weekly, nail trims every 3 weeks, paw pad checks after field activity, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Mountain Cur.brushingRoutine: Brush 1-2 times weekly with a firm bristle brush or rubber curry comb. The Mountain Cur's short, dense double coat is rugged and low-maintenance, designed for working conditions. A deshedding tool helps during heavy shedding seasons. The coat is naturally weather-resistant and should feel dense and close-lying.
Mountain Cur.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks or as needed -- Mountain Curs are working dogs whose coat naturally repels dirt. Use a mild, no-frills shampoo. These are not fussy dogs, so keep grooming practical. A deodorizing shampoo helps after particularly muddy or intense outdoor sessions.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding from the dense double coat
Scratches, cuts, and abrasions from hunting and working
Ear infections from debris entering during fieldwork
Tough, cracked paw pads from rugged terrain
Ticks and burrs embedded in the dense coat after outdoor activity
Mountain Cur.extendResults: Check the entire coat thoroughly after every outdoor working session for ticks, burrs, and embedded debris. Apply paw pad balm weekly to keep foot pads healthy. Clean ears after field activity. Use a deshedding tool 2-3 times weekly during spring and fall coat blows to manage the heavy shedding indoors.
Mountain Cur.seasonalCare: Mountain Curs blow their dense undercoat heavily in spring and moderately in fall. Daily deshedding is needed during these transitions. In Houston summers, the double coat provides insulation but ensure shade and water access. Tick prevention is critical year-round in Houston. Winter care is minimal.
Mountain Cur.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly (daily during shedding), full body tick check after outdoor activity, ear cleaning weekly, paw pad conditioning weekly, nail trims every 3-4 weeks, professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

American Foxhound.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a firm bristle brush or rubber hound mitt to remove loose hair from the hard, medium-length, close-lying hound coat. A deshedding tool helps manage the moderate shedding. The coat is naturally dirt-resistant and lies flat without much effort. Daily grooming mitt passes keep shedding under control.
American Foxhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a deodorizing shampoo formulated for hound breeds. American Foxhounds have a moderate hound scent that intensifies in warm weather. Rinse thoroughly and dry the long ears carefully. A second lather may be needed after particularly active outdoor periods.
commonIssues: Moderate hound odor, especially in humid weather
Ear infections from long, soft, pendant ears
Heavier shedding than expected from a medium-length coat
Thrombocytopathy (platelet disorder) causing bleeding concerns during grooming
High energy making grooming sessions challenging without exercise first
American Foxhound.extendResults: Exercise the dog before grooming sessions to ensure calmer handling. Clean ears every 3-4 days with a drying solution. Use a deodorizing coat spray between baths. A grooming mitt used daily significantly reduces shedding on furniture. Keep nails trimmed since their active lifestyle keeps them naturally shorter.
American Foxhound.seasonalCare: American Foxhounds shed moderately year-round with heavier seasonal shedding in spring and fall. In Houston summers, their athletic build tolerates heat better than many breeds, but provide shade and water during hunts or exercise. The hard coat requires no special winter care.
American Foxhound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with bristle brush, daily grooming mitt, ear cleaning every 3-4 days, nail trims every 3-4 weeks, deodorizing spray as needed, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

English Foxhound.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a firm bristle brush or rubber curry comb to maintain the short, dense, hard hound coat. A hound glove provides excellent daily maintenance. Use a deshedding tool during spring and fall coat transitions. The coat is naturally glossy and lies flat with minimal grooming.
English Foxhound.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a deodorizing hound shampoo. English Foxhounds have a moderate pack hound scent. Use a deep-cleaning formula and pay attention to the ears, which trap oil and debris. Rinse completely to maintain the coat's natural hard texture and sheen.
commonIssues: Pack hound scent that can be stronger than solo-kept hounds
Ear infections from long, pendant ears lying flat against the head
Shedding that increases significantly during seasonal transitions
Thick skin that can develop calluses on elbows
Social grooming stress if separated from pack or family during sessions
English Foxhound.extendResults: Clean ears every 3-4 days since the flat, heavy ears create a warm, moist environment perfect for infections. Use a deodorizing spray between baths. Provide soft bedding to prevent elbow calluses. A weekly chamois cloth rub brings out the natural shine in the hard, glossy coat.
English Foxhound.seasonalCare: English Foxhounds shed their dense undercoat in spring and produce a thicker coat in fall. Daily deshedding is helpful during these transitions. In Houston summers, the hard coat provides reasonable heat management but ensure shade during exercise. The breed handles Houston winters without any special coat care.
English Foxhound.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with bristle brush, daily hound glove wipe, ear cleaning every 3-4 days, nail trims every 3-4 weeks, elbow callus prevention with moisturizer, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Harrier.brushingRoutine: Brush weekly with a firm bristle brush or rubber hound mitt to maintain the short, dense, weather-resistant coat. A grooming mitt used 2-3 times weekly captures loose hair and distributes natural oils for a healthy sheen. Use a deshedding tool during spring and fall shedding periods.
Harrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 5-7 weeks with a mild deodorizing shampoo. Harriers have a moderate hound scent -- less intense than larger hound breeds but still noticeable. Clean the pendant ears thoroughly during each bath. A whitening shampoo helps maintain brightness on the white portions of the tricolor coat.
commonIssues: Moderate hound odor in warm weather
Ear infections from pendant ears trapping moisture
White coat portions staining from grass and outdoor activity
Heavier shedding during seasonal transitions than expected
Active temperament making calm grooming challenging
Harrier.extendResults: Clean ears every 3-4 days with a drying ear solution. Use a whitening spray on stained white coat areas between baths. A daily grooming mitt session significantly reduces loose hair on furniture. Exercise before grooming sessions for calmer handling. The tricolor coat looks best when regularly maintained.
Harrier.seasonalCare: Harriers shed moderately year-round with notable increases in spring and fall. In Houston summers, their athletic build and short coat manage heat reasonably well, but provide shade during extended outdoor activity. The dense coat insulates in winter. Ear care increases in importance during humid months.
Harrier.maintenanceSchedule: Weekly brushing with bristle brush, grooming mitt 2-3 times weekly, ear cleaning every 3-4 days, nail trims every 3-4 weeks, whitening spray on stained areas as needed, professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Grand Basset Griffon Vendeen.brushingRoutine: Brush 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and wide-toothed comb to work through the long, rough, harsh double coat. The GBGV's coat should look naturally tousled and rustic -- never over-groomed into a polished look. Use a mat splitter on tangles that form behind the long, pendulous ears and in the leg furnishings. Hand-strip loose dead coat every few months.
Grand Basset Griffon Vendeen.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that preserves the coat's rough, casual texture. Avoid heavy conditioners that flatten the naturally full, tousled coat. The GBGV should look like an elegant but rugged French hound, not a salon dog. Clean the long ears separately during every bath.
commonIssues: Ear infections from the extremely long, hairy, pendulous ears
Matting in the dense undercoat, especially behind ears and in the armpits
Beard and mustache matting and odor from food and drool
Hound odor intensifying in humid conditions
Debris collection in the rough coat during outdoor activity
Grand Basset Griffon Vendeen.extendResults: Clean ears every 2-3 days -- the GBGV's exceptionally long ears are among the most infection-prone of any breed. Comb the beard after every meal. Trim excess ear hair to improve airflow. Keep foot pad hair trimmed. Pull loose dead coat by hand between professional grooming sessions to maintain the correct harsh texture.
Grand Basset Griffon Vendeen.seasonalCare: GBGVs shed moderately in spring and fall when the undercoat loosens. In Houston's humid climate, the long ears are extremely prone to yeast and bacterial infections -- increase ear cleaning to daily during summer months. The rough coat provides some heat insulation but monitor for overheating. Winter care is minimal.
Grand Basset Griffon Vendeen.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily beard cleaning, ear cleaning every 2-3 days (daily in summer), foot pad trimming bi-weekly, hand-strip loose coat as needed, nail trims every 3 weeks, professional grooming every 4-6 weeks.

Chinook.brushingRoutine: Brush the Chinook's double coat 2-3 times per week with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. Focus on the thick ruff around the neck and the feathering on the legs and tail. During heavy shedding periods, daily brushing with an undercoat rake is essential to remove loose fur from the dense undercoat.
Chinook.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks using a mild oatmeal-based shampoo that preserves the coat's natural oils. The Chinook's tawny coat benefits from a color-enhancing shampoo. Always blow-dry thoroughly since the dense undercoat holds moisture and can develop odor if left damp.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding that mats if not managed
Hip dysplasia affecting grooming positioning comfort
Skin allergies in humid climates
Ear infections due to floppy ear structure
Chinook.extendResults: Use a finishing spray with light conditioning to maintain coat sheen between grooms. Apply a detangling spray before brushing to prevent breakage. Keep the belly and inner thigh areas clear of tangles since these mat fastest on active working breeds.
Chinook.seasonalCare: Chinooks blow their undercoat heavily twice a year in spring and fall. During these periods, daily deshedding sessions of 15-20 minutes are recommended. In summer, never shave the double coat as it provides insulation from heat. In winter, the coat naturally thickens and requires extra undercoat raking.
Chinook.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Check and clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional groom every 8-10 weeks for bath, blow-out, and sanitary trim.

Norwegian Lundehund.brushingRoutine: Brush the Lundehund's dense double coat twice weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. Pay attention to the dense ruff around the neck and the shorter coat on the head. A bristle brush works well for finishing and distributing natural oils across the weather-resistant outer coat.
Norwegian Lundehund.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a gentle protein-enriched shampoo. The Lundehund's coat is naturally weather-resistant, so avoid stripping oils with harsh detergents. Use lukewarm water and ensure thorough rinsing, as residue in the dense undercoat can cause irritation.
commonIssues: Intestinal issues (Lundehund syndrome) affecting skin and coat quality
Dense undercoat matting behind ears and under legs
Sensitivity to dietary changes reflecting in coat condition
Extra toes requiring careful nail trimming attention
Norwegian Lundehund.extendResults: A high-quality omega-3 supplement supports coat health and reduces shedding. Use a leave-in conditioner after bathing to keep the outer coat smooth. Between grooms, spot-clean with waterless shampoo wipes to maintain freshness without stripping natural oils.
Norwegian Lundehund.seasonalCare: Sheds heavily in spring and moderately in fall. During spring coat blow, daily undercoat raking is necessary for 2-3 weeks. The coat thickens considerably for winter. In Houston's heat, ensure the undercoat is well-thinned to prevent overheating, but never shave down to skin.
Norwegian Lundehund.maintenanceSchedule: Brush twice weekly year-round, daily during spring blow. Clean ears weekly with veterinary ear solution. Trim all toenails (including extra dewclaws) every 2 weeks. Professional grooming every 10-12 weeks.

Mudi.brushingRoutine: Brush the Mudi's wavy to curly coat 2-3 times weekly using a pin brush and wide-tooth comb. The coat ranges from smooth on the face to wavy-curly on the body. Work through the curly sections gently with a detangling spray to prevent pulling. A slicker brush helps remove debris from the dense body coat.
Mudi.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo designed for curly coats. Follow with a lightweight conditioner to define the natural wave pattern. Avoid heavy conditioners that weigh down the coat. Blow-dry on low heat while scrunching curls to maintain the breed's characteristic texture.
commonIssues: Curly coat matting in friction areas like armpits and behind ears
Debris trapping in wavy body coat after outdoor activity
Color fading in merle varieties from sun exposure
Ear canal hair trapping moisture
Mudi.extendResults: Apply a curl-defining spray after grooming to maintain the coat's natural wave pattern. Brush with a wide-tooth comb between professional grooms to prevent mat formation. Keep friction areas like collar line and armpits free of tangles with daily finger-checking.
Mudi.seasonalCare: The Mudi sheds moderately year-round with a heavier shed in spring. In summer, the coat naturally thins but should not be clipped short as it provides UV protection. In winter, the undercoat thickens. Merle-colored Mudis need sun protection spray on lighter patches during peak summer months.
Mudi.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly with pin brush. Check for mats daily in friction zones. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for bath, trim, and coat shaping.

Pumi.brushingRoutine: Brush the Pumi's distinctive corkscrew curls every 3-4 days with a wide-tooth comb, working section by section. Never use a slicker brush on dry coat as it destroys the curl formation. Comb through when damp with a detangling spray. After combing, let the coat air-dry or scrunch with hands to restore the characteristic curly texture.
Pumi.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a sulfate-free, curl-enhancing shampoo. Apply a light leave-in conditioner and comb through while wet. The key to the Pumi look is allowing the coat to air-dry naturally after combing, which restores the trademark corkscrew curls. Blow-drying straight will ruin the breed's signature appearance.
commonIssues: Curl loss from improper brushing technique or blow-drying
Matting at the base of curls if not combed through to skin
Ear hair trapping moisture and debris
Beard and mustache staining from food and water
Pumi.extendResults: Between grooms, dampen the coat lightly with a spray bottle and scrunch to refresh curls. Keep the beard clean by wiping after meals. Trim ear hair to prevent moisture buildup. Use a curl refresher spray every few days to maintain definition and prevent frizz.
Pumi.seasonalCare: The Pumi's curly coat sheds minimally year-round, making them relatively low-shedding. In humid Houston summers, the coat may frizz more and benefit from anti-humidity curl products. In winter, the coat provides good insulation but may need extra moisture to prevent dry, brittle curls.
Pumi.maintenanceSchedule: Comb through every 3-4 days when damp. Wipe beard and face daily. Clean ears weekly with drying solution. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for bath, comb-out, and shaping trim.

Finnish Lapphund.brushingRoutine: Brush the Finnish Lapphund's profuse double coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. The thick, stand-off outer coat and dense undercoat require thorough line-brushing to reach the skin. Focus on the heavy mane around the neck, the feathered legs, and the plume tail where mats form easily.
Finnish Lapphund.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks using a double-coat specific shampoo that penetrates the dense fur. The coat is naturally dirt-resistant, so over-bathing strips essential oils. Use a high-velocity dryer after bathing to fully dry the dense undercoat and separate the fur for a fluffy finish.
commonIssues: Extreme seasonal shedding requiring daily management
Mat formation behind ears, in armpits, and around collar area
Hot spots in humid climates from trapped moisture in undercoat
Paw pad fur overgrowth collecting debris and ice
Finnish Lapphund.extendResults: Apply a light coat spray between grooms to keep the outer coat smooth and static-free. Use a greyhound comb weekly to check for developing mats in trouble spots. Keep the paw pad fur trimmed to prevent slipping and debris collection. A deshedding treatment at each professional groom extends results by weeks.
Finnish Lapphund.seasonalCare: Blows coat dramatically twice yearly, typically spring and fall, with shedding lasting 2-4 weeks. Daily undercoat raking is mandatory during these periods. In Houston's heat, ensure the undercoat is thoroughly thinned to prevent overheating. Never shave a Lapphund as the double coat regulates temperature in both heat and cold.
Finnish Lapphund.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Trim paw pads monthly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming with deshedding treatment every 8-10 weeks.

Icelandic Sheepdog.brushingRoutine: Brush the Icelandic Sheepdog's thick double coat 3 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. Line-brush through the dense ruff, the bushy tail, and leg feathering. A pin brush works well for the softer areas around the ears. The breed has a naturally full, stand-off coat that requires thorough brushing to prevent undercoat compaction.
Icelandic Sheepdog.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a clarifying shampoo followed by a light conditioner. The coat is somewhat self-cleaning and weather-resistant. Use a high-velocity dryer to fully dry and separate the dense undercoat. Incomplete drying leads to musty odor and potential hot spots.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding producing large tufts of undercoat
Hot spots from damp undercoat in humid environments
Mat formation in the ruff, behind ears, and leg feathering
Excessive paw pad hair growth
Icelandic Sheepdog.extendResults: A thorough deshedding treatment every 8 weeks dramatically reduces loose fur between grooms. Use a finishing spray to add shine and reduce static. Between grooms, use a greyhound comb to check behind ears and under the collar for developing mats.
Icelandic Sheepdog.seasonalCare: Sheds profusely in spring and fall with the undercoat coming out in large clumps. Daily brushing is mandatory during these 3-4 week periods. In Houston summers, thin the undercoat thoroughly but never clip the outer guard hairs. In winter, the coat thickens naturally and benefits from extra moisture conditioning.
Icelandic Sheepdog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Trim paw pads every 3-4 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 8-10 weeks.

Lancashire Heeler.brushingRoutine: Brush the Lancashire Heeler's short, dense double coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush or bristle brush. A deshedding tool like a Furminator works well during heavy shedding to pull loose undercoat. The coat is flat and smooth, making brushing sessions quick but thorough attention to the thicker fur on the neck and hindquarters is important.
Lancashire Heeler.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo. The short coat dries quickly and does not trap moisture easily. A rubber grooming mitt during the bath helps loosen dead undercoat. Rinse thoroughly as even short coats can trap shampoo residue against the skin.
commonIssues: Seasonal undercoat shedding heavier than expected for a short-coated breed
Dry skin in low-humidity or over-bathed conditions
Tan markings can show skin irritation more visibly
Lens luxation requiring gentle handling around the face
Lancashire Heeler.extendResults: A weekly wipe-down with a damp microfiber cloth keeps the coat shiny between baths. Supplement with omega fatty acids for coat sheen. The short coat shows dirt easily, so spot-cleaning after outdoor play maintains a polished appearance.
Lancashire Heeler.seasonalCare: Sheds moderately in spring and fall. Despite the short coat, the undercoat is dense and produces noticeable shedding. In summer, the coat is naturally heat-efficient. In winter, the undercoat thickens. Regular brushing year-round keeps shedding manageable.
Lancashire Heeler.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry brush. Wipe coat with damp cloth between baths. Clean ears every 1-2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath and deshed every 8-10 weeks.

Teddy Roosevelt Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Teddy Roosevelt Terrier's short, smooth coat once weekly with a bristle brush or rubber grooming mitt. The coat lies close to the body and requires minimal maintenance. A hound glove adds extra polish and stimulates skin circulation. Pay attention to the slightly longer hair around the neck and tail base.
Teddy Roosevelt Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a mild shampoo formulated for sensitive skin. The smooth coat does not trap dirt readily and is relatively self-maintaining. A rubber curry mitt during bathing lifts dead hair efficiently. Dry with a towel; the short coat air-dries quickly.
commonIssues: Sensitive skin prone to contact allergies from grasses
Dry, flaky skin in winter months
Minimal coat protection from sun exposure on lighter-colored areas
Active breed prone to minor cuts and scrapes that need monitoring
Teddy Roosevelt Terrier.extendResults: Weekly wipe-downs with pet-safe grooming wipes maintain coat cleanliness between baths. A light coat oil spray keeps the short fur glossy. Moisturizing spray helps during dry winter months. The short coat shows scratches and skin issues readily, so regular visual checks are important.
Teddy Roosevelt Terrier.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly year-round with a moderate increase in spring and fall. The thin coat offers minimal protection from sun, so apply pet-safe sunscreen on light-colored areas in summer. In winter, this breed benefits from a coat or sweater for outdoor time in cold weather.
Teddy Roosevelt Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush once weekly with bristle brush. Wipe coat with grooming wipes as needed. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 8-12 weeks.

Biewer Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Biewer Terrier's long, silky coat daily with a pin brush and fine-tooth comb. The coat is similar to a Yorkshire Terrier's but tri-colored and parts down the spine. Work through sections methodically from ends to roots, using detangling spray to prevent breakage. Pay extra attention to behind the ears, under the legs, and around the harness area where mats form quickly.
Biewer Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a silky coat-specific shampoo and follow with a moisturizing conditioner. Leave conditioner on for 3-5 minutes before rinsing. The fine, single-layer coat tangles easily when dirty, so regular bathing is essential. Wrap in a towel to blot moisture, then blow-dry on low heat while brushing straight.
commonIssues: Rapid mat formation without daily brushing
Tear staining on white facial markings
Fragile coat prone to breakage
Dental disease affecting overall health and coat quality
Topknot and facial hair tangling
Biewer Terrier.extendResults: Use a silk protein leave-in conditioner after every bath to reduce static and tangling. Maintain a topknot or short face trim to keep hair from eyes. Wrapping the coat in tissue paper wraps preserves length for show dogs. For pet trims, a puppy cut every 6-8 weeks dramatically reduces daily maintenance.
Biewer Terrier.seasonalCare: The single coat does not shed seasonally but grows continuously. In humid summers, the coat may become limp and tangle more easily; use a lightweight finishing spray. In winter, static buildup causes tangling; use a humidifier indoors and anti-static spray on the coat.
Biewer Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush and comb. Clean face and eyes daily with tear stain wipes. Bathe every 2-3 weeks. Trim nails every 2 weeks. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks for trim and full conditioning treatment.

Russian Toy.brushingRoutine: For smooth-coated Russian Toys, brush once weekly with a soft bristle brush or grooming mitt. For long-haired varieties, brush 2-3 times weekly with a small pin brush, focusing on the distinctive ear fringes and tail feathering. Use a fine-tooth comb through the ear fringe daily to prevent tangles in this breed's most distinctive feature.
Russian Toy.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, tear-free shampoo suitable for toy breeds. The tiny size means bathing in a sink with a spray attachment works best. For long-haired varieties, use a light conditioner on the ear fringes and feathering. Pat dry carefully and blow-dry on the lowest heat setting; this breed chills easily.
commonIssues: Ear fringe tangling and breakage in long-haired variety
Fragile bones requiring extremely gentle handling during grooming
Dental overcrowding affecting facial grooming
Tear staining on lighter faces
Skin sensitivity to harsh products
Russian Toy.extendResults: Apply a silk serum to the long-haired variety's ear fringes after grooming to keep them smooth and tangle-free. For smooth coats, a weekly chamois buff adds shine. Keep the ear fringes wrapped or protected during meals to prevent food contamination that leads to breakage.
Russian Toy.seasonalCare: Smooth coats shed lightly year-round. Long-haired coats shed minimally. Both varieties are extremely cold-sensitive and need protective clothing in winter. In summer, protect from overheating due to small size. Keep ear fringes trimmed slightly in humid months to prevent moisture-related matting.
Russian Toy.maintenanceSchedule: Smooth coat: brush weekly. Long-hair: brush 2-3 times weekly, comb ear fringes daily. Clean teeth 3-4 times weekly. Trim nails every 2 weeks. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks.

Coton de Tulear.brushingRoutine: Brush the Coton's cotton-like coat daily with a pin brush and wide-tooth comb. The dry, fluffy texture mats differently than silky coats, forming tight knots close to the skin if neglected. Mist the coat lightly with detangling spray before brushing to prevent breakage. Line-brush in sections, lifting layers to reach the skin. The areas behind ears, under legs, and around the collar mat fastest.
Coton de Tulear.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a whitening or brightening shampoo for white coats, followed by a light conditioner. The cottony texture absorbs water like a sponge, so allow extra time for rinsing. Blow-dry completely on medium heat while brushing the coat out straight; air-drying creates matted clumps in this coat type.
commonIssues: Cotton texture creates tight mats close to skin if not brushed daily
White coat shows staining from tears, saliva, and food
Coat discoloration from environmental factors
Ear hair trapping moisture and debris
Belly and leg mats from friction
Coton de Tulear.extendResults: Apply a dry texture spray between grooms to maintain the coat's characteristic fluffy appearance. Use tear stain prevention wipes daily around the eyes. A weekly whitening spray keeps the coat bright between baths. Keeping a light cornstarch dusting on damp areas prevents mat formation.
Coton de Tulear.seasonalCare: The Coton does not shed seasonally but the coat grows continuously. In humid Houston summers, the coat absorbs moisture from the air and mats faster; increase brushing frequency. In winter, static makes the coat flyaway; use a light anti-static spray. The white coat reflects heat well in summer.
Coton de Tulear.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush and comb. Clean face and eyes daily. Bathe every 2-3 weeks with blow-dry. Trim nails every 2 weeks. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks for bath, blow-out, and trim.

Bolognese.brushingRoutine: Brush the Bolognese's fluffy, cotton-textured coat daily with a pin brush and wide-tooth comb. The coat forms in loose, open ringlets rather than lying flat, and mats easily at the base close to the skin. Brush in sections, misting with detangling spray, and work from tips to roots. Pay special attention to behind the ears, leg joints, and belly where friction creates mats.
Bolognese.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a gentle, whitening shampoo for the pure white coat. Use a lightweight conditioner that does not weigh down the naturally fluffy texture. Rinse extremely thoroughly as residue dulls the white coat. Blow-dry completely while fluffing with a pin brush; never air-dry as this compacts the coat.
commonIssues: Dense undercoat mats rapidly without daily brushing
Tear staining on white facial fur
Yellow discoloration around mouth from food and water
Coat texture changes during puppy-to-adult transition
Ear canal hair growth trapping moisture
Bolognese.extendResults: Daily facial cleaning with a damp cloth prevents tear and saliva stains from setting into the white coat. Use a texturizing spray to maintain the fluffy, open ringlet look between grooms. A cornstarch-based grooming powder absorbs oils and refreshes the coat between baths.
Bolognese.seasonalCare: The Bolognese has minimal seasonal shedding but the coat grows continuously. In humid summers, increase brushing frequency as moisture accelerates matting. In winter, the coat provides moderate insulation but this small breed still needs protection from cold. Static control products help in dry heated environments.
Bolognese.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with pin brush. Clean face morning and evening. Bathe every 2-3 weeks. Trim nails every 2 weeks. Pluck ear hair monthly. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks for shaping and deep conditioning.

Lowchen.brushingRoutine: Brush the Lowchen's long, wavy coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and medium-tooth comb. The silky, moderately dense coat does not have an undercoat, making it easier to manage than double-coated breeds. For dogs in the traditional lion clip, brush the long sections thoroughly while keeping the clipped areas tidy. Focus on the chest, ear fringes, and leg feathering.
Lowchen.bathingTips: Bathe every 3-4 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo for silky coats. The single coat responds well to a light rinse-out conditioner. In the lion clip, wash clipped areas separately with attention to skin health. Blow-dry on medium heat while brushing straight to enhance the flowing coat texture.
commonIssues: Matting in the longer sections of the lion clip
Clipper irritation on shaved areas for lion-clipped dogs
Ear hair tangles and ear canal moisture
Sun exposure on clipped skin sections
Coat softness loss from over-bathing
Lowchen.extendResults: Use a silk protein spray on the long portions of the coat to keep them smooth and tangle-resistant. For lion-clipped areas, apply a soothing balm after clipping to prevent irritation. A leave-in conditioner on the mane and tail plume maintains the flowing appearance between grooms.
Lowchen.seasonalCare: The single coat sheds minimally year-round. In summer, dogs in lion clips need pet-safe sunscreen on clipped areas. In winter, the single coat provides limited insulation; sweaters or coats recommended for outdoor time. The coat may become drier in heated indoor environments during winter.
Lowchen.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly. Check clipped areas for irritation weekly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks for lion clip maintenance or 6-8 weeks for pet clips.

Toy Poodle.brushingRoutine: Brush the Toy Poodle's curly, dense coat daily with a slicker brush and metal comb. Line-brush in sections from skin outward to prevent the tight curls from compacting into mats. A small slicker brush designed for toy breeds provides better control. Check behind ears, between legs, and around the collar daily as these areas mat within 24-48 hours if neglected.
Toy Poodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a moisturizing shampoo formulated for curly coats. Apply a hydrating conditioner and leave on for 3-5 minutes. The curly coat traps dirt and product buildup, so thorough rinsing is essential. Blow-dry completely on medium heat while brush-drying straight or fluff-drying for the desired style; never air-dry as it creates matted clumps.
commonIssues: Rapid mat formation in the dense curly coat
Tear staining especially in lighter colors
Ear canal hair growth causing trapped moisture and infections
Dental disease common in toy breeds affecting coat health
Patellar luxation requiring careful positioning during grooming
Toy Poodle.extendResults: A leave-in conditioning spray applied after every groom reduces static and slows mat formation. Maintain the chosen clip pattern by spot-trimming face, feet, and sanitary areas between full grooms. Use a small greyhound comb daily through the topknot and ears to catch developing tangles early.
Toy Poodle.seasonalCare: Poodle coats do not shed seasonally but grow continuously, requiring consistent grooming year-round. In humid Houston summers, the coat absorbs moisture and mats faster; consider a shorter sporting clip. In winter, longer styles provide warmth but require extra moisture conditioning to prevent brittle, dry curls.
Toy Poodle.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with slicker brush. Clean face and eyes daily. Bathe every 2-3 weeks. Pluck ear hair every 2-3 weeks. Trim nails every 2 weeks. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks for clip maintenance.

Miniature Poodle.brushingRoutine: Brush the Miniature Poodle's curly coat daily with a slicker brush and stainless steel comb. The coat is identical in texture to the Standard Poodle but on a smaller frame. Line-brush methodically from skin outward, parting the curls in sections. Pay particular attention to the legs, where the curly coat wraps around the limbs and mats quickly, and behind the ears where friction from head movement creates tangles.
Miniature Poodle.bathingTips: Bathe every 2-3 weeks with a high-quality moisturizing shampoo and conditioner designed for curly coats. The dense curls trap product, so dilute shampoo before applying and rinse until the water runs completely clear. Blow-dry fully while brush-drying; incomplete drying in the dense coat creates mats and potential skin issues.
commonIssues: Dense curly coat mats rapidly without daily brushing
Ear infections from hair growth in the ear canal
Tear staining in lighter coat colors
Coat texture changes after spaying/neutering
Clipper tracks showing in fine curly coat
Miniature Poodle.extendResults: Apply a lightweight leave-in conditioner after every bath to maintain curl elasticity and reduce matting. Between grooms, use a detangling spray before brushing sessions. Maintain face, feet, and tail base trims at home every 2 weeks to extend the professional groom look.
Miniature Poodle.seasonalCare: Non-shedding coat grows continuously regardless of season. In Houston's humid summers, keep the coat in a shorter clip to prevent moisture retention and overheating. In winter, a longer coat provides warmth but requires extra conditioning to prevent dry, brittle curls from indoor heating.
Miniature Poodle.maintenanceSchedule: Brush daily with slicker brush and comb. Clean ears weekly, pluck ear hair every 2-3 weeks. Clean face daily. Trim nails every 2 weeks. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks for clip maintenance.

Australian Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Australian Terrier's harsh, straight outer coat and soft undercoat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and stripping knife. The coat should have a rough, weather-resistant texture that is maintained through hand-stripping. Focus on the softer topknot, the ruff around the neck, and the apron on the chest. A bristle brush adds finishing polish to the flat-lying body coat.
Australian Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a texturizing shampoo that preserves the coat's harsh feel. Over-bathing softens the wiry texture. Apply conditioner only to the softer topknot and chest apron, never on the harsh body coat. The small size makes sink bathing convenient. Towel dry the body and blow-dry the topknot and ruff.
commonIssues: Coat softening from clipping instead of stripping
Topknot tangles and breakage
Skin allergies causing coat thinning
Patellar luxation requiring gentle handling
Ruff and apron matting
Australian Terrier.extendResults: Pull loose dead hairs weekly between professional strips to maintain coat texture. The topknot needs daily fluffing with fingers to prevent it from going flat. A texturizing spray on the body coat between grooms maintains the harsh, natural feel. Keep the apron tidy with light combing.
Australian Terrier.seasonalCare: The double coat sheds moderately in spring and fall. Hand-stripped coats shed less than clipped ones. In summer, the wiry outer coat protects from sun and heat. In winter, the undercoat thickens for warmth. The topknot may need extra care in dry winter air to prevent static and breakage.
Australian Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly. Fluff topknot daily. Hand-strip every 8-12 weeks or clipper trim every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Tibetan Spaniel.brushingRoutine: Brush the Tibetan Spaniel's silky double coat 2-3 times weekly with a pin brush and medium-tooth comb. The coat is moderately long with a smooth flat-lying body coat, longer feathering on the ears, legs, and plumed tail, and a distinctive mane around the neck. Focus brushing on the ear feathering, leg pants, and neck mane where mats form. A slicker brush works for the finer areas behind the ears.
Tibetan Spaniel.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle shampoo and light conditioner. The silky coat is relatively self-maintaining and over-bathing strips natural oils. Pay attention to the ear feathering during washing, combing through while conditioner is applied. Blow-dry on low heat while brushing the coat smooth and fluffing the mane.
commonIssues: Ear feathering tangling and matting
Mane mats around collar area
Tear staining on lighter faces
Leg feathering collecting debris during walks
Cherry eye requiring gentle handling around face
Tibetan Spaniel.extendResults: A silk protein spray on the feathering after grooming keeps it smooth and tangle-resistant. Remove the collar at home when possible to prevent mane mats. Between grooms, comb through ear feathering and check behind ears daily. A light detangling spray before walks prevents debris from sticking to leg feathering.
Tibetan Spaniel.seasonalCare: Sheds the undercoat moderately in spring and fall. During shedding periods, increase brushing to daily with an undercoat rake. In summer, the coat provides sun protection but the undercoat should be thinned for Houston's heat. In winter, the mane and undercoat thicken naturally for insulation.
Tibetan Spaniel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during shedding. Comb ear feathering every other day. Clean eyes and face daily. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks.

Japanese Spitz.brushingRoutine: Brush the Japanese Spitz's pure white, stand-off double coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. The profuse coat has a thick, soft undercoat beneath a straight, stand-off outer coat. Line-brush in sections from skin outward to prevent undercoat matting. Focus on the heavy ruff around the neck, the thick leg furnishings, and the plume tail. A slicker brush reaches dense areas behind the ears and thighs.
Japanese Spitz.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a whitening or brightening shampoo designed for pure white coats. The coat is remarkably non-stick due to its texture, and dirt often dries and falls off naturally. Use a high-velocity dryer to blow out loose undercoat and fully dry the dense fur. Follow with a finishing spray for shine.
commonIssues: White coat staining from tears, saliva, and environmental factors
Dense undercoat matting during shedding season
Coat discoloration from rolling in grass or dirt
Excessive tearing causing facial staining
Static and flyaway coat in dry conditions
Japanese Spitz.extendResults: Apply a whitening spray between baths to maintain coat brightness. Use tear stain prevention wipes daily around the eyes. A finishing spray with UV protection helps prevent yellowing of the white coat from sun exposure. Brush the undercoat every other day to prevent mat foundation from forming.
Japanese Spitz.seasonalCare: Blows the undercoat heavily twice yearly, typically in spring and fall. During these periods, daily brushing and weekly baths are recommended. In Houston summers, thin the undercoat thoroughly with a deshedding treatment but never shave. In winter, the full coat provides natural insulation. White coat reflects heat well in summer.
Japanese Spitz.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Clean face and eyes daily. Bathe every 6-8 weeks with whitening treatment. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 8-10 weeks.

American Eskimo Dog (Miniature).brushingRoutine: Brush the Miniature American Eskimo's dense white double coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. The stand-off coat is profuse with a thick undercoat that mats if neglected. Line-brush through the heavy ruff, leg feathering, and plume tail. Use a greyhound comb to check for mats behind ears, under legs, and where the harness sits.
American Eskimo Dog (Miniature).bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a whitening shampoo and light conditioner. The white coat shows dirt but is naturally resistant to soiling. Use a high-velocity dryer for a thorough blow-out that removes loose undercoat and fluffs the coat. Never skip the blow-dry as the dense undercoat holds moisture for hours and can develop odor or hot spots.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding producing clouds of white fur
White coat staining and yellowing
Tear staining around eyes
Dense undercoat creating hot spots in humidity
Coat matting around collar and harness areas
American Eskimo Dog (Miniature).extendResults: A deshedding treatment at each professional groom significantly reduces loose fur between visits. Apply a whitening spray weekly to maintain coat brightness. Use a leave-in conditioning spray to reduce static and keep the coat smooth. Remove collar and harness at home to prevent friction matting.
American Eskimo Dog (Miniature).seasonalCare: Blows coat heavily twice yearly with intense shedding lasting 2-4 weeks. Daily brushing with an undercoat rake is mandatory during these periods. In Houston summers, ensure the undercoat is well-thinned to prevent overheating. Never shave the double coat as it disrupts natural temperature regulation. In winter, the coat thickens and requires extra brushing to prevent undercoat compaction.
American Eskimo Dog (Miniature).maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Clean face and eyes daily. Bathe every 6-8 weeks with deshedding treatment. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Volpino Italiano.brushingRoutine: Brush the Volpino's long, stand-off double coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. The profuse coat stands away from the body and has a dense undercoat. Line-brush thoroughly through the heavy ruff, leg feathering, and bushy tail. Use a fine comb around the ears and face where the coat transitions from long to short.
Volpino Italiano.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a volumizing shampoo that maintains the coat's natural stand-off quality. The predominantly white coat benefits from a whitening treatment. Apply a light conditioner to the outer coat only. Blow-dry with a high-velocity dryer while brushing outward to maximize volume and remove loose undercoat.
commonIssues: Undercoat matting if brushing does not reach the skin
White coat staining and discoloration
Heavy seasonal shedding
Ruff and collar area matting
Coat losing its stand-off quality from heavy products
Volpino Italiano.extendResults: Use a texturizing spray to maintain the coat's characteristic volume between grooms. Avoid heavy conditioners or silicone-based products that weigh down the stand-off coat. A light cornstarch dusting absorbs oils and refreshes the white coat between baths. Brush the ruff daily to prevent mat formation.
Volpino Italiano.seasonalCare: Sheds the undercoat heavily in spring and moderately in fall. During heavy shedding, daily brushing with an undercoat rake is essential. In summer, thin the undercoat but maintain the outer guard hairs for sun protection. In winter, the coat thickens considerably and requires extra brushing attention.
Volpino Italiano.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during shedding. Clean face daily. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming with deshedding every 8 weeks.

German Spitz.brushingRoutine: Brush the German Spitz's abundant double coat 3-4 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. The profuse stand-off coat with its dense, cotton-like undercoat requires thorough brushing to the skin. Focus on the luxurious mane, the thick leg feathering, and the bushy tail curled over the back. Line-brush in sections, lifting the coat to access the undercoat layer.
German Spitz.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a pH-balanced shampoo. The coat has excellent self-cleaning properties and over-bathing softens the naturally crisp texture. Use a volumizing conditioner on the outer coat. Blow-dry completely with a high-velocity dryer while brushing outward to maximize the stand-off appearance.
commonIssues: Dense undercoat matting during seasonal shedding
Coat compaction from lack of thorough brushing
Hot spots from trapped moisture in humid conditions
Mane and ruff mats from collar friction
Tail fur tangling where it curls over the back
German Spitz.extendResults: Apply a texturizing spray after grooming to maintain coat volume. Between grooms, check the mane, behind-ear area, and tail base for developing mats. A weekly undercoat combing session with a greyhound comb catches tangles before they become solid mats. Remove collars at home to prevent mane damage.
German Spitz.seasonalCare: Blows the thick undercoat heavily twice yearly. During the 3-4 week shedding period, daily brushing and a professional deshedding treatment are recommended. In Houston summers, thin the undercoat thoroughly but never shave, as the double coat provides crucial heat insulation. In winter, the full coat is in its element.
German Spitz.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Check for mats daily in ruff area. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming with deshedding treatment every 8-10 weeks.

Eurasier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Eurasier's medium-length double coat 3 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. The coat is moderately long with a thick, soft undercoat and a straight-to-slightly wavy outer coat. Focus on the longer fur around the neck ruff, behind the ears, the leg feathering, and the bushy tail. A slicker brush works well for areas prone to matting like the armpits and behind the ears.
Eurasier.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a mild double-coat shampoo. The Eurasier's coat is moderately self-cleaning and frequent bathing is unnecessary. Use a light conditioner on the feathering and ruff. Blow-dry completely with a high-velocity dryer to prevent moisture retention in the dense undercoat.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal shedding with large tufts of undercoat
Mat formation behind ears and in armpits
Hot spots in humid climates from damp undercoat
Coat color changes over time complicating grooming product selection
Sensitivity requiring calm, quiet grooming environment
Eurasier.extendResults: A deshedding treatment at each professional groom reduces shedding by up to 80% between visits. Use a leave-in conditioning spray on the longer furnishings to prevent tangling. The Eurasier bonds deeply with their groomer, so consistent appointments with the same stylist produce the best cooperation and results.
Eurasier.seasonalCare: Sheds heavily twice yearly with the undercoat coming out in clumps over 2-4 weeks. Daily brushing is mandatory during these periods. In Houston summers, thorough undercoat removal is critical to prevent overheating. Never shave a Eurasier. In winter, the coat achieves its fullest density and requires regular maintenance brushing.
Eurasier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3 times weekly, daily during shedding. Check friction areas every other day. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming with deshedding every 8-10 weeks.

Thai Bangkaew.brushingRoutine: Brush the Thai Bangkaew's thick double coat 3-4 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The profuse coat features a dense undercoat and medium-length outer coat that stands off the body. Focus on the heavy mane, the thick leg feathering, and the plume tail. Line-brush thoroughly, as the dense undercoat mats easily when neglected.
Thai Bangkaew.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a deep-cleansing shampoo that reaches through the dense double coat. The thick undercoat requires extra time for soaking, lathering, and rinsing. Use a high-velocity dryer for complete drying; the dense coat holds moisture for hours if air-dried. A deshedding conditioner during bathing helps release loose undercoat.
commonIssues: Extremely dense undercoat mats rapidly without regular maintenance
Heavy seasonal shedding requiring daily management
Hot spots from moisture trapped in undercoat in humid climates
Coat debris from outdoor activity
Strong-willed temperament requiring experienced handling during grooming
Thai Bangkaew.extendResults: A professional deshedding treatment every 6-8 weeks dramatically reduces loose fur. Use a finishing spray to reduce static and add shine. Between grooms, focus on trouble spots with a greyhound comb. This breed benefits from a consistent grooming routine established from puppyhood for cooperative behavior.
Thai Bangkaew.seasonalCare: Blows coat heavily twice yearly. In Houston's humid climate, proactive undercoat removal before summer is essential to prevent heat stress and hot spots. Daily brushing during shedding season is non-negotiable. In winter, the coat reaches peak density and requires thorough maintenance to prevent matting.
Thai Bangkaew.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Check undercoat density monthly. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 6-8 weeks.

Jindo.brushingRoutine: Brush the Jindo's dense double coat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The coat is medium-length with a soft, dense undercoat and straight, harsh outer coat. Focus on the thicker areas around the neck, chest, and hindquarters. The Jindo is naturally clean and the coat lies flat and neat when well-maintained. Use a bristle brush for finishing to add sheen.
Jindo.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a gentle, natural shampoo. The Jindo is famously clean and almost cat-like in self-grooming habits, so frequent bathing is unnecessary and counterproductive. The coat has natural water-resistance. Use a high-velocity dryer to remove loose undercoat during baths. This breed may be particular about grooming; go slowly with new handlers.
commonIssues: Intense seasonal shedding, also called 'blowing coat'
Independent temperament making grooming challenging for unfamiliar handlers
Undercoat compaction from infrequent brushing
Sensitivity to unfamiliar grooming environments
Jindo.extendResults: Consistent brushing twice weekly prevents undercoat compaction that leads to excessive shedding. A deshedding treatment during the twice-yearly coat blow removes the bulk of dead undercoat efficiently. Establishing a grooming routine from puppyhood is crucial as Jindos are slow to trust new handlers.
Jindo.seasonalCare: Blows coat dramatically twice yearly, with the shedding producing impressive amounts of fur over 2-3 weeks. Daily brushing is essential during these periods. In Houston summers, ensure the undercoat is thoroughly thinned. Never shave a Jindo. In winter, the coat naturally thickens and the breed handles cold well.
Jindo.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 8-10 weeks, more frequently during shedding season.

Shikoku.brushingRoutine: Brush the Shikoku's harsh, straight double coat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The medium-length coat has a dense, soft undercoat and a harsh outer guard coat. Focus on the thicker fur around the neck, chest, and hindquarters. A bristle brush on the outer coat adds polish. The Shikoku's coat lies naturally neat but the undercoat requires regular attention.
Shikoku.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a mild, pH-balanced shampoo. The coat has natural dirt-resistant properties similar to other Japanese breeds. Use a high-velocity dryer after bathing to blow out loose undercoat and ensure complete drying. Avoid over-bathing as it strips the coat's natural oils and harsh texture.
commonIssues: Heavy seasonal undercoat shedding
Independent temperament requiring patient grooming approach
Undercoat matting if brushing is inconsistent
Sensitivity to handling by unfamiliar groomers
Shikoku.extendResults: Regular twice-weekly brushing prevents undercoat buildup that causes the heavy shedding clumps. A deshedding conditioner during bath time loosens dead undercoat for removal. This breed benefits from a consistent grooming routine with the same handler. A finishing spray maintains the coat's natural sheen.
Shikoku.seasonalCare: Blows coat twice yearly with intense shedding in spring and moderate shedding in fall. Daily undercoat raking is needed during the 2-3 week shedding period. In Houston summers, thin the undercoat well but maintain the guard coat for sun protection. In winter, the undercoat fills in densely for insulation.
Shikoku.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Bathe every 8-10 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 8-10 weeks.

Kai Ken.brushingRoutine: Brush the Kai Ken's medium-length double coat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The coat is harsh and straight with a thick, soft undercoat. The distinctive brindle coloring shows best on a well-groomed coat. Focus on the denser areas around the neck, chest, and hindquarters. A bristle brush adds a finishing polish that highlights the brindle pattern.
Kai Ken.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a gentle shampoo that does not strip natural oils. Like other Japanese breeds, the Kai Ken is naturally clean. A color-enhancing shampoo can bring out the depth of the brindle markings. Use a high-velocity dryer to fully dry the dense undercoat and blow out loose fur.
commonIssues: Seasonal undercoat shedding producing large amounts of fur
Reserved temperament requiring patient, gentle grooming approach
Undercoat compaction from insufficient brushing
Brindle coat showing dandruff or dry skin more visibly
Kai Ken.extendResults: A high-quality omega-3 supplement enhances the brindle coat's depth and shine. Regular brushing with a bristle brush distributes natural oils across the coat. A deshedding treatment at each professional groom reduces shedding significantly. Use a finishing spray designed for dark or brindle coats to enhance color.
Kai Ken.seasonalCare: Blows coat twice yearly with heavy shedding in spring. Daily brushing during shedding season removes the dense undercoat efficiently. In Houston summers, thin the undercoat thoroughly to prevent overheating but never shave. In winter, the coat reaches full density and the brindle pattern deepens.
