Kai Ken.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 8-10 weeks.

Kishu Ken.brushingRoutine: Brush the Kishu Ken's short-to-medium double coat twice weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The coat is dense with a harsh, straight outer layer and thick, soft undercoat. The predominantly white coat shows well when brushed smooth. Focus on the denser areas around the neck and chest ruff. A bristle brush provides a finishing polish.
Kishu Ken.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a whitening shampoo for the predominantly white coat. The Kishu is a clean breed by nature and does not develop strong odor. Use a high-velocity dryer to fully remove loose undercoat and dry the dense fur. A brightening rinse after shampooing enhances the white coat.
commonIssues: White coat showing stains and discoloration readily
Seasonal undercoat shedding
Reserved nature requiring a trusted groomer
Dry skin in low-humidity environments showing as dandruff on white coat
Kishu Ken.extendResults: Weekly wipe-downs with whitening wipes maintain coat brightness between baths. A deshedding treatment at each professional groom reduces loose fur. Omega-3 supplements improve coat shine and reduce flaking that shows prominently on the white coat. Use a UV-protection finishing spray to prevent yellowing from sun exposure.
Kishu Ken.seasonalCare: Blows coat twice yearly with heavier shedding in spring. Daily brushing during the 2-3 week shedding period. In Houston summers, thin the undercoat well and apply sun protection spray to prevent the white coat from yellowing. In winter, the coat thickens and the white fur maintains well in cooler weather.
Kishu Ken.maintenanceSchedule: Brush twice weekly, daily during coat blow. Wipe coat weekly with whitening wipes. Bathe every 8-10 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional grooming every 8-10 weeks.

Hokkaido.brushingRoutine: Brush the Hokkaido's thick, harsh double coat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The medium-length coat has a very dense undercoat adapted for cold climates and a harsh, straight outer coat. Focus on the heavy ruff around the neck, the dense hindquarters, and the bushy tail. Line-brush to the skin to prevent undercoat compaction.
Hokkaido.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a clarifying shampoo that penetrates the dense double coat. The coat is naturally weather-resistant. Use a high-velocity dryer for a thorough blow-out; the extremely dense undercoat holds moisture and must be completely dried to prevent hot spots and odor. A deshedding conditioner during bathing helps release trapped undercoat.
commonIssues: Extremely heavy seasonal shedding from the dense undercoat
Hot spots and skin issues in humid Houston climate
Undercoat compaction from insufficient brushing depth
Strong-willed temperament requiring experienced handling
Coat retained moisture leading to mildew odor
Hokkaido.extendResults: A professional deshedding treatment is essential for this breed's dense coat. Use a finishing spray between grooms to maintain coat texture and reduce static. Regular undercoat checks with a greyhound comb catch developing mats before they compact. This breed's coat is designed for subarctic conditions, so extra vigilance is needed in Houston's heat.
Hokkaido.seasonalCare: Blows coat heavily twice yearly, producing enormous amounts of shed fur. Daily intensive brushing for 3-4 weeks during each shedding cycle. In Houston's heat and humidity, the extremely dense undercoat is a particular concern; thoroughly thin it in spring and monitor for heat stress through summer. Never shave. Winter coat is extremely thick.
Hokkaido.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Check undercoat density regularly. Bathe every 8-10 weeks with deshedding treatment. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 6-8 weeks, especially before summer.

Taiwan Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush the Taiwan Dog's short, tight-fitting coat once weekly with a rubber curry brush or bristle brush. The smooth coat lies close to the body with minimal undercoat. A grooming mitt works well for removing loose hair and distributing natural oils. The thin, fine coat requires gentle pressure to avoid skin irritation.
Taiwan Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a gentle, moisturizing shampoo. The short coat dries quickly and does not trap dirt. A rubber grooming mitt during bathing lifts dead hair effectively. The thin coat provides minimal barrier, so use lukewarm water and avoid harsh products that can irritate the skin.
commonIssues: Thin coat providing minimal protection from sun, cold, and abrasion
Dry skin in winter months
Sensitivity to harsh grooming products
Minimal coat insulation in cold weather
Taiwan Dog.extendResults: Weekly wipe-downs with a damp microfiber cloth keep the smooth coat gleaming. A light coat oil massage monthly adds shine and moisturizes the skin. The short coat shows skin condition clearly, making it easy to spot issues early. A chamois cloth buff after brushing adds extra sheen.
Taiwan Dog.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly year-round with minimal seasonal variation. In Houston summers, apply pet-safe sunscreen on lighter-colored areas. In winter, this breed needs a coat or sweater for outdoor time as the thin coat provides little insulation. The breed tolerates Houston's heat relatively well due to the thin coat.
Taiwan Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush once weekly with rubber curry or bristle brush. Wipe coat weekly. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 8-12 weeks.

Carolina Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush the Carolina Dog's short-to-medium double coat twice weekly with a slicker brush and bristle brush. The coat is moderately dense with a soft undercoat and straight outer coat. The coat is thicker around the neck and shoulders. A rubber curry brush removes loose hair effectively from the smooth body coat. Use a bristle brush for finishing.
Carolina Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a gentle, natural shampoo. The Carolina Dog's coat is naturally clean and somewhat self-maintaining. Over-bathing strips the natural oils that give the coat its weather-resistant quality. A rubber grooming mitt during bathing helps release loose undercoat. Towel dry; the moderate coat dries relatively quickly.
commonIssues: Moderate seasonal shedding
Undercoat buildup if not regularly brushed
Natural primitive coat may have strong natural odor when wet
Sensitive temperament requiring gentle grooming approach
Carolina Dog.extendResults: Regular twice-weekly brushing keeps shedding manageable and distributes natural oils for a healthy sheen. A deshedding tool used once weekly during shedding season removes bulk undercoat. The natural coat does well with minimal intervention; avoid heavy products that alter the coat's natural texture.
Carolina Dog.seasonalCare: Sheds undercoat moderately in spring and fall. During shedding periods, increase brushing to every other day. In Houston summers, the moderate coat handles heat reasonably well. In winter, the undercoat thickens. The primitive coat is well-adapted to outdoor conditions year-round.
Carolina Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush twice weekly with slicker and bristle brush. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath and deshed every 8-12 weeks.

New Guinea Singing Dog.brushingRoutine: Brush the New Guinea Singing Dog's short-to-medium plush double coat twice weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The coat is dense and somewhat plush with a soft undercoat. The coat is thicker around the neck, shoulders, and hindquarters. Use a bristle brush for finishing on the shorter body areas. Handle gently as this primitive breed may be cautious about grooming.
New Guinea Singing Dog.bathingTips: Bathe every 10-12 weeks with a mild, natural shampoo. The primitive coat is naturally self-cleaning to a degree. Over-bathing disrupts the natural coat oils. Use lukewarm water and gentle handling, as this breed can be wary. Towel dry primarily; blow-dry on low only if the dog tolerates it.
commonIssues: Primitive temperament making grooming challenging without established trust
Moderate seasonal shedding
Natural coat odor when wet
Sensitivity to handling that requires patience and trust-building
Dense undercoat in Houston's heat
New Guinea Singing Dog.extendResults: Establish a consistent, gentle grooming routine from the earliest possible age. Short, positive sessions build trust better than infrequent long sessions. A weekly brushing routine maintains coat health and builds the handler-dog bond crucial for this primitive breed. Minimize grooming products; the natural coat needs little enhancement.
New Guinea Singing Dog.seasonalCare: Sheds undercoat in spring and fall. During shedding, increase brushing frequency to every other day using an undercoat rake. In Houston's heat, ensure the undercoat is adequately thinned. The primitive coat provides natural insulation in both heat and cold. Never shave this double-coated breed.
New Guinea Singing Dog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush twice weekly with patience and positive reinforcement. Bathe only as needed, every 10-12 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks (may require conditioning). Professional grooming with an experienced handler every 10-12 weeks.

Dogo Argentino.brushingRoutine: Brush the Dogo Argentino's short, smooth white coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt. The single-layered coat is thick but short, lying flat against the muscular body. A natural bristle brush adds finishing shine. Despite the short length, the coat sheds moderately and regular brushing significantly reduces loose hair on furniture.
Dogo Argentino.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a whitening shampoo designed for pure white coats. The short coat makes bathing straightforward and quick-drying. Use a medicated or hypoallergenic shampoo if skin sensitivities are present, as the breed can be prone to skin allergies. A rubber grooming mitt during bathing lifts dead hair. Towel dry; the coat dries within minutes.
commonIssues: White coat showing stains and discoloration easily
Skin allergies and sensitivity common in the breed
Sunburn on the white skin and coat
Deafness in some individuals requiring visual grooming cues
Strong muscular build requiring confident handling
Dogo Argentino.extendResults: Apply pet-safe sunscreen to exposed areas, especially the nose and ears, before outdoor time. Weekly wipe-downs with whitening wipes maintain coat brightness. A high-quality diet with omega fatty acids supports skin health and coat shine. Monitor for red, irritated skin patches which are common in this breed.
Dogo Argentino.seasonalCare: Sheds moderately year-round with a slight increase in spring. In Houston summers, the white coat reflects heat but the exposed skin is vulnerable to sunburn; provide shade and sunscreen. In winter, the single short coat provides minimal insulation; sweaters may be needed for extended outdoor time in cold snaps.
Dogo Argentino.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry. Apply sunscreen before outdoor time. Wipe coat weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks with whitening treatment. Clean ears weekly (check for deafness). Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 6-8 weeks.

Fila Brasileiro.brushingRoutine: Brush the Fila Brasileiro's short, smooth coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush and bristle brush. The thick, smooth coat lies close to the massive body. Use firm, confident strokes with the rubber curry to remove dead hair and stimulate circulation. A natural bristle brush adds finishing sheen. Handle with confidence as this breed is large and may be wary of unfamiliar handlers.
Fila Brasileiro.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo suitable for sensitive skin. The large size requires a spacious bathing area. Pay attention to cleaning the facial wrinkles and jowls thoroughly, as moisture and debris collect in the folds. Dry all wrinkles and folds completely to prevent bacterial or yeast growth. Towel dry the short coat.
commonIssues: Facial fold dermatitis from trapped moisture and bacteria
Heavy drooling causing chest and chin staining
Skin fold infections requiring regular cleaning
Large size making grooming logistics challenging
Strong guarding instinct requiring trusted groomer relationship
Fila Brasileiro.extendResults: Clean facial wrinkles and jowls daily with antimicrobial wipes. Keep a drool cloth handy and wipe the chin regularly to prevent skin irritation and staining. A light coat spray weekly maintains shine. Establish the grooming routine from puppyhood to ensure cooperation as the dog reaches full size.
Fila Brasileiro.seasonalCare: Sheds moderately year-round with increased shedding in spring. The short coat handles Houston's heat reasonably well but the large body mass generates significant heat. Ensure access to shade and cool surfaces in summer. In winter, the short coat provides limited insulation for the breed's South American origins.
Fila Brasileiro.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry. Clean facial folds daily. Wipe drool as needed. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 6-8 weeks.

Perro de Presa Canario.brushingRoutine: Brush the Presa Canario's short, coarse coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush or firm bristle brush. The coat is flat-lying and somewhat rough to the touch, with no undercoat. Use firm strokes to remove dead hair and stimulate circulation in the thick skin. A chamois cloth adds finishing shine to the short coat.
Perro de Presa Canario.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo. The short coat without undercoat makes bathing quick. Pay special attention to cleaning the facial wrinkles around the muzzle, which trap moisture and debris. Dry all folds completely. The coarse coat dries quickly with toweling.
commonIssues: Facial wrinkle infections from trapped moisture
Skin allergies manifesting as redness and itching
Coarse coat showing dandruff when dry
Large size and strong temperament requiring experienced handling
Elbow calluses from lying on hard surfaces
Perro de Presa Canario.extendResults: Clean facial wrinkles daily with antimicrobial wipes and dry thoroughly. Apply a moisturizing balm to elbows to prevent callus formation. A weekly wipe-down with a damp cloth maintains coat cleanliness. Omega fatty acid supplements improve coat sheen and reduce skin dryness.
Perro de Presa Canario.seasonalCare: The single coat sheds lightly year-round with minimal seasonal variation. In Houston summers, the short coat dissipates heat well but the dark brindle colors absorb sun. Provide shade during peak hours. In winter, the single coat offers minimal insulation; keep outdoor time limited during cold weather.
Perro de Presa Canario.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry. Clean facial folds daily. Apply elbow balm weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 6-8 weeks.

Central Asian Shepherd.brushingRoutine: Brush the Central Asian Shepherd's dense double coat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush, undercoat rake, and pin brush. The coat ranges from short to moderately long depending on the variety, but all types have a dense, protective undercoat. Focus on the thick ruff, the heavily furred hindquarters, and the tail. The massive size of this breed means grooming sessions cover a lot of area; work in sections.
Central Asian Shepherd.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a deep-cleansing shampoo designed for thick double coats. The coat is naturally weather-resistant and self-cleaning. Due to the breed's size, a walk-in shower or outdoor bathing setup works best. Use a high-velocity dryer to fully dry the dense undercoat; this takes significant time on such a large dog.
commonIssues: Extreme seasonal shedding producing massive amounts of fur
Dense undercoat creating hot spots in humid climates
Size making home grooming logistically challenging
Guardian temperament requiring established trust with groomer
Undercoat compaction from insufficient drying
Central Asian Shepherd.extendResults: Professional deshedding treatments every 6-8 weeks are essential for this breed. Between grooms, check the ruff and hindquarters for developing mats. A finishing spray helps maintain coat texture. Establish the grooming routine from puppyhood when the dog is still manageable in size.
Central Asian Shepherd.seasonalCare: Blows coat extremely heavily in spring, shedding the entire undercoat over 3-4 weeks. Daily brushing during this period is mandatory. In Houston's heat and humidity, proactive undercoat removal is critical; this breed is adapted for continental climates. Never shave. In winter, the coat reaches maximum density.
Central Asian Shepherd.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Bathe every 8-12 weeks with deshedding treatment. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 6-8 weeks in warm months, every 10-12 weeks in winter.

Caucasian Shepherd.brushingRoutine: Brush the Caucasian Shepherd's massive, thick double coat 3-4 times weekly with a large pin brush, undercoat rake, and dematting tool. The long, coarse outer coat and extremely dense undercoat make this one of the most maintenance-intensive coats among all breeds. Focus on the enormous mane, the heavily feathered legs, and the bushy tail. Line-brush methodically in sections; a full grooming session on this giant breed takes 1-2 hours.
Caucasian Shepherd.bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks using a heavy-duty double-coat shampoo and conditioner. The enormous size requires a walk-in bathing facility. The coat absorbs enormous amounts of water and takes considerable time to saturate, wash, and rinse. A high-velocity dryer is absolutely essential; this coat can take 2-3 hours to fully dry. Incomplete drying leads to skin problems.
commonIssues: Extreme coat density creating heat stress in warm climates
Massive seasonal shedding producing garbage bags of fur
Mats forming in the thick mane, behind ears, and in leg furnishings
Coat requiring hours to fully dry after bathing
Giant size and protective temperament making professional grooming challenging
Caucasian Shepherd.extendResults: Professional deshedding treatments are essential and should include a thorough blow-out. Between grooms, daily checks of the mane and behind-ear areas prevent mat formation. Use a heavy-duty detangling spray before brushing. This breed requires a groomer experienced with giant protective breeds.
Caucasian Shepherd.seasonalCare: Blows coat extremely heavily once or twice yearly, producing extraordinary amounts of shed fur over several weeks. In Houston's climate, this breed faces serious heat stress concerns; aggressive undercoat removal in spring is critical for health. Daily intensive brushing during shedding season. Never shave. Winter coat is at maximum density and impressive volume.
Caucasian Shepherd.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3-4 times weekly, daily during coat blow (expect 30-60 minutes per session). Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 6-8 weeks in warm months.

South African Boerboel.brushingRoutine: Brush the Boerboel's short, dense, smooth coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush and bristle brush. The coat is short but thick, lying flat against the powerful body. Use firm circular motions with the rubber curry to remove dead hair and stimulate the skin. A natural bristle brush adds finishing shine. The breed's large size means covering a lot of surface area.
South African Boerboel.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo. The short coat makes bathing relatively straightforward despite the large size. Pay attention to cleaning the facial wrinkles around the broad muzzle. A rubber grooming mitt during bathing removes dead coat efficiently. Towel dry; the short coat dries quickly.
commonIssues: Facial wrinkle infections from moisture and debris
Skin allergies common in the breed
Short coat provides minimal protection from sun on lighter-colored dogs
Large size requiring spacious grooming setup
Elbow and hock calluses from lying on hard surfaces
South African Boerboel.extendResults: Clean facial wrinkles 3-4 times weekly with antimicrobial wipes. Apply balm to elbows and hocks to prevent calluses. A weekly chamois buff adds shine to the short coat. Omega fatty acid supplements improve skin health and coat condition. Monitor for hot spots, especially in Houston's humidity.
South African Boerboel.seasonalCare: Sheds moderately year-round with a slight increase in spring. The short coat handles Houston's heat reasonably well for a large breed. In summer, provide ample shade and cool surfaces. Lighter-colored dogs need sun protection. In winter, the short coat provides limited insulation.
South African Boerboel.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry. Clean wrinkles 3-4 times weekly. Apply callus balm weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 6-8 weeks.

Tosa Inu.brushingRoutine: Brush the Tosa Inu's short, dense coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush and firm bristle brush. The coat is short, hard, and dense, lying flat against the massive body. Use confident, firm strokes with the rubber curry to remove dead hair. A chamois cloth or grooming mitt provides a final polish. Handle with calm confidence as this breed responds to assured handling.
Tosa Inu.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo. The short coat is low-maintenance for bathing but the breed's large size requires adequate space. Clean the facial folds and jowls thoroughly during each bath. Rinse completely and dry all wrinkles and folds to prevent bacterial growth. Towel dry the body coat.
commonIssues: Facial fold dermatitis from trapped moisture
Heavy drooling causing skin irritation on chin and chest
Large size requiring experienced, confident handling
Short coat providing limited sun protection
Elbow calluses from hard surfaces
Tosa Inu.extendResults: Daily facial fold cleaning with antimicrobial wipes prevents skin infections. Keep a drool cloth accessible and wipe the chin regularly. Apply protective balm to elbows weekly. A weekly coat wipe-down with a damp microfiber cloth maintains cleanliness. Establish grooming routines from puppyhood.
Tosa Inu.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly to moderately year-round with minimal seasonal variation. In Houston summers, the dark coat absorbs heat; provide shade and limit exercise during peak temperatures. In winter, the short single coat provides minimal insulation. The breed's Japanese origins make it adaptable to moderate climates.
Tosa Inu.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry. Clean facial folds daily. Wipe drool as needed. Apply elbow balm weekly. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 6-8 weeks.

Akita Inu (Japanese).brushingRoutine: Brush the Japanese Akita's thick, plush double coat 3 times weekly with a pin brush and undercoat rake. The coat is dense and somewhat shorter than the American Akita's, with a very thick, soft undercoat and straight, harsh outer coat. Focus on the heavy ruff, the thick hindquarter coat, and the curled tail. Line-brush in sections to reach the extremely dense undercoat.
Akita Inu (Japanese).bathingTips: Bathe every 8-10 weeks with a pH-balanced double-coat shampoo. Like other Japanese breeds, the Akita is naturally clean and fastidious. The dense undercoat requires thorough soaking, lathering, and rinsing. Use a high-velocity dryer for a complete blow-out that removes loose undercoat. Incomplete drying can lead to hot spots.
commonIssues: Extreme seasonal shedding producing massive amounts of undercoat
Hot spots from moisture trapped in dense undercoat
Coat matting in the dense ruff and thigh areas
Breed-typical independence requiring patient grooming approach
Sebaceous adenitis risk in some lines affecting coat quality
Akita Inu (Japanese).extendResults: A professional deshedding treatment every 6-8 weeks dramatically reduces loose fur. Between grooms, check the ruff, behind ears, and thighs for developing mats. A finishing spray maintains the coat's natural sheen. Consistent omega-3 supplementation supports the coat's density and health.
Akita Inu (Japanese).seasonalCare: Blows coat twice yearly with extraordinary amounts of shedding, particularly in spring. Daily brushing for 3-4 weeks during shedding season is mandatory. In Houston's heat, thorough undercoat removal is critical for this cold-climate breed. Never shave an Akita. In winter, the coat achieves impressive fullness.
Akita Inu (Japanese).maintenanceSchedule: Brush 3 times weekly, daily during coat blow. Bathe every 8-10 weeks with deshedding treatment. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 6-8 weeks in warm months.

Kai Ken (244).brushingRoutine: Brush the Kai Ken's medium-length double coat 2-3 times weekly with a slicker brush and undercoat rake. The coat features a harsh, straight outer layer and thick undercoat, with the distinctive brindle pattern visible throughout. Focus on the thicker areas around the neck, chest, and hindquarters where the undercoat is densest. A fine-tooth comb helps check for small tangles near the skin.
Kai Ken (244).bathingTips: Bathe every 8-12 weeks with a gentle, natural shampoo that preserves coat oils. The Kai Ken is naturally clean and odor-free, consistent with Japanese breed characteristics. A color-enhancing shampoo deepens the distinctive brindle markings. Use a high-velocity dryer to remove loose undercoat during the bath process.
commonIssues: Seasonal undercoat shedding
Independent temperament requiring trust-based grooming relationship
Undercoat compaction if brushing is irregular
Brindle coat masking skin conditions that need careful inspection
Kai Ken (244).extendResults: Consistent brushing schedule maintains the brindle coat's luster and prevents undercoat buildup. A finishing spray designed for dark coats enhances brindle depth. Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation supports coat health. This breed's natural cleanliness means less product intervention is needed between professional grooms.
Kai Ken (244).seasonalCare: Blows coat twice yearly with more intense shedding in spring. Daily undercoat raking during shedding season. In Houston's heat, ensure the undercoat is adequately thinned while preserving the guard coat. The brindle pattern may appear lighter in summer when the coat is thinner. In winter, the coat fills in with rich density.
Kai Ken (244).maintenanceSchedule: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during shedding season. Bathe every 8-12 weeks. Clean ears every 2 weeks. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional deshedding groom every 8-10 weeks.

American Bully.brushingRoutine: Brush the American Bully's short, smooth, glossy coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt. The coat is short and stiff, lying flat against the muscular body. Use circular motions with the rubber curry to stimulate circulation and remove dead hair. A soft natural bristle brush adds finishing shine. Despite the short coat, this breed sheds moderately.
American Bully.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a moisturizing, hypoallergenic shampoo. American Bullies are prone to skin sensitivities, so gentle products are essential. A medicated shampoo may be needed for dogs with skin allergies. Clean all facial wrinkles and skin folds thoroughly during each bath. The short coat dries quickly with toweling.
commonIssues: Skin allergies and environmental sensitivities common in the breed
Facial fold dermatitis in shorter-muzzled varieties
Skin fold infections between body wrinkles
Short coat providing minimal sun protection
Elbow and hock calluses from the heavy build
American Bully.extendResults: Clean skin folds and wrinkles daily with antimicrobial wipes. Apply a leave-on conditioning spray weekly to maintain coat shine. Omega-3 supplements significantly improve skin health in this allergy-prone breed. Use a high-quality coat oil spray to add sheen between baths. Monitor for red, inflamed skin regularly.
American Bully.seasonalCare: Sheds moderately year-round with slight increase in spring. In Houston summers, the short coat handles heat well but wrinkled or lighter-skinned dogs need sun protection. In winter, the single coat provides little insulation; sweaters or jackets are recommended for cold weather outings.
American Bully.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry. Clean skin folds daily. Apply coat conditioning spray weekly. Bathe every 4-6 weeks with hypoallergenic shampoo. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 4-6 weeks.

Olde English Bulldogge.brushingRoutine: Brush the Olde English Bulldogge's short, medium-density coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush and bristle brush. The coat is short and somewhat coarse, lying flat against the stocky body. Use firm strokes with the rubber curry to remove dead hair and stimulate circulation. A bristle brush adds a clean finish. Pay attention to the coat around the neck wrinkles and facial folds.
Olde English Bulldogge.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo. The breed's facial wrinkles and body folds require thorough cleaning and drying during each bath. Use a wrinkle-specific cleanser inside folds. Rinse all shampoo thoroughly as residue in folds leads to irritation. Towel dry the short coat and use a separate cloth to dry all wrinkles completely.
commonIssues: Facial wrinkle infections from moisture and bacteria buildup
Skin fold dermatitis
Skin allergies common in bulldog types
Tail pocket infections requiring regular cleaning
Short coat providing minimal environmental protection
Olde English Bulldogge.extendResults: Daily wrinkle cleaning with antimicrobial wipes is the single most important maintenance task. Check and clean the tail pocket daily if present. A weekly coat conditioning spray maintains shine. Omega fatty acid supplements improve skin health. Keep bedding clean to reduce skin irritation.
Olde English Bulldogge.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly to moderately year-round. In Houston summers, the brachycephalic structure means heat sensitivity; keep grooming sessions short and cool. In winter, provide a jacket for outdoor time. Wrinkle care is a year-round daily commitment regardless of season.
Olde English Bulldogge.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly. Clean wrinkles and folds daily. Check tail pocket daily. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 4-6 weeks with thorough wrinkle treatment.

Alapaha Blue Blood Bulldog.brushingRoutine: Brush the Alapaha's short, stiff coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush and natural bristle brush. The coat is short, close-fitting, and somewhat coarse. Use firm circular motions with the rubber curry to remove dead hair from the muscular body. A bristle brush provides a finishing polish. The breed's merle and brindle patterns show best on a clean, well-brushed coat.
Alapaha Blue Blood Bulldog.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo. The short coat makes bathing straightforward. Clean facial wrinkles thoroughly during each bath, as the breed has moderate facial folding. Use a hypoallergenic shampoo if skin sensitivity is present. Towel dry the short coat and carefully dry all facial folds.
commonIssues: Facial wrinkle irritation and infection
Skin sensitivities and allergies
Short coat offering limited sun protection especially on lighter patches
Elbow calluses on hard surfaces
Entropion requiring gentle handling around eyes during grooming
Alapaha Blue Blood Bulldog.extendResults: Clean facial wrinkles 3-4 times weekly with antimicrobial wipes. Apply pet-safe sunscreen on lighter-colored patches before outdoor time. A weekly coat conditioning spray adds shine. Omega supplements support skin health. Keep bedding padded to prevent callus formation.
Alapaha Blue Blood Bulldog.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly to moderately year-round. In Houston summers, the short coat dissipates heat well but lighter patches need sun protection. Monitor for heat stress during grooming sessions. In winter, the short single coat provides limited insulation; jackets recommended for extended cold exposure.
Alapaha Blue Blood Bulldog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly with rubber curry. Clean wrinkles 3-4 times weekly. Apply sunscreen as needed. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 6-8 weeks.

Victorian Bulldog.brushingRoutine: Brush the Victorian Bulldog's short, smooth coat once or twice weekly with a rubber curry brush and soft bristle brush. The coat is fine, short, and close to the body. Use gentle circular motions with the rubber curry; the Victorian Bulldog has less extreme facial wrinkling than the English Bulldog but still requires attention to any folds. A chamois cloth buff after brushing adds a smooth sheen.
Victorian Bulldog.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a hypoallergenic, moisturizing shampoo. Clean all facial folds and wrinkles during each bath with a dedicated wrinkle cleanser. The breed has moderate wrinkles that need thorough drying. Use lukewarm water and avoid drafts during drying. The short coat air-dries quickly on the body.
commonIssues: Facial fold irritation though less severe than extreme bulldog types
Skin allergies and sensitivities
Tear staining
Short coat showing skin conditions readily
Moderate drooling causing chin irritation
Victorian Bulldog.extendResults: Clean facial wrinkles every other day with antimicrobial wipes. Wipe tear stains daily with a gentle eye wipe. A weekly conditioning spray maintains coat sheen. The breed's moderate structure means fewer extreme grooming challenges than other bulldog types, but consistent fold care is still essential.
Victorian Bulldog.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly year-round with slight increase in spring. In Houston summers, keep grooming sessions in air conditioning and monitor for heat stress. In winter, provide a coat for outdoor walks. Wrinkle care remains consistent year-round. The moderate build handles Houston's climate better than more extreme brachycephalic types.
Victorian Bulldog.maintenanceSchedule: Brush 1-2 times weekly. Clean wrinkles every other day. Clean eyes daily. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 4-6 weeks.

Shorty Bull.brushingRoutine: Brush the Shorty Bull's short, smooth coat once weekly with a rubber curry brush or grooming mitt. The coat is fine, short, and glossy, requiring minimal brushing. A soft bristle brush adds finishing shine. Despite the low-maintenance coat, weekly brushing removes dead hair and keeps the coat gleaming. Pay attention to any skin folds around the face and body.
Shorty Bull.bathingTips: Bathe every 4-6 weeks with a gentle, hypoallergenic shampoo formulated for sensitive skin. The compact size makes bathing easy in a sink or small tub. Clean any wrinkles and folds carefully during each bath. The short coat dries rapidly with toweling. Use lukewarm water as the breed can be temperature-sensitive.
commonIssues: Skin fold irritation if wrinkles are present
Skin allergies and sensitivities common in bull breeds
Short coat providing minimal protection from sun and cold
Tear staining on lighter-faced dogs
Compact body holding heat in summer
Shorty Bull.extendResults: Clean any facial wrinkles daily with antimicrobial wipes. A weekly wipe-down with a damp microfiber cloth keeps the coat fresh between baths. Apply a light coat conditioning spray for shine. The short, easy-care coat is the breed's grooming advantage; focus maintenance efforts on skin health and fold care.
Shorty Bull.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly year-round. In Houston summers, the compact build can overheat easily; keep grooming sessions cool and short. Apply sunscreen on lighter areas. In winter, sweaters and jackets are necessary for this short-coated breed. Skin fold care is a year-round daily task.
Shorty Bull.maintenanceSchedule: Brush once weekly with rubber curry or grooming mitt. Clean folds daily if present. Bathe every 4-6 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2 weeks. Professional bath every 4-6 weeks.

Miniature Bull Terrier.brushingRoutine: Brush the Miniature Bull Terrier's short, flat, glossy coat once weekly with a rubber curry brush or hound glove. The coat is harsh to the touch and lies flat against the muscular body. Use firm circular motions with the rubber curry to remove dead hair and stimulate the skin. A natural bristle brush or chamois cloth provides a high-polish finish. The breed's unique egg-shaped head has very fine coat that needs gentle handling.
Miniature Bull Terrier.bathingTips: Bathe every 6-8 weeks with a gentle, pH-balanced shampoo. The short coat makes bathing quick and easy. For white or predominantly white dogs, use a whitening shampoo. The breed can be prone to skin allergies, so hypoallergenic products are recommended. Towel dry; the short coat dries within minutes. Avoid getting water in the distinctive downturned ears.
commonIssues: Skin allergies and contact sensitivities common in the breed
White coat staining and discoloration in white varieties
Dry skin and flaking in winter months
Sunburn on white and light-colored skin
Lens luxation requiring gentle handling around the eyes
Miniature Bull Terrier.extendResults: A weekly coat conditioning spray with aloe or oatmeal soothes sensitive skin and adds shine. For white dogs, apply whitening spray weekly. Omega-3 supplements significantly improve skin condition in this allergy-prone breed. A chamois buff after brushing brings out the coat's natural hard gloss. Monitor for skin reactions after walks in grass.
Miniature Bull Terrier.seasonalCare: Sheds lightly to moderately year-round with slight increase in spring. In Houston summers, apply pet-safe sunscreen on white and light-colored areas before outdoor time. The short coat handles heat well. In winter, provide a coat or sweater for outdoor walks as the thin single coat provides minimal insulation.
Miniature Bull Terrier.maintenanceSchedule: Brush once weekly with rubber curry and bristle brush. Apply sunscreen on light areas before outdoor time. Bathe every 6-8 weeks. Clean ears weekly. Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Professional bath every 6-8 weeks.
